This searchable PDF file is the main text for PHI 340, RSLM (Religion, Science, Life and Mind,
374 pages). It is revised every time that the course is taught. Exam and quiz questions often
reflect those revisions. Use the current term’s version. Be sure to study the Preface and the
Introduction as well as the rest. The syllabus COURSE SCHEDULE AND REQUIRED READINGS specifies which chapters to read when.
Other readings covered by exam and microQuiz questions are *REQUIRED* readings linked
from the home page and specified in the matching syllabus COURSE SCHEDULE AND REQUIRED
READINGS. Make sure to download those readings – all are searchable PDF files – from the
current term’s home page.
There are also required pages in the one hard copy, Kitcher, Abusing Science, as specified in
the syllabus COURSE SCHEDULE AND REQUIRED READINGS and on the home page.
The more of the *REQUIRED* readings accessed, the higher the course grade tends to be.
Students who access only RSLM and none of the other *REQUIRED* readings flunk.
Exam#1 covers the syllabus-specified chapters in RSLM and these other readings:
• Whyte & Worthen, “The Right to Your Opinion” & “Stop Saying ‘I feel like’”
• The Oatmeal on core beliefs & backfire
• 186 Scientists and Feyerabend, “Strange Case of Astrology”
• Gilkey, “The Creationist Controversy”
• Ruse, “Creation Science is Not Science”
• Laudan, “Commentary: Science at the Bar”
• Kitzmiller v Dover (but almost all of what’s required is also quoted in RSLM)
• Kitcher, Abusing Science (see syllabus and website home page for pages assigned)
• Glymour and Stalker, "Winning through Pseudoscience."
Exam#2 covers the syllabus-specified chapters in RSLM and these other readings:
• Hall and Hall - “Is the War between Science and Religion Over?”
• Prothero, “Introduction,” God Is Not One
• Davies, “Physicist's Conception of Nature”
• Plantinga - “Is Belief in God ‘Properly Basic’?”
Exam#3 covers the syllabus-specified chapters in RSLM and these other readings:
• Haugeland, “Mind Design”
• Glymour, "Cosmic Censorship."
See the syllabus and home page for details about the Optional Final coverage. Two RSLM
chapters are covered by the Optional Final only: “The Mystery of Our Being: Problem of Freedom and Determinism” and “Cosmology with a New Purpose.” All the rest of RSLM is
*REQUIRED* reading for microQuizzes and Exams #1, #2 or #3.

vi

Preface
Preface
There is no difference between religion and science.
I want to persuade you that this rather surprising claim is worth taking far more seriously
than it usually is. The best way I've found to do that is to show that the most common, simple
reasons for denying it don't work. Doing only that much will not, of course, show that the
claim is true (and I anyway think it's an overstatement), but it might get you to look more
carefully at your own views about science, religion, and pseudoscience and to consider
responsible opposing viewpoints. That would be a very good thing, because the
contemporary debate about the relationship between religion and science, mired in
profound ignorance and confusion, has become so badly overheated that children are being
harmed. For example, in an understandable, but ultimately indefensible, reaction to the US
courts’ understanding of the US Constitution’s First Amendment as requiring a suitable
separation of church and state, many schools have excluded discussions of religion from all
of their curricula. Some friends of religion (including of course religious believers) have
understandably, but indefensibly, (over)reacted by trying to present religious ideas as if they
were something else. But both sides err in thinking that there are clear differences between
religion and science and that each is therefore entitled to special status. If you work through
this book, I hope you will no longer be confident that the differences are so very clear, even
if you remain convinced that there are important differences.
If you work through this book, there will be some questions you're better able to answer.
Here's one of the more complex ones:
Suppose that a system of statements and practices makes reference to uncontrolled,
unobserved things, encourages selective expression of emotion, expresses an attitude
of awe and wonder toward Nature, seeks to discover some important moral truths, is
based partly on faith, explains in terms of purposes and intentions, originated in
ancient times, contains vague terms, focuses on phenomena for which there are no
fully understood physical mechanisms, and has been misused by many evil people
who were also utterly dogmatic in their attitudes toward it.
Could such a system of beliefs be a scientific theory, a religion, a pseudoscience - or none or
all of these? (You can turn to the end of the book for an answer, but that will spoil your fun,
and the answer won't make much sense to you if you skip what comes in between.)
I have found it useful to assume at the outset of most of our investigations that there are
clear, sharp, simple differences. In every case, however, this assumption turns out to be false;
seeing why is highly instructive. While not every sharp difference in kind is best replaced by
a difference of degree, most are. This insight is also one that studying this book will help to
reinforce.
People often speak of doing “good science,” to praise those whose work in science is of high
quality. One consequence of the arguments presented here is that it makes as much sense to
speak of “good religion” as it does to speak of “good science.” It is at least as important and
difficult to do the one as to do the other. Because doing science and doing religion are
activities undertaken by fallible human beings, they sometimes fail, despite their best efforts,
to achieve what’s good; bad science or bad religion will likely then result. Surely it is worth
vii

Preface
being able to tell the difference between good and bad science and between good and bad
religion. The ideas in this text can help us to do so.
If I had made this text comprehensive and detailed, no one who might benefit from its
instruction would be able to read it, and very few would be able to lift a hard copy. I decided
instead to focus on the most prevalent and persistent confusions and misunderstandings
about these issues that I have encountered in more than forty-five years of teaching over
fifteen thousand students. Just as an introduction to, say, physics should not present the most
recent original work in physics because it would then be unintelligible to beginners (or, even
worse, might create an illusion of understanding), so this book strives for accessibility and
attempts to avoid originality in anything except perhaps presentation of ideas. My approach,
at least, has the virtue of having been repeatedly field-tested and found workable by those
on the front lines (including six colleagues who have taught courses based on various
versions of this material). Working through the modest amount of material here has also
encouraged a fair number of students to pursue more advanced study in an attempt to get
clearer on the questions. If you find that your favorite misunderstanding has been slighted,
however, please let me know, and I'll address any widely shared favorites in any future
editions (and web pages).
.

viii

Introduction
Introduction
Whenever our society faces a difficult question, it almost always looks to some branch of
science for help. Should you allow a nuclear waste dump in your neighborhood? How about
a nuclear power plant to keep it full? Is the global climate warming? If so, can anything be
done about it? What should we do about AIDS and other sexually transmissible diseases?
Does smoking cause cancer? Is homosexuality biologically determined? Can we build a shield
against nuclear weapons? Billions have been spent on looking for answers to such questions,
mainly because we feel reasonably confident that, with enough time and money, scientists
can give us the truth. On the other hand, some of what in our society masquerades as science
is really bunk. The next time you pass through the supermarket checkout, examine some of
the reading material you find there. It's full of readily discernible bunk. And then there is the
Internet itself.
But it's not always easy to tell when something is science and when it's not, especially if the
alleged experts themselves disagree or, even worse, if you can't figure out who, if anyone, is
an expert on the question at hand. Consider for example the following scenario:
Unless you do about thirty sit-ups every morning (knees raised!), it's virtually certain that
you will lose at least a few days' work to low back pain at some point during your life. Some
of you will be even more unlucky - one of your spinal shock-absorbers will suffer a blow-out
(what people mislabel a 'slipped disc') that irritates one of the main nerves to your legs, the
sciatic nerve. Sciatica can be chronic and so excruciatingly painful that even large doses of
narcotics don't provide much relief and they bring their own problems as well.
Suppose you are one of the unlucky ones who suffers a spinal blow-out and a friend stops by
to visit you in your bed of pain. She says, "I know what you're going through. I've been
through it myself. And I know what you should do: see my chiropractor, Dr. Smith. He'll do
some adjustments and you'll feel like a new person. But don't go near any of those knife-happy
spine surgeons! They'll make you worse, if you can imagine that!"
Then another friend visits and advises, "I know what you're going through. I've been through
it myself. And I know what you should do: see my spine surgeon, Dr. Jones. She's very careful
- not at all knife happy - and she'll recommend surgery only if it's really necessary. While
she's doing the necessary tests and exams, she and her marvellous staff of physical therapists
will make you more comfortable. But the one thing that you must not do under any
circumstances is to go to one of those chiropractic quacks! I went to one of those
pseudoscientists, and I left feeling even worse, if you can imagine that!"
If you're more thorough than most, you might examine some chiropractic and orthopaedics
textbooks, though they're so large and heavy that it won't be easy in your present condition.
Even worse, chiropractic and medical schools use some of the same texts, and all of them are
full of impressive terminology and pictures. You need to take action. Which health care
professional should you see? When faced with such an urgent practical matter, wouldn't it
be useful to have a simple, sharp, clear, brief principle that you could use to sort out science
from pseudoscience, so you could take the most reasonable course of action?

ix

Introduction
What we would need, then, is a 'gatekeeper', some general principle of demarcation that will
help us to separate real science from its junky imitation. And we'd prefer a simple, clear,
sharp, practical answer to the question, How does science differ from pseudoscience?
So, at least initially, let's adopt an appealingly simple picture: let's think of science as a highly
competitive race for the truth, with strict qualifying conditions that determine which
theories, hypotheses or explanations deserve a place at the starting line (and which do not).
Just as not everyone can enter the Olympics, not every theory deserves scientific
consideration. Our resources are too scarce and precious to waste on underqualified,
pseudoscientific bunk. And just as it makes no sense for an Olympic competitor to be a 'bit
of a starter', it makes no sense to see the difference between science and pseudoscience as
one of degree. A theory is either a starter (scientific) or a nonstarter (pseudoscientific);
there's no middle ground. Of course, not every starter in the race will be a winner: most
theories that really do deserve a run will turn out to be false, but a theory can be worse than
false: it can be pseudoscientific!
We'll begin by looking at some allegedly clear examples of pseudoscience: astrology,
phrenology, parapsychology, and UFOlogy. Astrology and phrenology were influential
attempts to account for human destiny and personality. Parapsychology, the study of "ESP"
and other so-called paranormal phenomena, explores the limits of human mental power.
UFOlogy seeks to discover if there are intelligent ETs mingling with human beings. Alas, none
of the gatekeepers commonly proposed works as well as the critics of pseudoscience hope.
To round out the discussion of gatekeepers, we will examine a widely touted view of science
as defined by paradigms, as well as the idea that what really matters in defining science's
role in making public policy is its practical utility together with its ability to remain free of
political influence and bias. None of these survives critical scrutiny, as our discussions of
Chaos theory, Chinese acupuncture, and sexology will show. As a reminder to be skeptical of
reports of scientific research in the media, we will take an extended look at how some
research on sexual orientation was badly misreported by a journalist for National Public
Radio (NPR) (but not by most other journalists).
We will then investigate the most controversial case of alleged pseudoscience: Scientific
Creationism and its ‘successor,’ Intelligent Design Creationism. We'll find that some of the
most widely accepted reasons for dismissing Scientific Creationism as pseudoscientific are
worthless; once we replace them with better reasons, we'll see that Scientific Creationists do
a great disservice to religion by misusing the very sort of purpose-directed explanation that
plays a central role in almost all religions. Intelligent Design Creationism does not engage in
the same kind of misuse, but pays a high price for the kind of use that it does advocate. (We
will also see that, given their views, there is a surprising gap in educational efforts of
Scientific Creationists because they fail to advocate the public school study of fallen angels
as agents of evil in human affairs.) But at least Scientific Creationists do not sink to the
intellectual depths of New Agers, who dilute purpose-directed explanation to the point of
disappearance by 'explaining' every similarity in this way (except when marketing
con$ideration$ are paramount).
By this point, we will have rejected the appealingly simple picture of science with which we
began, having replaced it with a more complex, gradualist view (developed most fully in
x

Introduction
Chapter 2 of Kitcher’s Abusing Science) according to which the distinction between science
and pseudoscience is a multidimensional difference of degree and not a simple, sharp
difference in kind. But being able to distinguish correctly between science and
pseudoscience does not resolve another important question that is often entangled with the
question of standards.
Although the advocates of Creation Science are typically religiously inspired, not all religious
believers are in sympathy with Creation Science. In fact, many clergy are adamantly opposed
to making Creation Science a part of public school teaching. There are many reasons for their
opposition, but perhaps the most widely and deeply held reason is that religion and science
are 'worlds apart': if religious beliefs are not open to scientific evaluation, then believers
have no reason to feel threatened by scientific findings. Some go further and say that there
isn't even any possibility of significant conflict between religion and science, so different are
their questions, approaches, and answers. So what, exactly, is the difference between
religion and science?
This is a question of great practical importance in part because concepts of religion are
components of so many US laws and policies. One of the main reasons for this is that the First
Amendment to the US Constitution gives religion a special legal status:
Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion, or prohibiting the
free exercise thereof; or abridging the freedom of speech, or of the press; or the right
of the people peaceably to assemble, and to petition the government for a redress of
grievances.
There are of course legal issues about what may be taught in public schools, as debates about
Creation Science illustrate.
But the concept of religion as characterized in US law is important in very many other ways
as well. Imagine for example the following headline:
“End the US Tax Exemption for Churches and Everyone Goes to College for Free!”

Under US tax laws that date from the 1890’s, churches are tax exempt. (The practice of exempting churches from taxation dates from the beginning of the US in the late 1700’s.) IRS
Publication 18281, provides relevant detail.
"Churches" Defined: The term church is found, but not specifically defined, in the Internal Revenue
Code. With the exception of the special rules for church audits, the use of the term church also includes conventions and associations of churches as well as integrated auxiliaries of a church.
Certain characteristics are generally attributed to churches. These attributes of a church have been
developed by the IRS and by court decisions. They include:
• Distinct legal existence
• Recognized creed and form of worship
• Definite and distinct ecclesiastical government
• Formal code of doctrine and discipline
• Distinct religious history
1 Tax Guide for Churches and Religious Organizations https://www.irs.gov/pub/irs-pdf/p1828.pdf [You are

never required to access material cited in footnotes in this book.]

Introduction
Membership not associated with any other church or denomination
Organization of ordained ministers
Ordained ministers selected after completing prescribed courses of study
Literature of its own
Established places of worship
Regular congregations
Regular religious services
Sunday schools for the religious instruction of the young
Schools for the preparation of its members
The IRS generally uses a combination of these characteristics, together with other facts and circumstances, to determine whether an organization is considered a church for federal tax purposes. (IRS
Publication 1828, p. 33)

One calculation of the resources expended by 271 U.S. congregations found that, on average,
‘operating expenses’ totaled 71 percent of all the expenditures of religions, much of that going to
pay ministers’ salaries. Financial contributions addressing the physical needs of the poor fall
within the remaining 29 percent of expenditures. … (Cragun, 40)

Another source reports:
…by 1971, the total of real and personal property owned by U.S. churches was around $110 billion. In New York City alone, the amount was $3 billion in 1989. A 1986 study showed religious
income in that year as roughly $100 billion, or about five times the income of the five largest corporations in the U.S. ….

Here is another way of thinking of the numbers:
And the grand total is ... A mere $62.6 billion dollars! According to new Department of Education
data, http://nces.ed.gov/pubs2013/2013183.pdf that's how much tuition public colleges
collected from undergraduates in 2012 across the entire United States. …3

If these estimates are correct, the tax exemptions for churches would pay for all college

Introduction
education in the US.4
Legal concepts of religion also pertain to making and applying laws concerning: abortion,
euthanasia, circumcision, birth control, blood tests and transfusions, tuberculosis screening,
genetic testing, vaccinations, Medicare, Medicaid, hospitals, legal guardianship, use of
marijuana, mushrooms, hoasca, khat, and other psychotropic substances, body and facial
piercing and other bodily decorations (e.g., tattoos), beards and hair length, clothing and
uniforms, autopsy, burial and disinterment, disability, child-raising, child abuse and neglect,
corporal punishment, child labor, marriage, polygamy, adultery, divorce, child custody,
parental rights, foster care, domestic violence, estates, trusts, wills and inheritance,
immigration, sexual behavior, pornography and obscenity, libel, slander and defamation,
real estate and other personal property, tithing, zoning, religious land use, mining rights,
housing, landlord-tenant relations, consumer fraud, contracts (e.g., within religious
organizations and between religious organizations and non-religious entities), military
service, conscientious objector status, copyright, trademarks and publishing, elections law,
criteria for employment, employee benefits, termination of employment, unemployment
benefits, workers’ compensation, welfare benefits, lending and debt collection practices,
right to bear arms or other weapons, home schooling, school busing, right to privacy (e.g., in
religious confession and pastoral counselling), torts, product liability and negligence
liability, taking of oaths, rights of prisoners, religious and non-religious discrimination,
security clearances, travel, police interrogation, search and seizure, display of religious
symbols, religious proselytizing, ... - the list could go on for even longer and with more detail.
Matters of life and death are clearly implicated.

4 This is far from an argument for ending the tax exemptions for religious organizations. That would require a
careful cost-benefit analysis of all relevant alternatives. A recent overhaul of the federal tax code did eliminate part of the exemption for religious organizations, and there have been calls to reverse this elimination.

xiii

Introduction
One of the most striking, recent examples of the importance of the concept of religion in US
law comes from the responses by state governments to the COVID-19 pandemic and thus
involves matters of life and death.
As the COVID-19 pandemic grew in the US, states began issuing “stay at home” orders, violation of which was typically a misdemeanor and thus a criminal act.5 The basic idea was to
limit contact between possibly or actually infected individuals and others. The orders were
fairly complex and contained long list of exemptions for many “essential” or “critical” businesses and organizations. The states’ orders relied on a Department of Homeland Security
characterization of 16 critical infrastructure sectors defined by the Department of Homeland
Security.6
But by the middle of March 2020, over a dozen states’ orders also contained explicit exemptions of some sort for religious gatherings.

exempting travel to and from one’s place of worship, and religious institutions are “essential businesses”

a room or confined space at a time and individuals shall adhere to Social Distancing Requirements as much as possible.

However, Idaho and Indiana made the lack of a religious exemption explicit. Idaho’s ban explicitly included faith-based, spiritual gatherings: “The recording or streaming of religious
services should be done by the minimum number of individuals necessary, following Social
Distancing Requirement.” Indiana ordered that large gatherings, including church services,
5 A relatively complete list of orders is at https://web.csg.org/covid19/executive-orders/ . The account that

follows is not exhaustive and omits discussion of states that issued restrictions but did not call them “stay-athome orders.” Eventually, every state issued some restrictions.
6 available at https://www.cisa.gov/critical-infrastructure-sectors. Chemical Sector, Commercial Facilities Sector, Communications Sector, Critical Manufacturing Sector, Dams Sector, Defense Industrial Base Sector, Emergency Services Sector, Energy Sector, Financial Services Sector, Food and Agriculture Sector, Government Facilities Sector, Healthcare and Public Health Sector, Information Technology Sector, Nuclear Reactors, Materials, and Waste Sector, Sector-Specific Agencies, Transportation Systems Sector, Water and Wastewater Systems
Sector. See also https://www.cisa.gov/identifying-critical-infrastructure-during-covid-19 .
xiv

Introduction
to be canceled, saying, “Religious leaders are encouraged to continue livestreaming services
while practicing social distancing with one another.” Given the dangers to the lives of
religious believers from gathering in large, tightly packed groups for extended periods of
time during which viruses would be exhaled while speaking or singing, one might reasonably
wonder why all states did not follow Idaho’s and Indiana’s lead.
Some religious organizations resented being labeled as “non-essential,” but the label was
inherited from the federal Department of Homeland Security and was not used with its
ordinary meaning of “unimportant.” Some clergy and religious organizations went well
beyond resentment and filed suits in state courts challenging the constitutionality of some
state orders that placed restrictions on religious activities, arguing that the First Amendment
prohibits any such restrictions or that the particular orders were unfairly favorable to some
organizations while disfavoring religious organizations.
Since the mid-1990s, I’ve subscribed to the Law and Religion Issues for Law online discussion
group in which some of the leading US legal scholars participate. When religious exemptions
began appearing in state orders, a long, complex discussion began among those experts
about whether such exemptions were required at all, and if so, on exactly what legal grounds.
(The discussion became so complex and voluminous that a separate discussion group was
created for that topic!) Towards the beginning of the discussion, Professor Mark Rush, a
political scientist at Washington and Lee University, remarked in part
If we could all declare ourselves churches of one, then we could all decide the laws to which we
would or would not object. That would lead to the chaos that [US Supreme Court Justice Felix]
Frankfurter described in the flag salute cases.7
No one wants to pretend that the legitimacy of one’s religion should be subject to its acceptance
by nonbelievers. On the other hand, we can’t allow every objector to gain conscientious street
cred by declaring a new religious affiliation. The problem, of course, is that the constitution alludes to religious freedom without ever setting forth a clear definition of “religion”. Absent a
clear definition, it boils down to court’s knowing religion when they see it, no?
So, this debate—as fascinating and important as it is—rests on a foundation that is very unstable
without a clear definition of “religion.” One solution would be to conflate religion with “conscience.” But that would beget Frankfurter’s chaos. The other would be to dispense with the notion of any conscientious objection. That would be equitable but just a tad inflammatory. [emphasis added] [4/20/20]

Since then, the US Supreme Court has heard appeals to overturn state orders limiting
7 See https://www.annenbergclassroom.org/resource/the-pursuit-of-justice/pursuit-justice-chapter-10-flag-

salute-cases/ about Minersville School District v. Gobitis, 310 U.S. 586 (1940) and West Virginia State Board of
Education v. Barnette, 319 U.S. 624. In his dissent to the latter, Justice Frankfurter wrote:
“When dealing with religious scruples we are dealing with an almost numberless variety of doctrines and beliefs entertained with equal sincerity by the particular groups for which they satisfy man's needs in his relation to the mysteries of the universe. There are in the United States more than 250 distinctive established religious denominations. In the state of Pennsylvania there are 120 of these, and in West Virginia as many as 65.
But if religious scruples afford immunity from civic obedience to laws, they may be invoked by the religious
beliefs of any individual even though he holds no membership in any sect or organized denomination. Certainly this Court cannot be called upon to determine what claims of conscience should be recognized and
what should be rejected as satisfying the 'religion' which the Constitution protects. That would indeed resurrect the very discriminatory treatment of religion which the Constitution sought forever to forbid.”
xv

Introduction
religious (and many other) gatherings and it has refused to rule on almost all.8
Stay-at-home orders concern in an obvious way matters of life and death. Without a clear
definition of “religion” as it is used in the First Amendment, preventing death and promoting
life is made more difficult.
Among the religious groupings involved in court cases concerning other religion-and-law
issues are some that you might not be familiar with: American Carpatho-Russian Orthodox
Church, Amish, Bahai, Baptists, Boy Scouts, Buddhists, Catholics and Catholic Church,
Christian Reformed Church in North America, Church of God in Christ, Church of the New
Song, Episcopal Church, Greek Orthodox Church, Hutterites, Ismailis, Jehovah's Witnesses,
Jews, Jews For Jesus, Lubavitch Hasidic Community, Lutheran Church, Masons, Masonic
Temple, Methodist Church, Methodist Episcopal Church, United Methodist Church, Full Moon
Gathering, Moorish Science Temple of America, Mormons, Narco Saints, Nation of Islam,
Nation of Yahweh, Orthodox Church in America, Polish National Catholic Church,
Presbyterian Church, Quakers, Rastafarians, Reformed Church in America, Santeria, Satmar
Chasidim, Seventh Day Adventists, Sikhs, Swedenborgian Church, Syrian Orthodox Church,
Taoism, Thelemites, Tony Alamo Christian Ministries, Waldorf Schools, Wesleyan
Pentecostal Churches, Wiccan Religion.
But there is great variety even within and among the most popular religious traditions: e.g.
Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism:

The geographical distribution of religious groups among the US states is quite uneven. Utah’s
population has the largest percentage of Christians (74%), Oregon the smallest (30%) and
North Carolina is ranked 30th with about 45% of its population reporting as Christians of
some sort. Minnesota and Massachusetts are ranked 5th and 8th with 61% and 59%, respectively. Given the variety in Christian denominations, however, this tends to overstate the
agreement within states on religious, social and political matters tied to membership in a
denomination.
For Christian denominations with 60,000 or more members, there are over 390,000 ordained clergy. The number of members per clergy varies very widely, from a low of 21 for
Church of God of Prophecy, to over 2,400 for Catholic and Greek Orthodox churches, and
nearly 12,000 for the Armenian Apostolic Church of America. This surely reflects some important differences among denominations’ beliefs and practices as well.
Using http://www.thearda.com/denoms/families/members.asp one can compare religious
groupings’ beliefs, practices and demographic characteristics. A tiny sample of the thousands
of available comparisons (shading indicates highest and lowest percentages)

Twenty-two religious groupings each have half a million or more adherents world-wide:
Christianity, Islam, Secular/Nonreligious/Agnostic/Atheist, Hinduism, Chinese traditional

Kyuseikyo, Sthanakavasis, Zenrinkai, Druze, Tensho Kotai Jingukyo, Friends (Quakers), Ennokyo, Digambaras, Reconstructionist, Parsis, Gabars.
According to the reference work Guide to the Gods,14 there are over 18,980 deities that have
been supposed to exist by various religions of the world. But this understates the possible
variety in religious beliefs, because some religions suppose that there are many deities and
perhaps there could be an extremely tolerant, all-embracing religious view that accepts
many possible combinations of the 18,980, a very large number.
Still, these enumerations and classifications omit, over-simplify and gloss over deep differences within the categories labeled - and they concern only the existing more popular religions.15 There are other less popular religions and probably infinitely many other possible
religions.
Many common religiously associated stereotypes are mistaken. For example:
Religious belief is not at all uncommon among scientists. Sociologist of religion Elaine Ecklund reports16 that over half of all living US scientists have religious beliefs, disbelief is a
minority phenomenon among the US scientists and strongly negative attitudes towards religion are rare among scientists. Kenneth Miller, an evolutionary biologist who testified in
Kitzmiller v Dover against teaching Intelligent Design Creationism in public school science
classes and who later wrote Finding Darwin’s God: A Scientist's Search for Common Ground
Between God and Evolution 17 is for example a Catholic. Francis Collins, a prominent geneticist
and the former director or the Human Genome Project, now director of the National Institutes of Health, became an evangelical Christian in his twenties and remains so.18
A recent analysis from the Pew Research Center addresses the effect of levels of education
on religious belief and practices.19 Here’s one summary:
Pew’s researchers looked at data from a number of recent surveys, including their 35,000-person
study of American religion from 2014. They found that educated people are generally less likely
to believe in God: Among all U.S. adults, only 83 percent of college grads said they think God
exists, while 92 percent of people with only a high-school degree or less said the same.
Within Christianity, though, the difference all but disappears. Among educated mainline
Protestants, 96 percent said they believe in God, compared to 97 percent among the less educated; among Catholics, 98 percent of both groups said the same. Among Mormons, black
Protestants, and evangelical Protestants, there was effectively no difference at all, because virtually everyone in those groups said they believe in God.
14 http://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/record/NCSU788249 Reference Material (1st floor, East Wing) BL473 .L43

1992

in some forms of Buddhism. See https://global.oup.com/academic/product/buddhist-fury-9780199793242

xxii

Introduction
Educational differences had a much bigger effect on religious practice. Sixty-eight percent of college-educated evangelical Protestants go to church every week, compared to 55 percent of those
who only went to high school. In fact, college grads show up in the church pews more often in
nearly every kind of Christian tradition: Among mainline Protestants, weekly attendance was 36
to 31 percent, more educated to less; among black Protestants, 59 to 52 percent; and among
Catholics, 45 to 39 percent. The effect was perhaps greatest among Mormons: 85 percent of Mormon college graduates go to church at least once a week, compared to 66 percent of their peers
with a high-school education or less.
The trends in religious-minority groups are different. Among Muslims, education didn’t make
much of a difference in people’s belief or practice: Roughly half of people of all levels of education
attend services at least once a week, and nearly all Muslims reported believing in God. Among
Jews, the effect was reversed: Roughly one-quarter of Jews with a high-school degree or less attend services every week, compared to roughly one-tenth of college-educated Jews. And 58 percent of less educated Jews believe in God, compared to less than a third of their more educated
peers. As Pew points out, this effect is likely driven in part by Orthodox Jews, who tend to be much
more observant and much less educated—at least in a secular sense—than their Reform and Conservative peers.
Perhaps high-school-educated Christians feel less able to find community.
Among Christians, the pattern of educated people being more involved in their religious communities makes sense. As I’ve written before,20 communal involvement of all kinds is increasingly
becoming a luxury good of sorts, with higher levels of income and education making people more
likely to participate in activities like church, book club, parent-teacher association, and more. It
could be that high-school-educated Christians feel less able to find and connect with a religious
community in a broader context of financial strain, family stress, and geographic isolation. Or it
could be that college-educated Christians put more of a premium on connecting with their brothers and sisters in the church. (italics added)

The last paragraph contains an interesting hypothesis. (I don’t know if the hypothesis is true.
It’s quoted because it’s interesting. There’s a lot that is quoted or cited in this text because it
is interesting but quoting or citing material is not the same as saying it’s true.)
Most people don’t know enough about philosophy to be surprised by the following, but a few
might be surprised, and you might find it interesting anyway: Philosophy has a 2,500+ year
history and for over 95% of that history, atheism was uncommon among philosophers. The
latter should not be surprising because for almost all their histories, no distinction at all was
made between theology and philosophy. Four of the philosophers on whose work the course
relies were or are deeply religious: the 17th century Descartes (Catholic), Princess Elisabeth
of Bohemia,21 Isaac Newton22 and Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz (Protestants), 21st century
Plantinga (Protestant)23. Even the 18th century historian and philosopher Hume, who was
one of the rare atheists, took religion so seriously that he wrote books about it, and his

xxiii

Introduction
Dialogues on Natural Religion (a brief excerpt from which is central in the chapter, “Separation by Explanation”) contains still-classic statements of intelligent design arguments for the
existence of God. Michael Ruse, most widely known for his testimony in McLean v Arkansas
(1981) against teaching Young Earth Creationism (=”Scientific Creationism”) in public
school science courses, was raised as a Quaker, has co-authored books with advocates of Intelligent Design Creationism, and has defended religion against attacks by the “New Atheists”
(e.g., Richard Dawkins). Similarly, Philip Kitcher, the author of Abusing Science: The Case
against Creationism, was raised in the Anglican Church, sang for many years in a choir, and
remarks in his most recent book on religion, “… I resist the now dominant [New A]theist idea
that religion is noxious rubbish to be buried as deeply, as thoroughly and as quickly as possible.”24
It might therefore be a good idea to put aside any simple stereotypes and preconceptions
about science, scientists, religion and religious identity, etc., in approaching course materials.
We will not be able to clarify every concept of religion that pertains to all of the preceding,
but we can at least make a good start by clearing away some influential, popular and
persistent confusions.
To that end, we will look at eight ways science and religion are commonly said to be
essentially and systematically different, eight “separation principles”:
1. Science does, but religion does not, attempt to meet high standards of explanation
(perhaps because science is a 'matter of the head', but religion is a 'matter of the
heart').
2. Religion does, but science does not, make reference to God, the Designer of the
Universe.
3. Religion does, but science does not, take an attitude of awe and wonder toward
Nature.
4. Religion aims for personal improvement, but science does not.
5. Religion says that changes in nonphysical objects cause changes in physical objects,
but science does not. (deferred until the discussion of mind-body dualism; see below)
6. Religion does, but science does not, try to say what morally ought to be.
7. Religion is, but science is not, based on faith. (-the favorite, by a wide margin)
8. Religion does, but science does not, need purpose-directed explanation.
We'll find that all except perhaps the last proposal are wrong. But even the last proposal
faces a problem: biology and psychology are full of 'purpose'-talk of some kind or another.
Therefore, to figure out whether or not there is a real difference between science and
religion, we will need to dig deeper and find out what kind of conceptual resources really are
needed in biology and psychology. (And, as an interesting footnote to the eighth way, we'll
take a brief look at the recent, but misguided, attempt by some physicists to re-introduce a
kind of purpose-directed explanation into cosmology, the branch of physics that theorizes
about the origin and nature of the universe.)
The final major question we investigate is therefore: Is some irreducible concept of purpose
necessary in either biology or psychology, or can we get along with a reduced stock of concepts
24 http://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/record/NCSU3122885

xxiv

Introduction
from other branches of science (for example, 'cause and effect' and a few others from
physics)? To raise the stakes for such questions about the reducibility of one kind of
explanation to another, we will consider a solution to the age-old Problem of Freedom and
Determinism, offered by early 20th century physicist Max Planck, in his essay, "The Mystery
of Our Being."
If living and thinking things were merely very complex and sophisticated machines, then a
reduced stock of concepts would suffice. And it is from an analogy with one kind of machine
- the modern computer - that Cognitive Science gets its inspiration and resources; it is based
on a computer model of the mind, a model that was motivated by the problems that bedevil
older dualist and behaviorist theories of mind. The computer model, however, suffers from
some problems of its own, problems that are neatly addressed if we include purposedirected explanation in our theory of the mind. This suggests that purpose-directed
explanation is needed in science as well as in religion. (Biology is a trickier case, but there is
also good reason to believe that the concept of normal biological function is irreducible.)
Our last and most promising way of distinguishing science and religion (8., above) thus
appears to fail, and we are left with none of these influential and popular reasons for thinking
that there is any systematic, essential difference between them.
How, then, are we to interpret the First Amendment to the US Constitution when it says,
"Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion ... or prohibiting the
free exercise thereof..."? If we can't clearly distinguish religion from science, how are we to
define "religion" in this context? Because the entire US system of public education – and as
emphasized in the above, much else in American life - is shaped by that definition, this is not
an issue we can afford to ignore. We need to do better. We need first to stop talking as if we
understood what “religion” and “science” mean.
An Important Note on Terminology: “Belief”

Some writers draw a distinction between opinions and beliefs. Some also reserve the term
“belief” for religious belief. I don’t do that in this text. Throughout this text, whenever I use
the word “belief” and its cognates (“believes,” “believing,” etc.) I will mean simply: thinks it’s
true. So for example to say, “Sam believes that there are chairs” is simply to say, “Sam thinks
it’s true that there are chairs,” or “Sam thinks that there are chairs.” Opinions are beliefs that
may in a given context be of particular interest. In the right context, any belief could aptly be
called an “opinion.” So I won’t draw any general distinction between beliefs and opinions
either. For variety, I will sometimes use “accept” and “believe” interchangeably. “Sam accepts
the claim that there are chairs” is thus another way of saying, “Sam believes that there are
chairs.”
A Useful Method: Taking a Martian Perspective

When a claim is very familiar, it can be difficult to discover the basis, if any, for accepting it.
If you are in the habit of accepting a claim without questioning it, that habit will get in the
way of asking questions. But it can be helpful and even important to ask questions about
what’s most familiar. Doing so can give you a better understanding of exactly what it is that
you accept. If you accept a claim literally without question, and the claim might shape your
behavior, then it is important to understand the claim fully.
xxv

Introduction
When we want to deepen understanding in this way, it helps to take a “Martian perspective.”25 Here’s how to take a Martian perspective.
Martians, we may suppose, are friendly, intelligent and curious aliens who are visiting Earth
and they want to learn about it and the creatures who inhabit it. They are very interested in
understanding why human beings behave as they do, and why humans have the highly varied
beliefs that they hold. So, Martians ask a lot of questions, including many that you’d not typically ask. A good host answers patiently, understanding that the Martians are not asking
these questions because they are unintelligent or want to annoy or irritate you. They’re “not
from around here” and simply don’t know the answers.
There is thus often something child-like about their questioning.26 Some parents do get annoyed with a child’s questions, but unless the child is asking simply to be annoying, a good
parent will respond kindly and patiently – as would a good host to a visitor’s question. (Of
course, time can run out and the parent may say, “Time for dinner now. We’ll talk more about
this later.”)
There are some sorts of answers that we can tell in advance are not going to be very helpful
to Martians. “That’s the way we do things,” will hardly ever be a good response, because it so
obviously invites the question, “Why do you do things in that way?” “That’s what we believe,”
will hardly ever be a good response, because it so obviously invites the question, “Why do
you believe that?” If there are a lot of beliefs that the Martian’s asking about, all or many of
which are written down in, say, a book, then pointing to the book and saying, ‘That says what
we believe,” is in effect a rather rude refusal to answer.
However, it isn’t at all rude, unkind or dismissive to say, honestly, “I don’t know,” when a
Martian asks a question to which you don’t know the answer. As knowledge-seekers, Martians respect those who realize what they don’t know. They do however become very puzzled
when someone shows little or no interest in finding out about something apparently quite
important, and they find utterly baffling a persistent refusal to investigate – though that kind
of behavior is sure to be added to the list of things about which they will want to ask questions.
Martians are inclined to feel pity for those who don’t know and seek to remain unknowing
because the Martians value knowledge so highly. “Knowledge is good for its own sake,” they
say, “quite apart from its consequences.”27 (This motto is displayed prominently over the
entry way to every Martian university.) Martians are not therefore wholly neutral inquirers.
How could they be wholly neutral and also motivated to inquire if they did not value
knowledge very highly? But they are sensitive beings and mean no harm by their questioning. Even if they are unable to understand why some would want to remain unknowing, they
will suspend their inquiries if their questions cause needless pain.

25 The title essay of Oliver W. Sacks, An Anthropologist on Mars: Seven Paradoxical Tales (Knopf, 1995)
http://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/record/NCSU869329 is about Temple Grandin and was part of the inspiration
here. The general method is common in doing philosophy.
26 For an argument that children are natural philosophers, see: Gareth B. Matthews, Philosophy and the Young
Child (Harvard University Press, 1980) http://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/record/NCSU528343

xxvi

Introduction
Opinions and Faith

There is another advantage of taking a Martian perspective. It can help us to avoid an
unwarranted sense of entitlement often expressed by saying, “I’m entitled to my opinion.” In
his essay, “The Right to Your Opinion,” Jamie Whyte argues that in any case where the claim,
“I have a right to my opinion [about ___],” might be apt, it is bound to be false, because
whenever an opinion (=belief) actually matters, it ought to be justified by evidence and
argument. (Remember the stipulation above: all opinions are beliefs.) Perhaps you can
believe pretty much anything that you want to believe, but the mere fact that you believe
something is not a good reason for anyone else to give much weight to what you believe. If
however you can support your belief with good evidence and a well-reasoned argument,
then you will have done what you must to earn some respect for your belief.
In “Stop Saying ‘I Feel Like’,” Molly Worthen argues that the overuse of the prefix, “I feel like”
also serves to stop rational argument:
… [it] cripples our range of expression and flattens the complex role that emotions do play in our
reasoning. It turns emotion into a cudgel that smashes the distinction — and even in our
relativistic age, there remains a distinction — between evidence out in the world and internal
sentiments known only to each of us.
We should not “feel like.” We should argue rationally, feel deeply and take full responsibility for
our interaction with the world. [emphasis added]

Neither Whyte nor Worthen28 talks about faith in their essays, but a similar point applies to
faith as well: the mere fact that you accept something on faith is not a good reason for anyone
else to give much weight to what you accept on faith, and is not by itself a good reason for
them to accept it on faith. Accepting some beliefs on faith is, as will be argued in “Separation
by Foundation,” perfectly reasonable. But, as the brief summary above of data about religions
helps to remind us, there are very many things that might be accepted on faith, not all of them
could reasonably be accepted on faith at once, and the choice of some in particular over
others is something that must still be justified. Some claims are clearly very poor candidates
for acceptance on faith. For example, you will not persuade your math instructor to give you
any points for the large blank space on your test by asserting, “For me, the Fundamental
Theorem of the Calculus is a matter of faith. There’s no need for me to prove it.” Other cases
may be less clear, and then argument will be needed to justify or resist acceptance of
particular beliefs on faith.
Taking a Martian perspective might also help with another problem. The problem is the
result of a universal fact about human beings: everyone takes some of his or her beliefs to
define the kind of person that they are. Everyone does this. Because all of us are fallible, it’s
certain that some of our beliefs are false, though of course we might not know which ones
are false, and we might not even know which beliefs are more likely to be false. And some of
those false beliefs might be among the ones that are taken to define the kind of person
someone is.29

28 Their essays are together the first required reading for the course.

29 Obviously, “false” is not functioning here as it does in, say, “false teeth.” A false belief is a real belief, but it

does not correspond to any actual fact.

xxvii

Introduction
If a belief helps to define the kind of person that you are, any questions about that belief can
feel as if they are intended as a direct attack on you. [One of the best explanations of this is
in a cartoon strip, The Oatmeal: http://theoatmeal.com/comics/believe_clean - which is
required reading not merely for the course but for the rest of your life. Reading it should take
about three minutes. You might think it’s silly for a college text to refer to a cartoon strip,
especially one named, “The Oatmeal,” but in this case it’s serious.30] Taking a Martian
perspective can help you to remember that, as the FAQs say, asking a question about a deeply
held belief is asking a question and not attacking.
The problem is that asking a question about a deeply held belief can nevertheless feel as if it
were an attack even when it is not intended as an attack and even when it is not an attack; it
is very difficult to continue to listen despite feeling as if you were under attack.
Continuing to listen despite having those feelings is a skill that takes practice to learn and
some determination to make into a habit. I bet that you have what it takes to practice the
skill and thus to make it into a habit. What I don’t know is whether or not you will use what
you have to acquire the habit. We’ll see.

you can find references to research on identity-defining beliefs and “motivated reasoning,” which is a way to
protect those beliefs from perceived attacks. It is not always a good idea to protect those beliefs, however. See
also http://culturalcognition.net.

Astrology
Astrology
The good Christian should beware of [astrologers], and all those who make empty
prophecies. The danger already exists that the [astrologers] have made a covenant
with the devil to darken the spirit and to confine man in the bonds of Hell.
St. Augustine (354-430) DeGenesi ad Litteram, Book II, xviii, 37
History of Astrology

Astrology has existed, in some form or other, for over 3,000 years. More than a few of the
greater minds of civilization, Western or Eastern, have taken it seriously. For many centuries
it was barely distinguished from what we now call "astronomy"; in fact, astronomy was
developed in part to allow the making of astrological predictions. The great astronomer
Kepler was also an adherent of astrology, writing that "one should only disbelieve in idiocy
and blasphemy; from the astrologers a useful and healthy wisdom can emerge." Even Isaac
Newton showed some sympathy with astrology (at least to the extent that it shared some
ideas with alchemy)33, though he hid his interest out of fear of religious condemnation.
And, much as our presidents now have economic advisers, monarchs and popes often used
to employ an astrologer full-time to help guide them in the making of important decisions;
the office of 'Astrologer to the Royal Court' was thus a potentially powerful position. The
present version of astrology, developed by the ancient Greeks around 400 BCE, was codified
by Claudius Ptolemy in the second century CE in his Tetrabiblos. So astrology's basic text has
remained pretty much unchanged during the last 18 centuries.
Look at the sky on a clear, dark night. The impression one may get is of many points of light
pasted on the inner surface of a great hollow sphere centered on Earth. As the world turns,
this celestial sphere appears to turn daily around Earth's own axis of rotation. The stars seem
to rise and set, but there are many fixed constellations in which the stars relative positions
remain constant. The planets Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn, all visible to the
unaided eye, seem to move with respect to the fixed stellar background. Their movements
all appear to be in roughly the same plane as that of Earth's motion around the Sun, a region
that forms a belt, centered on the Sun's path, around the celestial sphere. According to
astrology, this celestial belt is divided into twelve equal regions, represented by the twelve
signs of the Zodiac. In order from East to West they are Aries, Taurus, Gemini, Cancer, Leo,
Virgo, Libra, Scorpio, Sagittarius, Capricorn, Aquarius, and Pisces. (It takes about a month for
the Sun to move through each region.) During the calendar year, each of the planets and the
Sun are in one or another of these regions. For example, on July 12, the Sun is in Cancer; on
May 11, the Sun is in Taurus; on February 23, in Pisces, etc. This gives us a crude coordinate
system for locating the planets and Sun with respect to the stellar background; a bit more
work is required to figure out how the planets and Sun are oriented in the Zodiac relative to
points on Earth from which they may be observed. From Earth, the celestial sphere seems to
rotate about Earth's axis; this is why heavenly bodies appear to rise (in the East) and set (in
33Newton’s interest in alchemy and theology were however much deeper than his interest in astrology.
http://www.astrozero.co.uk/articles/Newton.pdf [You are never required to access material cited in footnotes in this book.]

3

Astrology
the West), gradually moving across the sky. The 12 astrological houses are simply stationary
regions of the sky, fixed with respect to the horizon, that can be used to locate planets and
signs relative to the Earth's vantage point (see next page). To determine a person's natal
horoscope one maps the locations of signs and planets in each of the twelve houses not only
at the time of that person's birth, but also from the vantage point of that person's birthplace.
It is a rather elaborate and somewhat arbitrary coordinate system for locating some
heavenly bodies relative to a person's birthplace and date. So far, so good – and not very
interesting.
Astrology's main interest derives from its assertion that certain human physical and
psychological characteristics are associated with each of the signs and planets and that each
house governs some aspect of a person's life. So, for example, the first house is alleged to
control temperament and personality. If Mars, the Roman god of war, is in that house, the
individual is likely to be aggressive; if Aries, the sign controlled by Mars, is also in that first
house, astrology would predict an extremely aggressive temperament. Of course, scores of
other factors may operate in a natal horoscope and hence astrology is not committed to
predicting that the individual with Mars and Aries in the first house is bound to be, say, an
axe-murderer; if in addition the tenth house, which controls career, were inhabited by
Neptune, a water (or wishy-washy) planet, a planet therefore associated with indecisiveness,
that aggressive person might never be able to make up his mind whom to attack. Interpreting
a chart and weighing all of the simultaneous influences is therefore a complex matter indeed.
Nevertheless, it is sufficiently well-ordered by rules of interpretation that much of the task
can be done efficiently by a suitably programmed microcomputer. As the world turns, so the
home front soap opera evolves.
Zodiac Sign

downs, optimistic, self-doubting, timid, and ready to sell themselves for praise

associated with farming, hardworking, content with silent praise, slow but strong
in both mind and body

associated with music and singing, opposed to war, adept at astronomy, living
easy lives full of love and pleasure

associated with finance and international trade, grasping and miserly, shrewd
and combative

associated with hunting and butchery, prone to anger and sullenness, honest,

and speech, held back by shyness, unlikely to have children

4

Astrology
Zodiac Sign

Sagittarius associated with any profession that requires a mastery over animals, such as a
