on evolution have been refuted by the scientific community."
By (2) he means that proponents of IDC suppose that if evolutionary theory is false, then IDC
is true. (3) obviously means that proponents of IDC have made discredited criticisms of
evolutionary theory.
But what do (2) and (3) have to do with the scientific status of IDC? We saw in our discussion
of astrology that the fourth criticism of it was badly confused because it blamed astrology,
the theory, for its misuse by unscrupulous astrologers. But the theory is not the same thing
as its proponents, nor do any of the proponents’ personal characteristics tell us anything
relevant about the quality of astrology as an explanation of human psychology or destiny.
In the same way, (2) and (3) are not about IDC at all. They are instead about the proponents
of IDC. So (2) and (3) are simply irrelevant to a determination of IDC's status as science or
religion.
One might have hoped that in 2005, almost thirty years since the last major court case
concerning Scientific Creationism, the federal judiciary might have made some progress in
learning elementary lessons of mid-20th century philosophy of science. Alas, that seems not
93

Creationism in the Courts
to have happened. Instead, previous errors have been repeated leaving First Amendment
establishment clause jurisprudence in a state of ossified error.
We have at least learned how to avoid some common and influential errors about how to
assess creationism. With Kitcher's help, we can, however, learn how properly to assess Scientific Creationism, as well as other theories that seek to explain the order of the world.

94

Reading Abusing Science
Reading Abusing Science
The main purpose of this chapter is give some guidance about how to read Kitcher’s Abusing
Science.96
A good rule of thumb: if you think that you might not fully understand material, then
you should ask me for help with that material. You do not have to think that you do
not understand fully; mere doubt (‘might not’) should trigger the request.
In what follows I present the essentials of Kitcher’s discussion of just two common, badly
misguided, creationist objections to modern evolutionary theory (EVT). Of course, it is
impossible to understand what Kitcher says unless you have first studied the assigned
Chapter 1 of Abusing Science – which is to say that it is impossible to understand the
debate between Creationism and modern evolutionary theory unless you have an
accurate idea of what modern evolutionary theory is. As Jamie Whyte would point out,
no one is entitled to any opinion about modern evolutionary theory unless they know which
theory it is. (The latter applies to everyone, including those who favor modern evolutionary
theory and those who reject it. Research on “motivated reasoning” has found that many in
both groups are equally lacking in knowledge about the theory. See the “Introduction.”)
Tautology Objection

On pages 55-60 of Abusing Science, Kitcher considers a common objection made by some
creationists to modern evolutionary theory, the Tautology Objection.
According to the objectors, EVT is really a very simple theory that can be summarized in the
familiar slogan, “survival of the fittest.” This is the Principle of Natural Selection and is the
statement, “The fittest organisms survive.”
By definition, an organism is fittest if it survives, so the Principle of Natural Selection is,
according to some Scientific Creationist critics, equivalent to the statement, “Those
organisms that survive, survive.” This is an example of what is called a “tautology.” Why
would that be a problem for EVT? To see why, we need to know a bit more about tautologies.
A tautology is any statement that is made true simply by the meanings of the words in it.
Other examples of tautologies are
All bachelors are unmarried.
If there are chickens, then there are birds.
Either some numbers are odd or it is not the case that some numbers are odd.
Because they are true by definition, tautologies are not just true but necessarily true – it is
not even possible for them to be false. So they are not the sorts of statements that need to be
supported by observation or experiment. It would be a sign of serious confusion if someone
said, “I want to be sure that if there are chickens, then there are birds, so I am going to find
as many chickens as I can and check to make sure that every one of them is also a bird.”
Tautologies are therefore not refutable, not falsifiable and not testable.

96 The required reading is: Ch. I, Ch. II, (pp. 30 - 54); Ch. III, (pp. 55 - 60, 63 - 66); Ch. IV, (pp. 85 - 89, 100 -

106); Ch. V, (pp. 124 - 164); Ch. VI, (167 - 172, 180 - 181)
95

Reading Abusing Science
At the end of “Astrology,” and again in the discussion of Ruse’s objections to SC, it was agreed
that, at a minimum, a theory had to be refutable in order to be scientific. That is, testability
is a necessary condition for scientific status even if it’s not a sufficient condition.
But if the objector is right and EVT is nothing more than the tautology, The Principle of
Natural Selection, then EVT is not a scientific theory.
The objection can be summarized in this way:
Tautology Objection to Evolutionary Theory
1. EVT = The Principle of Natural Selection
2. The Principle of Natural Selection = “The fittest organisms survive”
3. “The fittest organisms survive” is a tautology.
4. No tautology is falsifiable.
5. Every scientific theory is falsifiable.
6. Therefore, EVT is not a scientific theory.
While the argument is valid – the conclusion follows from the premises – it does not establish
its conclusion. It is a very weak argument. As Kitcher explains, it goes wrong at its first
premise. EVT is not the Principle of Natural Selection. In fact, as Kitcher observes, a check of
standard texts on EVT reveals that there is no such principle stated in those texts.
There is of course a concept of natural selection that is essential in EVT but it is not applied
primarily to organisms but to genetic material (allelic pairs – Abusing Science, 57) within
organisms. While this might not sound like a big difference, Kitcher spends several pages
explaining in detail how and why it is a big difference.
Here is an analogy that might be helpful. Suppose that I announce my discovery, over
morning coffee, that when I pour milk into my coffee, it gets lighter: “Lightener lightens
coffee!” No one’s going to nominate me for a Nobel Prize. And even if I can say by how much
the coffee is lightened by a given amount of milk, there still won’t be much excitement. But
if, for any two fluids under any conditions in any environment I can say precisely how they
will mix, then I can expect a Nobel Prize and a great deal more, because I will have solved all
of the problems of fluid dynamics.
Saying that EVT boils down to the Principle of Natural Selection is like saying that fluid
dynamics boils down to “Lightener lightens.” The analogy is fairly exact, because the
equations that describe the diffusion of one fluid in another are the same sorts of equations
as those that describe the diffusion of genetic material with specified fitnesses in a
population of organisms.
One further comment:
Any theory or explanation that contains definitions (e.g., of technical terms, or of terms
drawn from mathematics) will contain some tautologies. But containing a tautology is very
different from being a tautology. In the pair of statements
(i) There are bachelors who live in downtown Raleigh.
(ii) B is a bachelor=def. B is a live adult unmarried male human.
(ii) is a tautology but the first is not. So the two statements together – “(i) & (ii)” -make one
96

Reading Abusing Science
more complex statement that is not a tautology and that is refutable.
EVT, like other scientific theories, contains plenty of tautologies. But, for the reason just
given, that does not make it irrefutable or untestable.
Trying Time

On pages 100-106 of Abusing Science, Kitcher considers a common objection made by some
creationists to EVT, according to which there hasn’t been enough time for complex
organisms to evolve from simpler organisms even under assumptions wildly favorable to
EVT.
How could, say, horses have evolved from bacteria? (pages 102-103) Relying on an estimate
supplied by an EVT theorist, the objector assumes that it takes a million mutations to get
from bacteria to horses. In fact, most mutations are not adaptive, but the objector generously
assumes that half are. Any given mutation has a probability of 1/2 of being upward directed,
so to speak. So the likelihood that there will be a favorable sequence of a million mutations
leading from bacteria to horses is the million-fold product of mutations each having a
probability of ½, or 2-1,000,000 which is approximately 10-300,000.
Although advocates of SC reject the current estimate of the age of the universe (about 13.8
billion years) for the purposes of this objection the objector generously assumes that the age
is 30 billion years. If a billion times every second (!) since the beginning of the 30 billion year
old universe, the one million step sequence of mutations had been played out, there would
157
have been 10 such “plays” since the beginning of the universe. But given that the likelihood
of a successful play is only 10
success even remotely likely.

-300,000

even having 10

157

plays would not be enough to make

And of course the assumptions that are being made here are absurdly favorable towards
EVT. So the creationist objector concludes there hasn’t been enough time for complex
organisms such as horses to have evolved from simpler organisms.
The Trying Time Objection to EVT
1. If EVT is true, then horses are likely to have evolved from bacteria under the
described conditions.
2. It is not at all likely that horses would have evolved from bacteria under the described
conditions.
3. Therefore, EVT is not true.
On page 104, Kitcher identifies the central error in this argument. There is something that is
calculated correctly in giving the argument but it is wholly irrelevant to EVT. What is
calculated correctly is the likelihood that a single surviving bacterium, call it Barry Bacterium,
undergoes a million favorable mutations, and that Barry is thereby transformed into a horse,
call him Herman Horse.
Of course, no such process is postulated by EVT. Barry would be dead long before the process
could be carried out.
Instead, the process that EVT envisions always involves an initial population of organisms in
an environment subject to natural selection and a final population of organisms in another
97

Reading Abusing Science
environment, where the populations in question contain hundreds of millions if not billions
of individuals and initial and final populations may be separated by hundreds of millions of
years. A helpful slogan: the Mutation Game is a Team Sport. Once the fact that it is a team
sport played with very large teams over very long time periods, the odds of success increase
exponentially (Abusing Science, 105).
[In “Seeking Scientific Creationism,” below, the discussion of the Random Argument gives
you a third example of a creationist criticism of EVT and Kitcher’s explanation of how it, too,
fails. The conceptual confusions evident in that creationist objection are of particular
importance because being confused about determinism and randomness leads some
creationists to reach too soon for design explanations – before non-design explanations have
been fully and properly explored. This is analogous to what many people do in reaching for
paranormal explanations of coincidences when the coincidences should instead be expected
in the normal course of events.]
Two Reactions to Kitcher’s Abusing Science

Among the reactions to Kitcher’s Abusing Science are two emotionally charged opposites and
it’s worth pointing out how each goes too far.
•
•

EVT is so obviously superior to Scientific Creationsm that Scientific Creationsm isn’t worth discussing.
Even to discuss it is to give Scientific Creationsm more attention than it deserves and there’s a risk
that such attention to it will lead some to over-value Scientific Creationsm.
If the goal is to evaluate the relative merits of EVT and Scientific Creationsm, then why focus so much
on the individual critics of EVT who are the proponents of Scientific Creationsm? And why make so
many negative comments about those proponents? They are simply defending their deeply held religious beliefs and Kitcher has no business attacking those religious beliefs.

Kitcher’s main goal in writing Abusing Science was to clarify the U.S. public debate, which was
at one of its periodic peaks when he wrote Abusing Science, by determining the relative
strengths and weaknesses of Scientific Creationism and of EVT, and to evaluate the main
criticisms made by Scientific Creationists of evolutionary theory. Evaluating the two theories
required him to articulate and defend the relevant criteria for evaluation, which he does in
Chapter 2, after having given a brief outline of EVT in Chapter 1.
There are some instances in which his exasperation with Scientific Creationists comes
through though he is generally quite restrained in that way. As noted, some of what the Scientific Creationists say is so clearly off the mark that it becomes at best difficult to tell
whether they are wholly incompetent to evaluate any scientific theories or are deliberately
presenting what they know is an incorrect description of EVT and its consequences. The suspicion that it's the latter in some cases is made more reasonable when they engage in rhetorical cheap shots. Nor have Scientific Creationists been reluctant to present mutually inconsistent criticisms of evolutionary theory, e.g., some creationist critics have ridiculed EVT
for having-been-tested-and-shown-false and have also ridiculed EVT for being untestable.
This kind of excess and inconsistency creates a vivid impression of critics aiming mainly to
land as many blows as possible, never mind whether any of them is rationally justified. Because Kitcher works so hard at presenting Scientific Creationists’ criticisms at their strongest
and sifts through a great deal of rhetorical noise and invective to extract views that might be

98

Reading Abusing Science
worth evaluating, it may not be apparent to his readers just how flawed the voluminous creationist writings are.
The flaws of much Scientific Creationist writing also gives rise to the worries of many clergy,
Christian and other, that Scientific Creationist attacks on EVT (and much of the rest of science
from which EVT is inseparable) encourage negative attitudes towards religion more generally. Those worries are not baseless. The clergy also worry that by presenting religious beliefs as if they were testable scientific hypotheses requiring empirical confirmation - which
is what many creationists do - they will open religious beliefs more generally to a range of
challenges that they will be unable to meet and which they should not have been asked to
meet in the first place. Even William Dembski, who with Behe was the main architect of modern Intelligent Design Creationism, and who is himself a Biblical inerrantist, is disturbed by
the Biblical literalism of many Scientific Creationists.97
Kitcher uses the criteria that he develops (Abusing Science, Chapter 2) in evaluating both
Scientific Creationism and the arguments given by creationists against EVT. Both Scientific
Creationism and the arguments given by Scientific Creationism against EVT are of course
given by individual authors. He must consider various possible interpretations of what those
individual authors actually wrote to figure out which theory they are advocating and which
arguments they are giving, and he must justify those interpretation(s). So, as a responsible
critic, he has no choice but to mention the authors of theories and arguments. Although he
goes to considerable lengths to interpret what they have written as charitably as possible,
some of what those authors say is so badly confused that it is almost impossible to determine
whether they are utterly incompetent or are knowingly misrepresenting EVT. Of course, this
makes them look bad, but the injuries are self-inflicted. Kitcher does express sympathy with
the plight of some religious parents who are concerned with the welfare of their children and
their educations though he argues that their concerns are often based on misunderstandings
of EVT or its relationships to their religious beliefs. He also quite fairly shows that some of
their rhetorical excesses will backfire (see, e.g., his discussion of rabbit coprophagia).
Kitcher encourages his readers to seek a deeper knowledge of the history of these debates
and of the philosophical issues that have been raised during them. Abusing Science is very
much an introductory text and there is much, much more to be learned about EVT, about
how to evaluate explanations and about the various relationships (which have changed over
time) between what's then called "religion" and what's then called "science." Kitcher himself98 has written other books about these issues and has sought to explain why those debates ought to matter to citizens in a democracy. As noted in the "Introduction," he's never
engaged in the kind of cheap criticism made by the "New Atheists" who like to contrast religion at its worst with science at (what they think is) its best.

97 https://uncommondescent.com/intelligent-design/bill-dembski-on-young-vs-old-earth-creationists-andwhere-he-stands/
98 https://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/?Nty=1&N=0&sugg=&Ntt=philip+kitcher&tab=Books+%26+Media&q=au%3Aphilip+kitcher&Ntk=Author&scope=ncsu

Reading Abusing Science
On the history of creationism in the US, historian of science Ronald Numbers is one of the
best sources, if not the best source.99
Opinions about a theory should not be based on misconceptions about it. Here are some of the
more common misconceptions about evolutionary theory.
Misconceptions about evolutionary theory and processes

Evolution is a theory about the origin of life.
Evolutionary theory implies that life evolved (and continues to evolve) randomly, or by
chance.
Evolution results in progress; organisms are always getting better through evolution.
Individual organisms can evolve during a single lifespan.
Evolution only occurs slowly and gradually.
Because evolution is slow, humans cannot influence it.
Genetic drift only occurs in small populations.
Humans are not currently evolving.
Species are distinct natural entities, with a clear definition, that can be easily recognized
by anyone.
Misconceptions about natural selection and adaptation

Natural selection involves organisms trying to adapt.
Natural selection gives organisms what they need.
Humans can't negatively impact ecosystems, because species will just evolve what they
need to survive.
Natural selection acts for the good of the species.
The fittest organisms in a population are those that are strongest, healthiest, fastest,
and/or largest.
Natural selection is about survival of the very fittest individuals in a population.
Natural selection produces organisms perfectly suited to their environments.
All traits of organisms are adaptations.

100

Seeking Scientific Creationism
Seeking Scientific Creationism
What are the correct criteria for evaluating scientific theories? We've been looking for them,
so far without notable success. What's shaped our inquiry so far is the hope that there is a
simple, clear, sharply defined line between starters and nonstarters in the race for truth. It's
high time that we reconsidered our hope because, it seems, it's gotten us nowhere worth
staying.
Our working model of successful theories has led some to embrace falsifiability as the
essential mark of the scientific. If a theory has any observational consequences at all, then it
should be possible to determine whether it is false by making the appropriate observations.
If, on the other hand, a theory has no observational consequences at all, it will be impossible
to test observationally, and thus incapable of predictive success or predictive failure.
Creationists have sometimes charged that, unlike the rest of science, evolutionary theory is
unfalsifiable and so not science. At least one historian has argued that Creationists operate
with an outdated conception of science, one that was current during the nineteenth century,
when their position was first enunciated in this country. Whether this claim is historically
correct, it is clear now - and has been clear to philosophers of science for at least thirty years,
and perhaps three hundred years - that the naive view of falsifiability and the 'predictive
success' model of theory assessment on which it is based are both fundamentally wrong.
They do not give a correct idea of how science has ever worked. This can be shown by
considering one of the greatest genuine successes of Newtonian mechanics, from which we
can also see how that insight comes into play.
The Discovery of Neptune

Newtonian celestial mechanics is one of the star achievements in the history of science. It
was used to compute the planets' orbits to extraordinary accuracy, and it is still used by
NASA in determining the trajectories of rockets. As predictive successes go, it was a great
one. By 1830, it was quite clear that the observations of Uranus' path diverged from the one
predicted by Newtonian mechanics - it wobbled. So something had to give. Astronomers
were faced with three main options: (a) give up on Newtonian mechanics altogether; (b)
suppose that there were special nongravitational forces operating on Uranus alone; (c)
suppose that there was an unobserved mass exerting a perturbing gravitational force on
Uranus. No one seriously considered taking the first option - but if predictive success were
really all that mattered, then we'd have expected (a) to be actively considered. The fact that
it was not is a clear signal to us that Newtonian mechanics had clearly perceived and wellappreciated virtues other than predictive success. The third option was independently
suggested in 1845 by two astronomers (Adams and Leverrier). They actually computed what
the orbit of a perturbing planet would have to be to explain the deviations of Uranus from
the predicted path. Astronomers looked for the perturbing planet, and, in 1846, Neptune was
discovered. (A similar procedure was followed later and led to the discovery, in 1930, of
Pluto, recently demoted from planet status.)
Although scientists saw that Newtonian celestial mechanics was worth saving, it was not just
because they thought it was a predictively successful theory. It was because it was part of a
larger theory that gave a highly unified explanation of much of what goes on in the physical
world. There are two aspects of this moral to the story.
101

Seeking Scientific Creationism
The first aspect has to do with unification: Newtonian physics had a single small set of
principles and strategies to use in solving a very broad range of problems and answering a
very broad range of questions about the physical world; it had the power of generality that
we have mentioned before (in our discussion of phrenology's alleged lack of systematicity).
The second aspect of this moral has to do with explanation. Although we do want predictive
success from scientific theories, we want more; that is, we want to know more than what will
happen, we also want to know why it will happen (or why it has happened, or why it is
happening - explanation is not restricted to the future; neither, by the way, is prediction).
There have been many times in the history of science when perfectly regular connections
have been observed and summarized in what are called "phenomenological descriptions";
but we are almost never satisfied by mere descriptions of a regularity or pattern. We also
want to know what the underlying reason is for the occurrence of that particular pattern.
To take one example from the recent history of science: atomic spectra were observed
around the turn of the 20th century. When atoms were bombarded with electrons or photons
of certain energies, they would emit radiation in highly regular series, or spectra, and,
contrary to all contemporary expectations, the spectra were full of gaps instead of being
continuous. But being able to predict that they would emit certain patterns of radiation was
not enough: physicists wanted to know why there were gaps, what the reason was for their
occurrence. That was what drove the search for a theory of atomic spectra. To be sure, having
that theory allows additional predictions to be made, which is a desirable consequence; but
the desire for explanation is paramount.
From current physics, we have another example: there are just four known forces in nature,
gravitational, electromagnetic, strong nuclear, and weak nuclear. Ever since it's been thought
that these are all the forces, physicists have been hard at work looking for a deeper
explanation of how they are related to one another, how they might be different
manifestations of a single, underlying reality. Grand Unified Theories (GUTs) promise a
unified understanding of all but the gravitational force. Totally Unified Theories (TUTs)
promise such understanding of all four forces including gravitational force in one unified
scheme. One of the hottest contenders for a TUT since 1983 – Superstring theory or a
successor M-brane theory - says that space has nine dimensions, six of which you haven't
noticed because they are curled up like tiny six-dimensional spheres attached to each point
in three-dimensional walking-around space. That shows you how far scientists will go to get
a wholly unified explanation.100 This is a reminder that science does not seek merely to find
facts, but to explain any facts that it finds.
The best scientific theories suggest new avenues of research, new questions to ask, and new
ways to look for answers. They have what Kitcher calls "fecundity." Whatever its flaws may
have been - and there were plenty - phrenology did force neuroscientists to give serious
consideration to a new hypothesis: localization of brain function. With the traditional
Cartesian view in place, investigation of 'higher' mental functions seemed pointless. It is
largely thanks to phrenology that modern neuroscientific theory enjoys great fecundity. This
is not to say that any theory that causes a lot of activity and investigation is a good one. An
100 See http://www.quantamagazine.org/20150803-physics-theories-map/ for a guide to other theories.

Some of them say that spacetime has a variable number of dimensions. (Have you been feeling dizzy lately?)
102

Seeking Scientific Creationism
awful theory can do that. The point is that scientific theories, even very good ones, can be
incomplete, and that that can be a good sign, or not necessarily a bad one. (This issue comes
up in evaluating evolutionary theory when it is criticized for not giving a complete account
of the origin of life on Earth. What drives the criticism is a misguided metaphor: the theory
is seen as a skyscraper which must have a firm foundation. But a better metaphor is that of
a vast, intricate, but incomplete mosaic or jig-saw puzzle, with many pieces filled in, and
known constraints on how the rest will look. Part of the picture that's missing some pieces
is the part that tells us how self-replicating molecules were formed in the first place.)
In the discussion of phrenology, we examined the idea that the most systematic theories are
the best ones. The five or six qualities that make an explanation systematic come in degrees,
so theories can be more or less good depending on how much of each they have. If we wish,
we may reserve the label "pseudoscientific" for those theories that are almost wholly lacking
in all of these qualities. But there's an important point about Kitcher's use of the term
"pseudoscientific": the difference between science and pseudoscience is not a sharp difference
- as between starters and nonstarters - but a difference in degree. Theories can be more or less
pseudoscientific, and there can be borderline cases. So if we employ Kitcher's way of drawing
the distinction, we should drop the race comparison, and talk simply of good, mediocre, and
bad theories. Our concern will then be with placing theories in a (multidimensional)
continuum, and not with deciding which theories shouldn't be allowed to start the race.
Evolutionary theory belongs in the very best region of this spectrum. There is no concise,
handy principle with which to dismiss theories that deserve to be discarded: there is no
substitute for the hard work of seeing whether a theory has the three complex and subtle
characteristics in question. Kitcher's own practice is good example: his book's very title
makes his attitude toward Scientific Creationism quite clear, but the only way to justify that
attitude is to go through a book-length examination of the Creationist criticisms and
proposals. Gilkey and Ruse thought that something less would suffice; they failed.
With all that in mind, we can evaluate Scientific Creationism fairly.
[I've often been asked if I 'practice what I preach' - always taking time to do the hard work of evaluating
thoroughly every idea that comes my way using the virtues of hypotheses. The answer is, of course, "No, and
that's not what I meant to be preaching.” I do what most people do: use a mish-mash of principles and desires
to decide what to investigate thoroughly. Like most people, I have some fairly well-defined professional and
family obligations that help narrow the field. Otherwise, I'm driven by curiosity, when I can afford that luxury.
Among the principles I use are those that most of us use to decide when (not) to trust a source of information
or assertion. Do they have relevant professional credentials? Have they been a reliable, reasonable source in
the past, and if so, is there anything special about the present case that should make me skeptical - for example,
they hate the theorist whose criticism they’re criticizing, they’ve not had a decent night's sleep in six weeks,
etc. So I am not recommending the abandonment of common sense.]

A Sign of Trouble to Come

It's worth pausing to look at a Scientific Creationist criticism of evolutionary theory that
threatens to gut the latter if it succeeds. In raising some issues about the nature of order and
disorder and how they can be explained, it will help us to evaluate Scientific Creationism's
use of the notion of design.
Everyone agrees that living things, taken either individually or in populations, are highly even hierarchically - ordered. A, perhaps the, main goal of biological theories - Scientific
Creationism and evolutionary theory among them - is to give an account of this Big Fact.
103

Seeking Scientific Creationism
Evolutionary theorists delight in surprising people by saying that the main driving force
behind evolution is a random mechanism, namely, mutation, which produces new bits of
genetic information (alleles). This sounds counterintuitive because, when we think of
randomizing, we think of disorder. Scientific Creationists urge us to hold tightly to that
contrary intuition; it spells doom for evolutionary theory. A quick trip to the dictionary
instructs us that random mechanisms operate purely by chance. When events occur by
chance, they follow no predictable patterns and so cannot be described by the highly regular
patterns mandated by natural laws. Instead, random mechanisms, by definition, produce
disorder - as anyone who's shuffled a deck of cards surely knows. It's just not plausible to
suppose that the random mechanism of mutation could produce the very high degree of
order that it is the goal of evolutionary theory to explain. And dragging in natural selection
as a constraint on the disorder is hardly enough to yield the extraordinarily high degree of
order that living things typify; constrained disorder is still disorder. So, evolutionary theory
deploys precisely the wrong sort of conceptual resources to achieve its explanatory goal. The
theory is rotten at its conceptual core. To summarize:
Random Argument
1. If evolutionary theory is true, then the order of living things is produced by a random
mechanism (i.e., mutation).
2. Random mechanisms operate purely through chance, so are not governed by law and
produce disorder.
3. Living things are highly ordered.
4. Therefore, evolutionary theory is not true
The first and less serious flaw in this argument is in the first premise. Although it is generally
agreed that mutation is the more fundamental of two mechanisms for producing variation,
there is also recombination of existing bits of genetic information. The first premise focuses
on mutation to the exclusion of recombination.
But this is a relatively minor flaw compared to the gross error in the second premise. There
is a distinction - not peculiar to evolutionary theory, but familiar to all of us - between
apparent randomness and real randomness. Many complex processes are wholly
deterministic in the sense that if one knew all the relevant values of variables, one could in
principle predict the outcome. But many such processes are so complex that it's unlikely that
we will in fact know all the relevant values of the variables. Although the motion of every
piece of matter involved may be rigidly predetermined by law-governed physical forces, it
all seems 'random' to us because of our partial understanding of how the forces are acting
on each piece at each moment. Suppose, for example, that I hurl an open box of ping-pong
balls into a room full of people. There'll be ping-pong balls everywhere, bouncing off walls,
furniture, ceiling, and the room's occupants. Eventually, things will settle down and there'll
be a final distribution of ping-pong ball resting places, aptly describable as a chaotic mess.
Although I don't recommend it as a pastime, we could determine this final distribution on a
ball-by-ball basis, calculating the trajectory of each through the obstacle course. Because
there is no theoretical or practical purpose served by doing so, we are content to rest instead
with incomplete, statistical descriptions of the final distribution. In this sense, apparent
randomness is an indicator of acceptable levels of ignorance about complex systems.
104

Seeking Scientific Creationism
Much of what evolutionary theorists talk about as random is just this apparent randomness,
and a lot of progress has been made in molecular biology and mathematics toward fuller
understanding of the processes involved. Another kind of randomness may play a significant
role in mutation, operating at the molecular or atomic level. If what a system will do next is
a really random event, then it is in principle unpredictable, even on the basis of all the
information there is about the system's present state; there is simply nothing in the system
now that determines exactly what it will do next. According to what most physicists think
about certain atomic processes, atomic events are really random. But even in this case, there
are also extremely well-verified laws governing groups of such events (the theory that
explains almost all chemical interaction, quantum electrodynamics, has been verified to over
ten decimal places), the probabilistic laws of quantum mechanics, which give numerically
and geometrically precise descriptions of how these random events array themselves in
groups. Ultimately, these laws generate the highly ordered Periodic Table of the Elements.101
The charge that no patterns are to be expected from random mechanisms is thus wildly off
the mark.
Evaluating Scientific Creationism

The time has now come for us to assess Scientific Creationism as its proponents insist that it
be assessed: as a scientific theory. In our discussion, we must keep firmly in mind that it is
the theory, not its proponents or its origins, that is to be assessed. Its proponents may
themselves be good, bad, or anything in between: that's irrelevant. They may have a hidden
political agenda: that's irrelevant. The view may be religiously inspired: that's irrelevant, too.
So it is no criticism of the theory in this context to say that its proponents want to change the
educational and political system by imposing their interpretation of the Bible and what they
believe follows from that interpretation. Even if all of those charges are correct, they detract
not one whit from Scientific Creationism itself.
This stricture cuts both ways, of course. In this context, it is no defense of the theory to
observe that it is religiously motivated, that it accords well with one interpretation of the
Bible, or that its defenders may be well-intentioned clergy, theists, and parents who want
the best for their children. (And if Scientific Creationism's proponents do wish to claim that
its fitting their interpretation of Genesis is evidence for the theory, then they open
themselves up to challenges for justification and must admit at least the possibility of
counter-evidence as well. Would that be a welcome result?)
So let's take a look at Scientific Creationism. When we try to do so, we encounter a rather
odd problem. Evolutionary theory, right or wrong, is a complex, well-articulated system of
hypotheses and explanations. It's been under development for over 150 years by thousands
of smart people, and it takes many hundreds of textbook pages just to summarize its current
state. But it is very difficult to find any theory at all in the writings of Scientific Creationists.
Once one gets beyond their negative remarks about or criticisms of evolutionary theory,
there is little in the way of a positive view to be found. So a brief look at the best of what they
have to say turns out to be enough.

101 http://periodic.lanl.gov/index.shtml

105

Seeking Scientific Creationism
Scientific Creationism: The Fossil Record

There is no disputing the fact that the fossil record is ordered, indeed stratified. Scientific
Creationism claims that the record was not produced by evolutionary mechanisms but by a
Great Flood that rearranged the remains of organisms made in the initial, simultaneous
creation. There is a good deal of vagueness in what they have to say, and a lot of backtracking
and sheer avoidance of difficult questions. But the most serious challenge to this claim
involves the matter of differential evidence: we have no better evidence for the claim that
aliens have periodically tampered with Earth's biosphere, than we have for believing the
Great Flood story (Kitcher, 132). It is true that the Great Flood story may accord better with
what's suggested by the Bible, but why should that count as evidence here? As long as we are
to understand Scientific Creationists to be proposing a scientific theory, as they insist we do,
we are entitled, even obligated, to ask them: What independent evidence is there for the truth
of this Biblical account, as you interpret it? And they have nothing persuasive to offer.
At this point, and at many others, the proponents of Scientific Creationism take refuge in
ignorance and mystery. They say that the detailed mechanisms for ordering the fossil record
are forever beyond the power of human beings to know, that the ways of God are many and
mysterious, and that it all goes according to God's plan for the world, the Great Design of the
Greatest Designer.
What exactly is wrong with this? There are two possible criticisms here, and we must not
confuse them. Perhaps you expect me to object at this point that mentioning God makes their
reply unscientific. Surely, you might say, if a theory makes reference to God, then it's got to
be religion masquerading as science:
Scientific Creationism is pseudoscience because it makes reference to God, the Great
Designer of the Universe.
But I do not want to object to Scientific Creationism in this way, and neither do any clearheaded critics. For example, Kitcher explicitly declines to make this objection, remarking that,
" ... postulating an unobserved Creator need be no more unscientific than postulating
unobserved [sub-atomic] particles." (125) And we have already considered and rejected
Gilkey's criterion, which is in the same unhappy family as this one. So mere reference to God
is not the issue. (I'll return to this point in “Separation by Reference.”) To see what the issue
really is, let's first look at another claim made by Scientific Creationism.
Scientific Creationism: The Fixity of Kinds

One of the central tenets of Scientific Creationism is that all the basic kinds of organisms were
created simultaneously; it allows, however, that since the Great Flood, some change has
taken place in the forms of some organisms, but not in the basic kinds. So the rule for
organisms and their ancestors seems to be: once of a basic kind, always of that basic kind.
This allows change of only a rather modest sort, because too much change would be
incompatible with the preservation of the structure of basic kinds.
One of the most striking recent successes of evolutionary theory is in accounting for the
newly discovered biochemical similarities among organisms. Humans and chimpanzees not
only share a great deal of genetic material, but are genetically similar, in precisely specified
ways, to horses and carp. So, according to evolutionary theory, animals that share a relatively
106

Seeking Scientific Creationism
recent ancestor have proteins with similar structures. 102
Comparative genomics on some degrees of genetic similarity:
Human to humans
99.5% - 100%
Human to chimpanzees
96%-98%
Humans to cats
90%
Cats to dogs
82%
Cats to chimpanzees
79%
Cats to rats
69%
Cats to mice
67%
Human to mice
92%
Humans to cows
80%
Humans to chickens
60%
Human to fruit flies
44%
Humans to yeast
26%
Human to weeds
18%
Scientific Creationism gives no reason at all to expect common ancestors or genetic similarity
among living things.
Can Scientific Creationism account for this sort of similarity? It is sometimes claimed by its
proponents that these biochemical similarities track with the more traditional similarities
among basic kinds, but this is not true in general. For example, humans and chimpanzees are
alleged to be of different basic kinds, despite the virtual identity of their cells' biochemical
structures. We won't go too far wrong about Scientific Creationism if we think of each basic
kind of organism as the object of a separate decision and creative act by God: God saw that
the plan required beetles, so God created beetles; God saw that the plan required humans, so
God created humans; etc. Given God's nature, however, it was within God’s power to have
fashioned each kind of organism with wholly disparate genetic codes or even from entirely
different materials, one carbon-based, another silicon-based, a third from some other
material stuff. There is nothing in Scientific Creationism that would lead us to expect the
actual overlap among organisms of different kinds.
When pressed, proponents of Scientific Creationism once again claim that the structural
similarities and differences among organisms of different basic kinds are all part of God's
Great Design: organisms have the features that they have because they need them to serve
their parts - their purposes - in the divine plan. What is wrong with this reference to design
is not simply the invocation of a deity, but the fact that it explains nothing. It's not that the
notion of a Great Design is used that's objectionable; it's how it is used in this context.
In this context, I find it very helpful to look at creation as did the seventeenth century
philosopher, mathematician (co-discoverer of the calculus), mining engineer,103 legal
theorist and theologian, Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz (1646-1716).
102 http://www.talkorigins.org/faqs/molgen/, http://www.talkorigins.org/faqs/comdesc/ and http://tolweb.org/tree/
103 http://www.nlb-hannover.de/Leibniz/Leibnizarchiv/english/Leibniz_Life/windmill.htm

107

Seeking Scientific Creationism
II: Against those who hold that there is in the works of God no goodness, or that
the principles of goodness and beauty are arbitrary
... it is in reflecting upon the works that we are able to discover the one who
wrought. They must therefore bear in themselves his character. I confess that the
contrary opinion seems to me extremely dangerous ....
VI: That God does nothing which is not orderly, and that it is not even possible to
conceive of events which are not regular
... God does nothing out of order. ... Thus we may say that in whatever manner
God might have created the world, it would always have been regular and in a
certain order. God, however, has chosen the most perfect, that is to say the one
which is at the same time the simplest in hypotheses and the richest in
phenomena, as might be the case with a geometric line, whose construction was
easy, but whose properties and effects were extremely remarkable and of great
significance. .... Leibniz, Discourse on Metaphysics (1686)
Leibniz expended an enormous amount of his considerable intellectual resources on trying
to understand creation and he devised an excellent model. According to this model, the
universe might have been quite different from the way it actually is - there are infinitely
many possible universe designs. Some differ little from the actual one (you and I might be
missing from the plan); others differ radically in omitting living things altogether and in
having entirely different natural laws (inverse cube, say, instead of inverse square). As an
omniscient being, God knows every detail about every possible design for a universe. As an
omnipotent being, it is within God's power to implement any one of these designs. But as a
supremely good, morally perfect being, it is in God's nature to choose the very best design
and to create a universe in accord with it. So, Leibniz argued, this, the actual universe, must
be the best of all possible worlds. And because God would not have populated this world with
sentient beings wholly unable to appreciate any of the details of His design, Leibniz held, we
are endowed with a divinely given cognitive capacity to discern these details and to
appreciate them through at least a partial understanding of their mutual fit. Science, on this
view, may be thought of as the attempt to discover the details and to answer the question,
Why this design above all others? There are more than a few scientists who view themselves
as professional students of God's Great Design. Some are even evolutionary biologists, who
study (what they take to be) the divinely initiated production process that resulted in us and
the world as we find it.
Once we accept the Scientific Creationists' invitation to assess their theory on its merits, we
find that their vague gesture in the general direction of a Great Design gives no explanation.
If anything is clear about the organisms on Earth, it is that their needs are in large part
determined by their 'design'. If fish didn't need to eat, they wouldn't have mouths, digestive
tracts, etc. Until we are shown, at least in outline, what the Great Design is and how, in detail,
it is a good - indeed, the best possible - design, we have been given no explanation of how its
parts allegedly fit together. So if the reference to Great Design is to offer any hope of
explanation, it must be specified in much greater detail than Scientific Creationism tries to
do. After all, lots of designs were possible: why this particular one?
If, at this point, Scientific Creationism retreats into mystery and ignorance, then it really has
withdrawn from the competition with evolutionary theory. It has conceded that explanations
108

Seeking Scientific Creationism
of the order of living things are impossible on its view.
But the picture is even worse for Scientific Creationism because, in the end, it is not just one
theory in one corner of biology that its proponents urge us to flush; geology, chemistry, and
physics will be forced down the drain, too, and plenty of modern medicine will follow.104 To
see why, let's look at what Scientific Creationism has to say about the age of Earth.
Scientific Creationism: Dating Earth

One of the most widely-known tenets of Scientific Creationism is its claim that Earth is not
over 4 billion years old, as most of science would have it, but just a few thousand years old.
The details of the procedures used by most scientists to date the rocks on Earth and Earth
itself are complex and rooted in extremely well-confirmed, thoroughly tested theories in
physics, chemistry, and geology - not to mention evolutionary theory itself. In radiometric
dating of rocks, one measures the relative proportions of certain radioactive elements in
rocks and then computes the rock's approximate age using a known rate of radioactive
decay. Because the way in which the procedures are applied is genuinely complex, it is open
to misunderstanding and challenge at a number of points. Kitcher discusses several
challenges, responds to them, and tells you where to find additional information. I will
mention just one challenge made by Scientific Creationism for the sake of illustration.
In determining the age of rocks by radiometric dating, one assumes that there is a constant
rate of decay for each element under consideration. Some Scientific Creationists challenge
the assumption of constancy of the rate. That assumption, however, is not a physicist's
fantasy but a consequence of the way that nuclear forces operate. Such forces are well
understood by physicists, and the particular claim of constancy has been subjected to
numerous tests and has passed with flying colors. What we are then faced with here is a
particularly stark choice: Scientific Creationism or modern physics.
Notice, as well, that even if Scientific Creationists were to succeed in raising well-taken
doubts about dating the age of Earth, they would not thereby establish that its age is but a
few thousand years. That requires additional justification - and, as I've emphasized before,
according with an interpretation of the Bible is not by itself sufficient justification.
Similar conflicts arise between Scientific Creationism and other well established chunks of
contemporary science.

In the end, we must reject either Scientific Creationism or much of what is best in science,
including the highest standards and best methods of science.

Seeking Intelligent Design Creationism
Seeking Intelligent Design Creationism
It’s safe to say that there is no more authoritative source on what is essential to Intelligent
Design Creationism (IDC) than Professor Michael Behe, a biochemist at Lehigh University
and Fellow of the Discovery Institute (http://discovery.org the chief advocate for IDC). It’s
safe to say this because Behe was the chief witness for IDC during the Kitzmiller v Dover trial.
It is therefore worth looking at what he has to say about the key conceptual distinction in
IDC and how he deployed it in his trial testimony.
Defining Irreducible Complexity

"By irreducibly complex I mean a single system composed of several well-matched,
interacting parts that contribute to the basic function, wherein the removal of any
one of the parts causes the system to effectively cease functioning. "Behe, M. J.
(1996) Darwin's Black Box: The Biochemical Challenge to Evolution. (39).
"... 'irreducibly complex' means roughly that if one removes a component from a
system, function is lost; ..." Behe, M. J. (2001) "Reply to my critics: A response to
reviews of Darwin's Black Box: The Biochemical Challenge to Evolution." Biology
and Philosophy 16: 685-709. (686).
Behe’s argument against evolutionary theory may be summarized in this way:
Behe’s Irreducible Complexity Argument against Evolutionary Theory
1. There are irreducibly complex biological systems.
2. If there are irreducibly complex biological systems, then there are systems that cannot be produced by adding functional parts to existing functional parts.
3. If there are systems that cannot be produced by adding functional parts to existing
functional parts, then evolutionary theory is false.
4. Therefore, evolutionary theory is false.
In place of evolutionary theory, Behe offers instead a design hypothesis:
Irreducibly complex systems must be the product of design – planning by an
intelligence – and not of evolution.
It is this hypothesis that is the core of IDC.106
A counterexample to Behe’s definition of irreducible complexity can be found in two early
20th century papers in genetics which discuss “Mullerian interlocking complexity”:

106 In his excellent, sympathetic critique of the use of design reasoning, Ratzsch points out that an object need
not be complex for design explanations to be appropriate because an anomalously simple object may call for
such an explanation as well. As an example, he gives something like the perfect and inscrutable black obelisk
in 2001: A Space Odyssey (both movie and book). Del Ratzsch, Nature, Design and Science: The Status of Design
in Natural Science (SUNY Press, 2001). http://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/record/NCSU1606431 Online Resource

111

Seeking Intelligent Design Creationism
Muller, H. J. (1918) "Genetic variability, twin hybrids and constant hybrids, in a case of
balanced lethal factors." Genetics 3:422-499.
Muller, H. J. (1939) "Reversibility in evolution considered from the standpoint of
genetics." Biological Reviews of the Cambridge Philosophical Society 14:261- 280.

David Theobald presents the counterexample simple and effectively: suppose that there are
stones strewn about a construction site which is subject to flooding after heavy rains. Three
stones are pushed into a row by the flood waters. Then with additional flooding a fourth
stone ends up on top of the three. The construction workers use the result as a bridge. With
more flooding, the center stone is pushed far away, leaving a functional bride in place. In
pictures:

Seeking Intelligent Design Creationism
This bridge is, under Behe’s definition, an irreducibly complex system. Yet it is clear that no
design was required. (If you wish, you may imagine that the stones rolled onto the construction site when an earthquake occurred.) Attempts to repair the definition have failed.
Behe’s Testifies at Kitzmiller v Dover: A Report

[Plaintiff's attorney Rothschild's cross-examination of Professor Michael Behe.107]
... Behe ... developed ... his own [definition of "scientific theory"]. Behe prefers to
define a scientific theory as "a proposed explanation which focuses or points to
physical, observable data and logical inferences."
Under his definition ... intelligent design could qualify as a scientific theory, Behe
explained. Rothschild referred to Behe's trial deposition. "I asked you, 'Is astrology
a theory under that definition?' And you answered, 'Is astrology? It could be, yes.'
Right?" Rothschild asked.
"That's correct," Behe conceded.
... Rothschild challenged Behe's dismissal that the "scientific literature has no
answers to the question of the origin of the immune system."
"I see no Darwinian explanation for such things," Behe said, arguing that the
immune system must therefore be irreducibly complex.
Rothschild trudged to the witness stand, plopping down a number of peerreviewed articles and stacks of thick books on the subject of the evolution of the
immune system. ...
After stepping back, Rothschild asked, "Now, these articles rebut your assertion
that scientific literature has no answers on the origin of the vertebrate immune
system?"
"No, they certainly do not," Behe said. "My answer, or my argument, is that the
literature has no detailed rigorous explanations for how complex biochemical
systems could arise by a random mutation and natural selection, and these articles
do not address that."
"So these are not good enough?" Rothschild asked.
So Rothschild tried again. And He continued piling material onto the stand until
the pile dwarfed the professor. By the time he was done, Rothschild had stacked
up ten textbooks such as Origin and Evolution of the Vertebrate Immune System
and fifty-eight articles from prestigious journals like Science, Nature, and
Molecular Cell, all of them detailing research on the evolution of the immune
system. Rothschild stared at Behe over the material "Is your position today that
these articles aren't good enough?" he asked.
... Behe, however, ignored the overwhelming body of work before his eyes. He
gazed over the pile and said he remained unaware of any evidence of work done
"in a detailed, rigorous fashion" detailing "how immune systems or their

Co., 2008) 153-157. https://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/trln/DUKE004078572

Seeking Intelligent Design Creationism
irreducibly complex components could have arisen through natural selection and
random mutation."
Then Rothschild turned his attention to what was really a pretty simple question.
Just as natural selection and genetic mutations are two of the driving forces of
evolution, Rothschild wanted to know, what made intelligent design work? Behe
maintained, in his expert report and in [his book] Darwin's Black Box, that
"intelligent design theory focuses exclusively on proposed mechanisms of how
complex biological structures arose:' He spells out that this is intelligent design's
only claim. Anything else, such as the identity of the designer, is speculation.
So, Rothschild asked, just what are the proposed mechanisms?
Behe couldn't answer the question. Instead, he recited, "We can infer design from
the purposeful arrangement of parts." Each time he repeated the phrase, he
raised his hands in a double-OK sign.
Rothschild repeated Behe's words: "Intelligent design theory focuses exclusively
on the proposed mechanism of how complex biological structures arose."
"Please describe the mechanism that intelligent design proposes for how complex
biological structures arose," Rothschild asked again.
Behe mentioned the Big Bang, he mentioned complex biological structures, he
mentioned explosive processes, but he didn't answer Rothschild's question.
"Back to my original question," Rothschild asked. "What is the mechanism that
intelligent design proposes?" "And I wonder, could - am I permitted to know what
I replied to your question the first time?" Behe asked.
"I don't think I got a reply, so I'm asking you, you've made this claim here,
'Intelligent design theory focuses exclusively on the proposed mechanism of how
complex biological structures arose.' And I want to know what is the mechanism
that intelligent design proposes for how complex biological structures arose?"
Rothschild asked.
"Again, it does not propose a mechanism in the sense of a step by-step description
of how those structures arose," Behe said. "But it can infer that in the mechanism,
in the process by which these structures arose, an intelligent cause was involved,"
"But it does not propose an actual mechanism?" Rothschild asked, refusing to let
it go.
Behe could not name the mechanism because there was none. ...
At one point during the lengthy exchange, Rothschild asked whether the designer
made every [bacterial] flagella or just "the first lucky one."
Behe said just the first one. Rothschild then wanted to know if the designer
designed the mutations in the flagella that followed. "The proper answer we don't
know," Behe said.
Because intelligent design proponents argue that one of the weaknesses of
evolutionary theory is that it cannot be observed, Rothschild asked if intelligent
design is observable. Behe said it is.
Rothschild asked Behe if he had witnessed newly designed structures appearing
in the past five years. Behe said the designed structures that he's written about
are much older. Rothschild asked if it was true that the intelligent designer might
114

Seeking Intelligent Design Creationism
not actually exist any longer, that he could be, in fact, dead. Behe agreed that was
true.
Rothschild ... asked, "Is that what you want to teach school students, Dr. Behe?"
None of this shows that IDC is not science. As was pointed out in the discussion of the third
criticism of astrology, knowing the exact mechanism by which a phenomenon takes place is
not necessary for initiating good scientific investigation.
Nor is the point to assess Behe's character or to infer properties of IDC from Behe's attitudes
towards it; his attitude would tell us nothing about the defining properties of IDC.
But theories must come from some source, and it is legitimate to rely on a source regarded
as authoritative by all sides for information about what the theory is.
Behe is regarded as one of the leading experts on IDC. The fact that he was unable to give any
substantive detail to IDC is good evidence that IDC has none to offer. In this respect IDC is
very much unlike modern evolutionary theory and molecular biology.
Given that no workable definition of "irreducible complexity" has been given, there is no way
to tell which physical systems IDC is allegedly needed to explain. And given further that no
detail is provided about how intelligent design would be involved in producing irreducibly
complex systems (if only one could tell which systems they were), what IDC seems to come
to is the expression of a hope that the idea of intelligent design will be of some value in biology.
That is no kind of explanation, and it is therefore not an alternative explanation to evolutionary theory.
The best reason not to teach IDC in the science classroom is that there is nothing to teach
that's comparable to evolutionary theory.
This also explains the way in which Judge Jones’ finding that IDC is not testable is misleading
at best. He implies that there IDC is a theory and that it is not testable. But considering what’s
just been pointed out about IDC, it is incorrect to say that IDC is a theory and thus at best
grossly misleading to say simply that IDC is not testable: there is no theory to test.
Legal issues aside, it might help to educate science students about the importance of relevant
detail and the explanatory power of evolutionary theory to have it contrasted with IDC as an
example of an appealingly simple, intuitive idea that has not been endowed with comparable
explanatory power despite having been around for over two millennia. This pedagogical use
of IDC would not please its proponents but pleasing them is not the purpose of science education.
Costly Confusion #3: Science aims mainly to find facts.

During Fall 2010 – Spring 2015, over 2400 students enrolled in PHI 340 responded to surveys asking for their opinions on issues discussed in the course.108 The same survey was
given at the beginning and at the end of each term. One of five responses was allowed for
each of the fifty survey items: strongly agree, agree, neither agree nor disagree, disagree,
strongly disagree. For six of the survey items, there was a significant negative relationship
between strength of agreement on the end-of-term survey and the grade-determining
108 You can find a summary of survey results for 1986 -2015 in the Getting Started in PHI 340 folder near the

top of the course web site home page. There are many interesting patterns in the responses.
115

Seeking Intelligent Design Creationism
course score. Each of the six items expressed a common mistake argued against in the
course. Students who persisted in making these mistakes thus tended to perform less well.
One of the items was:
The main, defining aim of science is to discover facts.

116

Interminable Debate?

Interminable Debate?
At about this point, I am often asked a question of the following sort:
Creationists and evolutionary theorists have been arguing with one another for a long
time. Neither side seems to make any progress in convincing the other and neither side
has proved its theory. They seem to be wasting their time. Why do they keep arguing?
On the claim that "neither side has proved its theory," it depends what one means by
"proved." By any reasonable standard - by the standards articulated in Chapter 2 of Abusing
Science - modern evolutionary theory is, as Kitcher explains in the rest of Abusing Science, a
massively well-confirmed theory with enormous explanatory power; Scientific Creationism
is inconsistent with massively well-confirmed theories not only in biology but in physics,
chemistry and geology and Scientific Creationism is devoid of explanatory power.
The misimpression among many that there is some sort of parity between the two theories
is the result of the failure of the US public school system to provide adequate science
educations, which leaves many graduating high school students profoundly ignorant of even
the rudiments of evolutionary theory (as presented for example in Chapter 1 of Abusing
Science). (I’m not saying the teachers are solely to blame. The blame is widely distributed.)
Explaining why Scientific Creationists continue to press their case given that the theory they
advocate has the aforementioned deficiencies is beyond the scope of this text and my
expertise. It is of course not uncommon for people to persist in beliefs after they have been
wholly discredited by reason and evidence, but the psychological account of the irrationality
usually depends on the specifics. I’ll offer some brief speculations.
Without question, design explanations, or as I prefer to call them, purpose-directed
explanations (PDEs) are important, useful and can be used correctly. But they can be and
often are used incorrectly. Paul Bloom of the Yale University Department of Psychology
suggests that there is an innate tendency in human beings to use PDEs very broadly. (See also
Paul Bloom and Deena Skolnick Weisberg, “Childhood Origins of Adult Resistance to
Science.”109) The 18th century Scottish common-sense philosopher Thomas Reid may also
have made a similar suggestion; this is discussed by Del Ratzsch in his Stanford Encyclopedia
of Philosophy article, “Teleological Arguments for God's Existence.”110
One of the reasons that PDEs are appealed to prematurely and thus incorrectly is that human
beings tend (as discussed briefly in “Parapsychology”) to be very bad at reasoning about
likelihoods and coincidences, and thus are surprised at coincidences when correct reasoning
would lead them to expect the coinciding events instead. And when the events are surprising,
people tend to reach for PDEs when there is no need to do so. There is a huge research
literature in psychology that documents how persistently bad even smart, well-trained
people (e.g., graduates of top medical schools) are at reasoning about likelihoods with
sometimes deadly consequences. (The recent literature begins perhaps with the alreadymentioned: Amos Tversky and Daniel Kahneman, “Judgment under uncertainty: Heuristics
109 http://www.yale.edu/minddevlab/papers/bloom&weisberg%20science.pdf
110 http://plato.stanford.edu/entries/teleological-arguments/

117

Interminable Debate?
and biases,” Science v185 (1974) 1124-1131, which has since been cited by other authors at
about 200,000 times in over 135,000 scholarly journal articles, and continues to be cited.
And that count does not include citations in books or non-scholarly periodicals. Kahneman
won a Nobel Memorial Prize in Economics in 2002 for this research; Tversky missed out by
dying in 1996.)
Because Scientific Creationism is the view of only a relatively small minority even among
Christians one might reasonably wonder why it has received so much critical attention. The
answer is that Scientific Creationists were far more skilfully and energetically active
politically than the majority and hence had a disproportionate effect on legislatures and
school boards - at least until the US Supreme Court invalidated “Creation Science” laws in
Edwards v Aguillard. The IDC movement was conceived quite deliberately as a political
strategy - "the Wedge" - for dealing with that political defeat, as its originator, University of
California at Berkeley law professor Philip Johnson explained.111 It, too, seems to have been
defeated in Kitzmiller v Dover.
But there are new efforts underway that have already had effects on public education in
Louisiana and may have effects in Alabama, Florida, Kentucky, Indiana, Missouri, Oklahoma,
Tennessee, Michigan, New Mexico, Ohio, South Carolina, Texas and other states. They portray
the issue as one concerning academic freedom to address the strengths and weaknesses of
any theories discussed - which sounds in itself very fair and reasonable - but where
evolutionary theory (and more recently anthropogenic climate change) is singled out for
special attention when it is claimed further that it has serious weaknesses best addressed by
discussion of creationist alternatives.112
Many of the proposed laws imply that the best way to deal with "controversial claims" is to
"present all sides and let students decide for themselves." (You can probably think of examples pertaining to human role in climate change, health insurance reform, banking regulation, same-sex marriage, abortion, vaccination and other issues in public debates - as well as
evolution). To apply such laws, one would need to know what, according to the law, makes a
claim controversial, what a side is, and how to present the sides; we'd also need to know what
kind of decision procedure the students should use when they “decide for themselves.” For
now, focus on the first issue: what makes a claim controversial?
One meaning of "controversial" is this:
x is controversial if a significant number of people (usually taken to be a number large
enough to tip the balance in an election) do in fact controvert x - that is, if they reject
or forcefully express doubts concerning x.
Call this the ‘IF-controversial meaning’ ("IF" for "in fact"). So, for example, it would be true
that
It is IF-controversial that smoking causes cancer.
It is IF-controversial that wearing a seat belt while driving saves more lives than it
costs.
It is IF-controversial that having unprotected sex increases the risk of getting a STD.
111 http://www.antievolution.org/features/wedge.pdf

112 About the latter sort of proposal in Texas, the NY Times published a letter that I wrote; a copy, “Strengths

and Weaknesses,” is available online at http://www.nytimes.com/2008/06/12/opinion/lweb12evolution.html.
118

Interminable Debate?
Because being IF-controversial does not require that those who forcefully express doubts
about or reject x have much if any knowledge about x, the simple fact that a claim is IFcontroversial is not a good reason to have any doubts about or reject x. ("Wherever there's
smoke, there's got to be fire," is wrong: sometimes, there's just a lot of dry ice in water.)
Another meaning of "controversial" is:
x is controversial if a significant proportion of those with relevant knowledge reason
correctly about that knowledge to reject or raise serious doubts about x.
Call this the ‘E-controversial meaning’ ("E" for "effective expert"). So, it would be true that
It is not E-controversial that smoking causes cancer.
It is not E-controversial that wearing a seat belt while driving saves more lives than
it costs.
It is not E-controversial that having unprotected sex increases the risk of getting a
STD.
because in each case, all or almost all the relevant experts agree that smoking does cause
cancer, wearing a seat belt while driving saves more lives than it costs, and having unprotected sex does increase risk of getting a STD. It might even be that "The ratio of the circumference of a circle to its diameter is a rational number" is IF-controversial even though it is
not E-controversial (perhaps because many people who understand the claim pretty well are
nonetheless confused enough to believe that any ratio must yield a rational number). Given
the results of some surveys of US high school students, it seems to be IF-controversial that
the US Civil War occurred during the 19th century!
The proposed laws rarely rely on the common-sense distinction between these two meanings of "controversial" as they would have to if they were actually intended to improve the
quality of education.
So the short but superficial answer to part of the question with which this chapter began is,
"Politics." A non-superficial answer would have to be sought in courses in religious studies,
history, political science, sociology and psychology. It would certainly be worth seeking such
an answer.113

113 See http://www.culturalcognition.net/ for interesting attempts to understand these tendencies in human

behavior.

This is the first time since 1982 -- when Gallup began asking this question using this wording -- that

by scientist Charles Darwin and others. Since 1982, agreement with the "secular" viewpoint, meaning humans evolved from lower life forms without any divine intervention, has doubled.
Americans' Views on Human Creation Related to Education, Religious Preference
Higher education levels are associated with less support for creationism and higher levels of belief
in the evolutionary explanation for human origins. Belief in creationism is 21% among those with postgraduate education versus 48% of those with no more than a high school diploma. Agreement with evolution
without God's involvement is 31% among postgrads versus 12% among Americans with a high school education or less.
However, even among adults with a college degree or postgraduate education, more believe God
had a role in evolution than say evolution occurred without God.

Views by people's religious preferences or lack thereof paint an illuminating picture as well. More

while those who attend church less regularly have less consensus on the question of human origins.

Data from a nationwide survey shows students who list their affiliation as "none" has skyrocketed
The number of college students with no religious affiliation has tripled in the last 30 years, from 10 percent
in 1986 to 31 percent in 2016, according to data from the CIRP Freshman Survey.118 Over the same period,
116 Allen Downey is a Professor of Computer Science at Olin College in Needham, MA. He is the author of Think

the number who attended religious services dropped from 85 percent to 69 percent. These trends provide
a snapshot of the current generation of young adults; they also provide a preview of rapid secularization in
the U.S. over the next 30 years.
Since 1966 the Cooperative Institutional Research Program (CIRP) has surveyed incoming college students
about their backgrounds, beliefs, and attitudes, including questions about their religious preference and
attendance at religious services. In 2016, they surveyed more than 137,000 first-time students at 184
colleges and universities in the U.S.
Figure 1 shows how the number of students whose religious preference is “None” has changed over time.
The retreat from religion starts around 1990 and accelerates, averaging almost 1 percentage point per year.

Credit: Allen Downey
Most of this growth comes at the expense of Catholicism, which dropped from 32 percent to 23 percent,
and mainstream Protestant denominations including Baptists (from 17 percent to 7 percent), and
Methodists (from 9 percent to 3 percent). At the same time the number of students choosing “Other
Christian” increased from 5 percent to 13 percent.
The fraction of “Nones” is higher at universities, 36 percent, than at four-year colleges, 26 percent, mostly
because more colleges than universities are religiously affiliated. Not surprisingly, religious colleges are
more religious, with only 17 percent Nones; and historically black colleges even more so, with 11 percent
Nones.
Starting in 2015, the CIRP survey includes “Agnostic” and “Atheist” in the list of religious preferences, along
with “None.” For consistency with past data, Figure 1 shows the total of these categories. In 2016, the
breakdown of students with no religious affiliation is 8.5 percent Agnostic, 6.4 percent Atheist, and 16
percent None, with all three categories up slightly since 2015.
Men are more likely than women to identify as Agnostic (10 percent versus 8 percent) or Atheist (8 percent
versus 5 percent). But it is not clear whether these differences are based on divergent belief or willingness
to identify with stigmatized labels like “Atheist."
Overall, more men than women report no religious affiliation, by about 4 percentage points. Figure 2 shows
that this gender gap has increased over time, but the rate of growth may be slowing.

Almost everyone in this survey is a traditional age college student—98 percent of them are 17-19 years
old—so this dataset provides a preview of the next generation of young adults. College students are not a
representative sample of the general population, but we can extrapolate.
According to the U.S. Census Bureau,120 65 percent of people in the U.S. age 25-24 attended some college,
so they could have appeared in the CIRP sample. That leaves 35 percent of the general population
unrepresented.
Using data from the General Social Survey, I looked at the relationship between religious affiliation and
college attendance (details in this Jupyter notebook).121 Since 2010, the fraction of Nones has been 4-6
percentage points lower for people who did not attend college.
Combining these results, I estimate that 29 percent of young adults have no religious affiliation, not very
different from the college students in the survey, at 31 percent.
This dataset provides a snapshot of students at the beginning of their college experience, but it doesn’t tell
us whether people are likely to join a church, or leave one, later in life. In a future article, I will explore
lifetime changes in affiliation and belief.
(Downey's analysis of data from the CIRP Freshman Survey is in this Jupyter notebook.122)

So, reports suggest significant changes in attitude and behavior on religious matters in the
US. But how much does this really tell us? Reponses to survey items can be understood only
if one knows how the respondents interpreted the items. Did all these respondents interpret
the items in the same way? Did they all take the word “God” to have the same meaning?

120 https://www.census.gov/content/dam/Census/library/publications/2016/demo/p20-578.pdf
121 https://github.com/AllenDowney/GssReligion/blob/master/college_religion.ipynb

122 https://github.com/AllenDowney/HeriReligion/blob/master/freshman/freshman_survey17.ipynb
