Evolution of Opinion about Evolution: US and NC State 1982-2017
If one asks the theologians from the most popular Western religious traditions – Judaism,
Christianity and Islam – for a definition of the word “God” as used in their tradition, then
their answers will overlap considerably and are motivated by similar arguments: they all
report that the standard definition is that God is the Supreme Being (where “supreme” is in
turn a defined term) because such a being is most worthy of worship. There are four
characteristics with respect to which God is supposed to be supreme, namely, knowledge,
power, goodness and existence.
Supremacy in knowledge: omniscience. “Omniscient” means knowing every truth at
every time.
Supremacy in power: omnipotence. “Omnipotent” means being able to do anything
logically possible, given one’s other essential characteristics.
Supremacy in goodness: omnibenevolence. “Omnibenevolent” means being wholly
incapable of any moral error, always preferring the better to the worse, being morally
absolutely perfect. (This implies that there is one objective standard of goodness.)
Supremacy in existence: necessary existence. “Necessarily existent” means existing
independently and in every logically possible situation.
In addition, the definition of “God” in these traditions includes three stipulations:
God is a purely spiritual, non-physical being;
as the creator of the universe, God is causally responsible for the universe’s existence
– all contingent, non-necessary beings (e.g., human beings, angels) depend on God for
their existence;
and
there is exactly one God (monotheism: the one and only Supreme Being).
Because these seven characteristics are taken to be part of the definition of the term “God,”
it is also supposed that each characteristic is essential: God not only has these characteristics
but must have them. (An analogy: the definition of “triangle” is makes being three-sided
essential to being triangle, but not, say, the characteristic of being purple. So, triangles must
all have exactly three sides, but none need be purple though some might be.)
The word “define” is sometimes used with the connotation of “limiting” or “restricting”
privileges, so some protest that giving such a definition of “God” is somehow disrespectful or
illegitimate. But that’s to confuse saying what a word means with trying to restrict the
activities of whatever the word applies to. It would be as silly to object, “How dare you
attempt to define the word, ‘God’!” as it would be to respond to a correct definition of
“triangle” with, “How dare you attempt to define the word, ‘triangle’!” Without a clear
definition of “God,” religious believers would literally not know what they were talking
about, and it would be disrespectful to them to accuse them of such profound ignorance
about the being that they’re worshipping.
When the surveys reported on above were done, those conducting the surveys did not check
on respondents’ ability to report the standard definition of “God.” While many of the
respondents were no doubt somewhat familiar with the standard definition, they might not
have had it firmly in mind when they responded. That might not make much difference to
the way in which the survey responses are interpreted. [Again, an analogy with “triangle” is
useful. Most people know that triangles must be three-sided. But they might not be clear on
whether all triangles must have internal angles that sum to 180 degrees or must have sides
that are straight line segments. And there are many theorems about triangles that also yield
necessary truths about them but which most people have forgotten or never knew. It seems
126

Evolution of Opinion about Evolution: US and NC State 1982-2017
reasonable to allow ourselves to talk about partially understanding a definition. In some
contexts, a quite limited partial understanding of a definition might be acceptable; in other
contexts, it might be necessary to acquire a less partial (i.e., fuller or better) understanding,
perhaps by consulting with the relevant experts and considering the arguments that
motivate the definition. American first-graders and high school students have a partial
understanding of what “triangle” means, and the high school students typically have a better
understanding than do first-graders, but even they will often lack the more nearly full
understanding that a mathematician would have. The high school students might be
surprised to hear that the internal angles of triangles can sum to less than 180 degrees or to
more than 180 degrees because they have learned about only plane triangles; but no
competent mathematician would be surprised.123]
With all of that in mind, let’s take a brief look at the various things that “God” might mean to
members of a few religious groups represented in the US population.
While it’s good to keep it in mind that “’data’ is not the plural of ‘anecdote’,” I have a great
deal of anecdotal evidence that some theists are not clear on what their own religion’s
definition of “God” is. In many discussions during the past forty-six years, I have each and
every term heard from students who seemed willing or anxious to abandon one or more of
the characteristics designated as essential in the definition of “God,” most often omnibenevolence, omniscience and being non-physical.
Fortunately, researchers in the cognitive science of religion have gone beyond anecdote to
collect and analyze relevant data, and what they report is consistent with what I have
observed. Here is an excerpt from one review of the relevant literature.
Larisa Heiphetz, Jonathan D. Lane, Adam Waytz, Liane L. Young, “How Children and Adults Represent God’s Mind,” Cognitive Science 40 (2016) 121–144 DOI: 10.1111/cogs.12232
… cognitive science has shown that, under some conditions, adults hold more anthropomorphic
views of God [than the standard definition allows]. Borrowing from prior work … we define anthropomorphism as the attribution of a human-like mind to non-human agents, objects, or phenomena. Importantly, this operationalization focuses on the attribution of a human-like mind (rather than the attribution of human-like behavior or appearance), given that both lay theories and
philosophical definitions of personhood center on mind as the defining feature of humanness. In
particular, this definition of anthropomorphism involves attributing emotions and analytic abilities that people perceive to be uniquely human, such as hope, guilt, prospection, and self-reflection …. This definition also includes attributing limitations of the human mind, such as ignorance,
to non-humans. Thus, anthropomorphic representations of God feature human characteristics
such as honesty, human emotions such as happiness, or human limitations such as ignorance. In
contrast, non-anthropomorphic representations of God are those in which God’s mind is represented as distinct from human minds. Within the domain of knowledge, for example, representing
God non-anthropomorphically would involve attributing knowledge to God that would not be attributed to humans.
In a study highlighting the boundary conditions of adults’ distinction between God’s mind and
human minds, Shtulman (2008) asked undergraduates at an American university as well as adults
from the community whether a set of adjectives typically used to describe humans (e.g., honest/dishonest, happy/sad) could be used to describe three types of beings: (a) religious beings
(angels, messiahs, Satan, and God); (b) fictional beings (fairies, ghosts, vampires, and zombies);
and (c) human beings. Adults attributed fewer human-like adjectives to religious beings than to
123 See, e.g., http://www.math.cornell.edu/~mec/mircea.html

127

Evolution of Opinion about Evolution: US and NC State 1982-2017
fictional beings (and fewer human-like adjectives to fictional beings than to actual humans), showing that, at an explicit level, adults rejected the idea that God has certain human-like properties.
Yet participants still attributed, on average, more than three (out of nine) human-like traits to
God. Although the traits were not necessarily uniquely human, Shtulman (2008) argued that these
findings reflected some degree of anthropomorphism as the traits are typically used to describe
humans. If anthropomorphism were entirely absent, participants would attribute zero human-like
traits to God. Furthermore, the majority of human-like traits attributed to God were psychological
(e.g., honest/dishonest) rather than biological (e.g., alive/dead) or physical (e.g., hot/cold). This
pattern of results shows that adults perceive that God, like humans, has a mind that engages in
human-like psychological processes.
Although adults report that God shares some human-like psychological traits, they also report
that God’s mind is different from human minds in certain respects. In a recent study, a primarily
Christian sample of adults completing an online survey responded, on average, that God could
have agency (the ability to plan and intend) but not experience (the ability to feel certain emotions; …). In this framework, God could form goals, but God could not be happy when those goals
were fulfilled, a result that may be partially explained by the specific emotions examined. For
example, adults were asked about the extent to which God could feel emotions associated with
bodily states (e.g., hunger, thirst) and reflection on one’s own wrongdoing (e.g., embarrassment).
Participants may have responded that God lacks the capacity for experiencing these specific emotions because Jewish and Christian Scriptures refer to God as flawless (e.g., “As for God, His way
is perfect” [Psalm 18:30]) and without physical needs (e.g., “God is a Spirit” [John 4:24]). Additionally, the Judeo-Christian view of God posits that God is bodiless, which may increase the
agency and reduce the experience attributed to God …. Indeed, other work has shown that adults
often attribute other emotional experiences, such as love, anger, and wrath, to God ….
In summary, although adults report that God shares some human-like psychological traits (e.g.,
the ability to feel love), they also report that God’s mind is different from human minds in other
respects. For example, adults commonly express the idea that God has more knowledge than do
humans and that, unlike humans, God is unable to experience emotions associated with reflection
on one’s own wrong actions, such as embarrassment. However, adults’ explicit reports may not
always match their implicit representations, and it is to this evidence we turn next. …[emphasis
added]
Participants heard stories such as the one below:
It was a clear, sunny day. Two birds were singing back and forth to each other. They were
perched in a large oak tree next to an airport. God was listening to the birds. One would
sing and then the other would sing. One bird had blue, white, and silver feathers. The
other bird had dull gray feathers. While God was listening to the birds, a large jet landed.
It was extremely loud: the birds couldn’t even hear each other. The air was full of fumes.
God listened to the jet until it turned off its engines. God finished listening to the birds.
The story is consistent with a theologically correct view of God’s perceptual abilities. For example,
the story mentions that the two birds could not hear each other over the noise of the jet but does
not say that the jet interfered with God’s ability to hear. Nevertheless, when paraphrasing the
story, many participants exhibited anthropomorphism by attributing human limitations to God.
For instance, one participant stated, “The noise was so loud God couldn’t hear the birds.” Such
paraphrasing occurred although most participants explicitly endorsed a theologically correct view
of God’s mind, claiming, for example, that God could know everyone’s thoughts, perform multiple
mental activities simultaneously, and hear things from far away. These findings suggest that participants’ explicit descriptions of God’s mind differ from their implicit representations.
128

Evolution of Opinion about Evolution: US and NC State 1982-2017
Participants may say that God is everywhere, knows everything, and defies physical constraints,
but on some level they also represent God’s mind as far more human-like.
… participants’ own ideological beliefs correlated more strongly with the ideological beliefs they
attributed to God than with the ideological beliefs they attributed to other people. Another group
of researchers … obtained a similar result: Christian adults perceived Jesus (who is portrayed as
God or God’s son in Christian traditions) to hold the same ideological beliefs they did, but more
strongly. That is, liberal Christians reported that a contemporary Jesus would hold an even more
liberal ideology, while conservative Christians reported that a contemporary Jesus would hold an
even more conservative ideology.
In summary, adults implicitly anthropomorphize God’s mind despite displaying an explicit tendency to distinguish God’s mind from human minds. For example, on an explicit level, adults may
attribute complete knowledge and superhuman perceptual abilities to God. However, on an implicit level, adults show similar patterns of brain activity when thinking about God and another
person—especially themselves. The differences between adults’ explicit and implicit religious cognition suggest that perceiving God’s mind as human-like may be implicit and that distinguishing
God’s mind from human minds may require deliberate reasoning. In the following section, we
draw on the developmental literature to investigate the origins of adults’ religious cognition ….

When interpreting survey results or engaging in face-to-face discussion of religious belief, it
would be prudent to try to discover how key terms are being understood at a time and over
time.

survey of 4,729 participants conducted December 4-18, 2017. The results are reported in

in a higher power, but only a slim majority [56%] believe in God as described in the Bible. (April
24, 2018).124 On page 10 of the report there is a chart – one of many such– that makes it clear
that few think of God as a supreme being as characterized above. Here’s a summary:

"Two Amish Men Accused of Cocaine Deals With Motorcycle Gang," Hanna Rosin, The
Washington Post June 24, 1998
Federal prosecutors in Pennsylvania yesterday accused two Amish men of buying cocaine from
a gang called the Pagan Motorcycle Club and distributing the drug to other young members of
the religious group at parties known as "hoedowns."
"We've seen plenty of underage drinking cases but a drug case is unheard of" among the Amish,
said John Pyfer, who is representing Abner Stoltzfus, 24. The other defendant is Abner King
Stoltzfus, 23, who is no relation.
The case in Lancaster County underscores the vulnerability of the Amish, who have seen
suburban development and tourism encroach on their once secluded lives. The suburbanites
and the gawkers have made it difficult for the Amish to close their eyes to what they consider
the corrupting influences of modernity. Members of the sect do not have electricity or
plumbing in their homes, and still make their way around the county in horse-drawn buggies.
The two men accused belong to the most conservative Amish sect, the Old Order Amish.
Most work as farmers or craftsmen, and do not stray far from their homes. Abner Stoltzfus
worked as a roofer, Pyfer said, and met Pagan members on his work trips outside Lancaster.
The two men were indicted yesterday on charges of participating in a conspiracy to distribute
more than $ 1 million worth of cocaine and methamphetamine. Federal prosecutors placed
most of the blame on eight members of the motorcycle gang, who were described as reckless
and violent. Emory Edward Reed, president of the Pagans' Chester County chapter, broke one
of the defendant's legs with an axe handle when he refused an order, and knocked out the
other defendant's teeth when he failed to pay on time.
While members of the biker gang were known as habitual lawbreakers, no one from the Amish
had ever been involved in such serious criminal activity, attorneys said. The two Amish men
allegedly bought the drugs from gang members between 1992 and 1997 and distributed them
at parties of youth groups known as the Antiques, the Crickets and the Pilgrims. A juvenile
identified only as CS also participated in the conspiracy, according to prosecutors, but was not
charged in the indictment.
Pyfer said "it was pretty clear" his client had been addicted to cocaine, although he no longer
is. No date has been set for the arraignment, but Pyfer said his client would plead not guilty.
During the five years the two men were allegedly distributing cocaine, they were participating
in an Amish rite of passage translated loosely in English as "sowing your wild oats," said Pyfer.
Amish men between the ages of 16 and 24 take a long break from the rigid rules of the
community to decide if they want to opt out. During the break, the men drink and drive "bright,
gaudy cars," said Pyfer, while "their parents are looking the other way." Taking drugs is not an
accepted part of that rite, however.

Bobby Jindal, “Physical Dimensions of Spiritual Warfare,” The New Oxford Review (December

“Bobby Jindal [former Governor of Louisiana and candidate for the 2016 Republican nominee for US

… Susan and I had developed an intimate friendship; indeed, our relationship mystified observers, who insisted on finding a romantic component where none existed. I called her after the
University Christian Fellowship (UCF) meeting …. Though the interdenominational group's
weekly program of songs and prayers had produced the usual emotional high among most
members, Susan had left the meeting in a very sullen mood. … I found that she had not gone
far, but was sobbing uncontrollably outside the auditorium. … She had noticed a lump on her
scalp, had visited the university health clinic, and had a biopsy performed. … The results had
indicated skin cancer. … Susan started saying words like “visions” instead of nightmares, and I
began to get worried and scared. …
Susan started describing various odors (which others would later ascribe to the sulphur that
supposedly accompanies the devil), sounds, and appearances that both she and her roommate
had witnessed. They had even called maintenance, which had found the odors but not the
cause. Her roommate, neither charismatic nor Christian, had seen, heard, and smelled the
same things, but had not known how to interpret the events …
While Susan's older sister flew in to provide comfort during this trying time, Susan visited the
doctor for one last set of tests. UCF had organized a prayer meeting that night for Susan's upcoming operation and the intense emotional trials she had endured … The meeting started, as
did any other UCF gathering, with group songs and a few prayers. We sat in a circle on the floor
so we could face each other. … Susan's sister then asked for a period of meditative prayer; the
entire group would fall silent while individuals would pray aloud as the Spirit led them. This is
a common practice in both Bible studies and group meetings within UCF. …
Suddenly, Susan emitted some strange guttural sounds and fell to the floor. She started thrashing about, as if in some sort of seizure. Susan's sister must have recognized what was happening, for she ordered us to gather around and place our hands on Susan's prostrate body. I refused to budge from my position and froze in horror. I will never forget the first comprehensible
sound that came from Susan; she screamed my name with such an urgency that the chill still
travels down my spine whenever I recall this moment. … I tentatively approached the group
and placed the edge of my fingertip on her shoulder, as if afraid of becoming infected with the
disease that was ravaging her body. I had yet to realize that the affliction was ravaging her soul.
In a voice I had never heard before or since, Susan accused me: “Bobby, you cannot even love
Susan.” Before I even noticed the sound of her voice, I thought it funny that Susan would refer
to herself in the third person. … Knowing that I was doing Susan no good, I quickly retreated to
the opposite side of the room. Susan proceeded to denounce every individual in the room,
often citing very private and confidential information she could not possibly have known on
her own. It was information capable of hurting individuals, attacking people, as she did, by
revealing their hidden feelings, fears, and worries. The night was just beginning!

The students, led by Susan's sister and Louise, a member of a charismatic church, engaged in
loud and desperate prayers while holding Susan with one hand. Kneeling on the ground, my
friends were chanting, “Satan, I command you to leave this woman.” Others exhorted all demons to leave in the name of Christ. It is no exaggeration to note the tears and sweat among
those assembled. Susan lashed out at the assembled students with verbal assaults. … Susan's
sister sent someone to call a local minister experienced in such matters. … Susan chose the
perfect opportunity to attempt an escape. She suddenly leapt up and ran for the door, despite
the many hands holding her down. This burst of action served to revive the tired group of students and they soon had her restrained once again, this time half kneeling and half standing.
Alice, a student leader in Campus Crusade for Christ, entered the room for the first time, brandishing a crucifix. Running out of options, UCF had turned to a rival campus Christian group for
spiritual tactics. … Alice's presence countered Susan's recent burst of energy, and Alice's confidence inspired us all. Surely Crusade's experienced leader would be able to rescue us and reaffirm our faith in Christ, the Bible, and everything good. … Susan's lunge for the door awakened
and invigorated me. Strangely, I found myself repeating the Hail Mary until it became a chant.
… The crucifix had a calming effect on Susan, and her sister was soon brave enough to bring a
Bible to her face. At first, Susan responded to biblical passages with curses and profanities.
Mixed in with her vile attacks were short and desperate pleas for help. In the same breath that
she attacked Christ, the Bible's authenticity, and everyone assembled in prayer, Susan would
suddenly urge us to rescue her. It appeared as if we were observing a tremendous battle between the Susan we knew and loved and some strange evil force. But the momentum had
shifted and we now sensed that victory was at hand. While Alice and Louise held Susan, her
sister continued holding the Bible to her face. Almost taunting the evil spirit that had almost
beaten us minutes before, the students dared Susan to read biblical passages. She choked on
certain passages and could not finish the sentence “Jesus is Lord.” Over and over, she repeated
“Jesus is Lord,” often ending in profanities. In between her futile attempts, Susan pleaded with
us to continue trying and often smiled between the grimaces that accompanied her readings
of Scripture. Just as suddenly as she went into the trance, Susan suddenly reappeared and
claimed, “Jesus is Lord.”
With an almost comical smile, Susan then looked up as if awakening from a deep sleep and
asked, “Has something happened?” She did not remember any of the past few hours and was
startled to find her friends breaking out in cheers and laughter, overwhelmed by sudden joy
and relief. …
Susan stayed in the house of a missionary with experience in spiritual warfare in foreign countries. Her sister thought it best she stay out of her own room. Susan's roommate, the daughter
of a Hmong faith healer, had decorated the room with supposedly pagan influences. Other
theories explaining the night's events soon surfaced. Susan's mother had once worshipped and
offered a sacrifice at a pagan altar in the Far East for her husband's health; though he had been
healed, she had been warned not to repeat such practices, but had returned to that same altar
in the Far East upon hearing of Susan's illness. The UCF staff member dismissed Susan's affliction as a psychological disorder, precipitated by the semester's stress, and advised her to seek
professional help. Susan, who had experienced visions and other related phenomena as a child,
thought her intense flirting with guys and straying away from God had led to this punishment.
When the operation occurred, the surgeons found no traces of cancerous cells. Susan claimed
she had felt healed after the group prayer and can remember the sensation of being “purified";

We would all like to know why the two Abners went astray. Given that a significant
percentage of the American population believes in angels, fallen or otherwise, there seems
to be a gap in the educational program of proponents of Scientific Creationism. "The Devil
made me do it" would seem from their perspective to have as much to recommend itself as
"God made all living things." But Scientific Creationists have not pressed for similar
application of purpose-directed explanation in psychology and the other social sciences.
Why not?
It may be that modern Satanism (not to mention various Inquisitions126) has given this idea
a bad name, but that suggests that it is all the more important for proponents of Scientific
Creationism to rescue the underlying ideas from such bad press. The matter becomes even
more puzzling when we look at the depth of parallels between Scientific Creationism and
Angelic Science. For the sake of brevity, let's look only at Satanic Science, or demonology; but
much of what can be said about the latter will also apply to the systematic study of non-fallen

Demonology Has Scriptural Warrant

Like Scientific Creationism, Satanic Science is well-grounded in the Bible and in subsequent
theological writings. (I say "is" rather than "would be" because there is an extensive
professional literature, focusing on both theory and application, in Christian demonology
alone. More of that later.) For example, the book of Revelation describes Satan in the form of
a ten headed dragon as he does battle with the armies of God, led by Michael. At Job 4:13: "If
God places no trust in his servants, if he charges his angels with error.." The following
passages at least can also be given as scriptural warrant for belief in angels:
Old Testament references: Cherubim at entrance to Garden of Eden Gn 3:24; Appear
to Abraham Gn 18:1-33; Saved Lot and his family Gn 19:1-22; Intervened with Isaac
Gn 22:11-18; Heavenly vision of Jacob's Ladder Gn 28:12; Accompanied Israel

1:19. Fall of the angels: Dt 32:17; 2 Pt 2:4; Jude 1:6; Rv 12:7-9.127
The Notion of Satanic Beings Does Important Conceptual Work

The notion that there are satanic influences in the universe also plays a key conceptual role
in the Free Will Defense, one resolution of the ancient Problem of Evil, most forcefully stated
as a challenge to the existence of God as typically conceived in the main Western religious
traditions128:
The Problem of Evil
1. If God exists, then there is an omnipotent, omnibenevolent, omniscient creator of the
actual universe (everything, as it actually is).
2. If there is omnipotent, omnibenevolent, omniscient creator of the actual universe,
then the actual universe is be the best possible universe.
3. If the actual universe is the best possible universe, then the actual universe contains
no evil.
4. The actual universe does contain evil.
5. Therefore, God does not exist.

127 Thomas J. Farrar & Guy J. Williams, “Diabolical Data: A Critical Inventory of New Testament Satanology,”

Journal for the Study of the New Testament v39 n1 (2016) 40–71. “Abstract: This study counts references to
Satan in the NT, by any designation. … Through exegesis, a final count of 137 references to Satan in the NT is
obtained. An alternative, probability-weighted approach estimates the number at 129.2 ….”
128 This may not be the way that you conceive of God (or gods), but even if your beliefs are not at issue here,
the beliefs discussed are so widely held that they are worth considering carefully. It is a very safe bet that you
will spend a good deal of time in the company of others who do have the conception of God described here. In
addition, there are powerful arguments (not given here) for the conception of God as the Supreme Being, and
of course the idea itself is a very interesting one.
135

Angelic Science
The first premise is true by the relevant definition of "God," as the Supreme Being. (See the
chapters, “Separation by Reference” and “Separation by Explanation” for more.)
The second premise is true because God, as an omniscient being, will know which universe
is best; being omnipotent, God will have the power to create any possible universe; and being
omnibenevolent, God, as a morally perfect being, must choose to do what is morally best by
creating the best possible universe.
In favor of the third premise, it seems clear that a universe containing no evil at all is better
than a universe containing some evil.
And the fourth premise seems to have all too much support: our own small corner of the
universe often seems teeming with evil.
This problem has a very long history, with many versions having been studied and many
more replies offered.129 The following summarizes replies made by past PHI 340 students to
the Problem of Evil. Each reply is followed by one or more responses describing further
issues, but it is not the purpose of this discussion to settle the issues; that would take far
more than several semesters’ work.
Reply 1: Why suppose that God is a supreme being? The Old Testament of the Bible says that
God kills, is vengeful and wrathful, etc. Even the New Testament says similar things. Doesn’t
the Bible say or imply that God is less than omnibenevolent?
Responses:
(i) Authoritative theologians from the three main Western religions (Christianity, Judaism, Islam) agree that omniscience, omnipotence, omnibenevolence and necessary existence are among the defining attributes of God.
(ii) Why settle for less than supremacy? That is: why worship a less-than-supreme being?
If it were not omnibenevolent and was still very powerful (and not omnipotent),
wouldn’t that be a very frightening being, more like a powerful alien from outer
space? Being frightened of someone is not the best reason for taking a worshipful
attitude towards that individual.
(iii)

There are many who do believe that God is the supreme being, and you live or
work with (or will live or work with) them. You yourself don’t have to believe in a
supreme being to find the Problem of Evil interesting.

Reply 2: There can be good only if there is also evil.
Response: Why is it impossible for a universe to be wholly good?
Reply 2a: It is necessary for human beings to understand and appreciate the difference between good and evil. Human beings can understand and appreciate the difference between
good and evil only if there are actual examples of each.
Responses:
(i) Why would it be necessary for human beings to understand and appreciate the difference between good and evil – especially if there were no evil in the
129 In evaluating replies to the Problem of Evil, it is useful to take a Martian perspective; see the “Introduction.”

136

Angelic Science
universe?
(ii) Even if understanding the difference is necessary, human beings understand lots of
distinctions without experience of what’s distinguished. For example, you
know without having suffered a fatal car crash that it would not contribute to
your well-being for you to be in one. You understand what unicorns, zombies,
vampires, Santa Claus and Batman would be even though they do not exist.
(iii)

The supreme being could create beings with any needed concepts in mind from
birth – innate concepts - already.

Reply to Response: But if the supreme being gave us such innate concepts we (a)
wouldn’t be free; or (b) we would never make any moral mistakes.
Responses:
To (a): If someone has real expertise and acts on it, we praise them for their
excellent work. We don’t say, “Oh, you’re an expert. You knew how to do that.
You deserve no credit.” Even if moral excellence resulted from expert application of innate concepts, that wouldn’t show that the application wasn’t praiseworthy.
To (b): Someone can have a concept but misapply it. We might have innate
concepts of good and bad but still make moral mistakes because we don’t always apply the concepts correctly.
Reply 3: There isn’t really any evil in the universe. What seems evil isn’t evil.
Response: We sometimes make mistakes about what’s good and what’s evil, and some of what
seems evil might not be. But is nothing whatever that we find evil actually evil? Not the Holocaust? The Russian Gulag? Black Plague? The rape, torture and beheading of innocent infants? The burden of proof will weigh very heavily on anyone who says nothing is really evil.
Reply 4: What’s good or evil is only a matter of opinion. I’ve got my opinions and the supreme being has other opinions. It’s all relative. Can’t we all get along? Be tolerant!!
Response: There is an absolute standard of goodness built into omnibenevolence. Therefore,
the Problem of Evil assumes that whatever is evil (or good) according to God, is what’s evil
(or good). And isn’t it right to be intolerant of evil?
(If you insist on talking about opinions, then you could say: the supreme being’s “opinion” is
the only one that counts. It’s better however to avoid such misleading “opinion”-talk. See also
Whyte on the supposed right to one’s opinion and “Separation by Fact and Value” on the
limits of tolerance and Naïve Moral Relativism.)
Reply 5: The ancestors of all human beings behaved badly when they could have chosen to
do better. All the descendants of those first humans therefore suffer original sin. It’s our own
(damnable) fault that there’s evil in the universe.
Responses:
(i) Why couldn’t God have designed the universe without including in it human beings
who behaved badly a long time ago? Why couldn’t God have created beings who
did not have such rotten natures? Isn’t that within the power of a being with God’s
137

Angelic Science
attributes? (See above, on innate concepts.)
(ii) Suppose that a great-great-great grandparent acquired some information that they
were not entitled to (e.g., looked at someone else’s business records thus gaining
an unfair advantage over a competitor) or for that matter suppose that the greatgreat-great grandparent murdered someone. Should the descendants of that
great-great-great grandparent, e.g., you, be prosecuted? Imprisoned? Executed?
Why hold you responsible for an action long ago in which you had no part
(because you’d not yet been born!)? And why suppose that bad natures are
heritable (so that you must share the warped character of any of your ancestors)?
Why then ought the descendants of those human beings who behaved badly a long
time ago be punished for (or inherit the defects of) those human beings’ bad
behavior long ago?130
Reply 6: The sacred scriptures of my religion (e.g., the Bible) tells me that there’s no problem
raised by the Problem of Evil.
Responses:
(i) What does the Bible (for example) say that tells you that? (Do you read the Bible in
its original languages or do you use a translation? Is the translation correct? How
do you know that the translation is correct without having checked the original?
Did you know that Biblical scholars may have to know over ten languages to do
their work [e.g., Akkadian, Arabic, Aramaic, Armenian, Coptic, Egyptian, Elamite,
English, Ethiopic, French, German, Greek (several kinds), Hebrew (several kinds),
Hittite, Latin, Phoenician, Slavonic, Syriac, Ugaritic]? And that’s just for two of the
hundreds or thousands of religions of the world.
(ii) Why do you – and why should others – rely on that one sacred scripture among the
many thousands that are available? (See the “Introduction.”) There are after all
very many things in which one could have faith: how do you choose one from all
the possibilities? When you were young, it may have made sense to accept whatever parents or other authorities told you. Now, you are responsible for what you
believe. Why continue to accept what you did as a child? Should everyone do that?
Even if they’ve been raised to kill those who don’t share their beliefs?
Reply 7: I’ve had direct experience of the supreme being and my experience makes me absolutely certain that there’s no problem raised by the Problem of Evil.
Responses:
(i) You probably know, or know about, some people who claim to be absolutely certain
of things that aren’t so. Some of them are hospitalized, to prevent them from acting on what they are sure of. Consider this pairing:
Paranoid schizophrenic (with no religious experiences): “I have heard the voice of the
supreme being. The supreme being told me that the agony of bone cancer and rape of
130 Remember that in taking a Martian perspective, it is not enough to refer to one’s favored sacred scriptures
because doing so will inevitably prompt the question, Why accept those scriptures as sacred given the apparently problematic reply that those scriptures give rise to?

138

Angelic Science
young children are really good and not evil.”
Person with Genuine Religious Experience: “I have heard the voice of the supreme being.
The supreme being told me that the agony of bone cancer and rape of young children are
really good and not evil.”
There must be some way of telling the difference between those who are deluded and
those who are not.
What is it about the experience that makes it one rather than the other? (Every religion
that values religious experience has some way of telling the difference. No one wants to
accept a false claim about what a divine being has said, if they believe that there are divine
beings.)131
(ii) You don’t live in isolation. You act on your beliefs (minute by minute). Your actions
affect others. You sometimes very much want others to agree with you. You vote.
If your direct experience of the supreme being carries any weight with you, then
it will often be important for you to be able to persuade others to accept beliefs
that stem from that experience. How will you do that?
Reply 8: The supreme being gave human beings free will. For without free will, humans
would do nothing right or wrong: humans would be morally inert robots. That would be terrible. Although humans might be made in the supreme being’s image, the image is a diminished one because all humans are less than supreme. So, with free will, human beings make
mistakes. That’s what introduces evil in the universe.
Responses:
(i) Why couldn’t the supreme being, as an omnipotent and omniscient being, engineer
the universe so that humans are free, and they don't make moral errors? If God
created humans with a very high-grade, innate knowledge of good and evil – a
better version of what some call a “conscience” – then humans would behave
themselves, even if they could make mistakes. Isn’t such a human being called
“saintly”?
Question: If the supreme being is omniscient, then at every time the supreme being knows
what every human being will be doing at that time. How could humans be free if the supreme being already knows this?
Answer: There’s a difference between “will do” and “must do.” Even if you do in fact
do X, it doesn’t follow that you could not have done otherwise (not-X). So even if the
supreme being knows that you will do X, it doesn’t follow that the supreme being
knows that it was impossible for you to do other than X. Nor does the supreme being
make you do X if you choose to do X.
Another Question: If God knows what every human will do, then God could always stop
humans from doing evil. But God can’t always stop humans from doing evil things without robbing them of free will.
131 Anthropologist Tanya M. Luhrmann http://luhrmann.net/about-the-author/ has done some very interest-

ing research on how various communities make the relevant distinctions.
139

Angelic Science
Answer: That wasn’t a question, but it does deserve a response. Consider this example:
You see that a tree limb is about to fall on a child who is clearly unaware of the danger. You know that
you are able freely to move the child out of harm’s way with no risk to yourself. If the limb falls on the
child, then that will kill the child, and you know that as well. You have as good a reason as any human
being ever has to believe that the child’s death will not be a good thing and will be a bad thing. (For
example, you have no reason to believe that the child carries a deadly mutation that could spread and
result in much suffering; on the contrary, you have as much evidence as any human being ever has that
the child carries no such mutation. And so on.) It seems clear that you morally ought to move the child
out of harm’s way.
Suppose also that the tree limb is about to fall on the child because another human being in the tree is
knowingly and freely choosing to make the limb fall on the child, knowing what the effect on the child
will be: severe injury or death. Suppose again that you have as good a reason as any human being ever
has to believe that the human being in the tree is freely so choosing and that the situation is otherwise
as described in the first paragraph above.

It’s still equally clear that you morally ought to move the child out of harm’s way. And
if you do what you ought to do, it has no effect on whether the human being in the tree
chose freely; intervening cannot retroactively erase the free nature of that (reprehensible) choice.
This kind of case is a challenge to the Free Will Defense (even if augmented to include
superhuman beings; see Reply 11, below) against the Problem of Evil. As an omniscient being, the supreme being knows for each and every free choice made by any beings, human or otherwise, what the consequences would be. [The supreme being has
after all decreed the laws that govern the behavior of all less-than-supreme beings
and thus knows all about the causes of that behavior. As an omnipotent being, the
supreme being can stop those consequences from occurring. (Such a being is not subject to any "cosmic speed limits.") As an omnibenevolent being, the supreme being in
the Problem of Evil recognizes a moral obligation to stop those consequences from
occurring if the consequences are bad.] As in the case of the child, the supreme being’s
intervention cannot retroactively erase the free nature of the choices. But the supreme being as described in presenting the Problem of Evil does not always intervene,
either for the free choices of human beings or the free choices of superhuman beings.
Rejoinder: Effects of free choices include effects on the mental states of those
who are doing the choosing. If the supreme being always intervened to prevent any bad consequences of any free choice, then the beings making those
choices will believe and feel that their choices don’t matter, and they will become despondent - unless the supreme being also changes their beliefs and
feelings to compensate. But such profound, frequent “corrections” to their
mental states will make it impossible for them to make decisions based on beliefs and desires that are their own; that is, their choices would not be free after
all.
Response: Why would such frequent intervention render the choices themselves less than free? Why couldn’t the supreme being hide the relevant effects
if that would be to the good? At the very least, some additional argument is
required here to support the claims made in the rejoinder.
140

Angelic Science
(ii) Why would it be terrible for humans to be morally inert robots if the entire universe
would be better if they were? There is after all much more to the universe than
human beings.
Reply 9: It’s all a mystery. Human beings are utterly unable to fathom the supreme being’s
nature (“divine hiddenness” as it’s often called). It would be the sin of pride to think that humans could understand the supreme being. No mere human should even hope to understand
the supreme being’s standards of good and evil. Human beings may well be radically mistaken about good and evil. Humans must take it on faith that everything goes according to
the supreme being’s design of the universe which, given the nature of supremacy, must be
the best design.
Responses:
(i) See the responses to Reply 3, 4, 6 and 7.
(ii) In Response to Reply 7, it was emphasized that religious beliefs are typically not morally inert: they motivate believers’ actions and believers very often want other to
behave similarly because they believe that is morally required. Think for example
of debates about abortion and euthanasia – matters of life or death where religious beliefs are highly relevant.
If the supreme being’s nature and reasons are wholly mysterious, why would anyone
believe in or respect or have any other (favorable) attitude towards the supreme being? How could anyone who did not already believe be persuaded to do so? Consider
this mini-dialogue:
“You must worship the supreme being!”
“Why?”
“The supreme being is mysterious. There is no answer to your question!”
“Are you threatening me?”
“YOU MUST WORSHIP THE SUPREME BEING!”
“Is fear the only motivation you can provide? Do you think that ‘might makes
right’? Is your supreme being a tyrant? How would you know even that much if the
supreme being is mysterious? For that matter, how would you know that the supreme being is good – or anything else?”
“It has been revealed!”
“So, the supreme being isn’t really mysterious – just difficult to get to know?”
(iii)

Even if a theist were oddly indifferent to the threat of moral inertness, there is an
enormous cost to supposing the supreme being is mysterious. The University of
Notre Dame’s late Professor Gary Gutting puts the point well:
The great theistic religions respond to deep human hopes, most importantly our hope
to be ultimately safe … in a world of peril. That is not to say that there’s nothing to
religion beyond this hope, only that a worldview without it will not be religiously fulfilling. … God, then, must be the sure source of that … [safety]. He must be good in
the sense of being fully committed to working for our … [safety] … and powerful to
the extent of assuring that no external circumstances … will interfere …. These are
what we might call the conditions of religious adequacy for a concept of God. … The
141

Angelic Science
only viable answer to the question How could an all good and all-powerful God allow
the evils of our world? is that such a God would have knowledge beyond our understanding. … the problem of evil is solved only by an appeal to our ignorance. …
The problem of evil … does require theists to reject conceptions of God that put him
on a moral plane with humans. … disarming the problem of evil requires thinking of
God as radically different from humans on the ethical level. These differences may
even lead us to raise questions about whether our faith can guarantee the ultimate
security [safety] that it promises. (emphasis added)132
Part of Gutting's argument can be summarized in this way:
1. Any religion guarantees ultimate safety.
2. Any religion according to which there is exactly one God guarantees ultimate
safety only if God guarantees ultimate safety.
3. God guarantees ultimate safety only if God is the supreme being.
4. Therefore, any religion according to which there is exactly one God is also a
religion according to which God is the supreme being.
However, the Problem of Evil, Gutting says, shows that such a God will be morally
incomprehensible, and that being morally incomprehensible will inevitably raise
doubts about whether ultimate safety is what God guarantees. How, after all, can one
trust fully what one cannot comprehend? There is thus a 'trade-off' between believing
in a morally perfect (omnibenevolent), all-powerful (omnipotent), all-knowing (omniscient) being, on the one hand, and satisfying the hope for ultimate safety, on the
other hand.
Reply 10: The Free Will Defense: (a) The best universe must contain some beings with free
will to be the best. (b) Beings with genuinely free will sometimes make moral mistakes. (c)
These mistakes result in evil in the best universe.
Responses:
(i) It should be clear by now that each of (a), (b) and (c) needs to be supported by arguments. 133
(ii) However, not all the evil in the world results from the freely made mistakes of
human beings. For example, there are natural disasters – natural evil - for
which humans are not responsible and over which humans little or no control.
The Free Will Defense does not account for such “natural evil.”
Reply 11: The Free Will Defense, Augmented: The best universe must contain some beings
with free will to be the best. Beings with genuinely free will sometimes make moral mistakes.
These mistakes result in evil in the best universe. Natural evil results from the free choices
of superhuman evil beings – Satan and his legions.
Response: See (i) of Response to Reply 10.
132 Gary Gutting, What Philosophy Can Do (Norton, 2015), Chapter 6. “Religious Agnosticism,” 122-125.
133 If you want to see some such arguments, carefully developed at the necessary length, use the [OPTIONAL]
Readings on the Problem of Evil on the home page to find them.

Angelic Science
None of the preceding discussion is intended to show that God (= the supreme being)
does not exist. The Problem of Evil, which has been around for over two thousand years, is
far too difficult to resolve in even several semesters. Rather, the preceding discussion is intended to show that the augmentation in Reply 11, i.e., the claim that
A fallen, superhuman entity Satan and his legions exist and affect the universe.
can have a serious role to play in addressing a difficult problem for religions that together
have over four billion adherents. This in turn helps to reinforce the central claim of this chapter: it is puzzling that none of the many creationists who believe that Satan and his legions
exist and interfere in human lives has ever suggested that demonology be part of the public
school (or public college) social science curriculum.
Satanic Science has Texts for Teaching

Like Scientific Creationism, modern demonology would be able to rely on a substantial prior
literature, one that stresses both theory and application. For theory, one of the best known
sources is the Malleus Maleficarum,134 the main guidebook of the Inquisition.135 As Paul
Feyerabend remarks in "The Strange Case of Astrology,"
The Malleus is a very interesting book. ... The description of phenomena is sufficiently
detailed to enable us to identify the mental disturbances that accompanied some cases.
The aetiology is pluralistic, there is not just the official explanation, there are other
explanations as well, pluralistic explanations included. ... alternatives are discussed and
so one can judge the arguments that lead to their elimination. This feature makes the
Malleus superior to almost every physics, biology, chemistry textbook of today. Even
the theology is pluralistic, heretical views are not passed over in silence, nor are they
ridiculed; they are described, examined, and removed by argument. The authors know
the subject, they know their opponents, they give a correct account of the positions of
their opponents, they argue against these positions and they use the best knowledge
available at the time in their arguments.
Of course, the way in which the theory was used by the Inquisitors was often itself horrific,136
but the theory is not to blame for the Inquisitors’ excesses; after all any theory can be
misapplied by, e.g., unscrupulous power-mongers. And in a time of shrinking budgets for
public education, the Malleus is free.
Satanic Science Aims to Explain Important Phenomena

In both biology and psychology it is important as well as very interesting to explain
malfunction as well as normal function, for it is in this way that the normal is clearly
distinguished from the abnormal. Some moral urgency may also attach to the restoration of
normalcy for a creature, human or otherwise, who has fallen below normal. Teratology,
which studies congenital malformations, provides some of the most poignant examples of
malfunction in the life sciences, and evolutionary theory and Scientific Creationism must
134 http://www.malleusmaleficarum.org/table-of-contents/

135 http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eMkjvCKTK3Q&list=PL9F2B35865253BA80

as horrific as some have alleged, not that this affects the point here http://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/record/OUPPb7928419

Angelic Science
both have something to say by way of explanation of these sometimes horrendous
"mistakes" of Nature.
Of course, for Scientific Creationism, there aren’t really any mistakes in God's design; there
are only apparent flaws that seem unnecessary because the relevant purpose-directed
explanation has not been given sufficient depth - and, on the Creationist view, it may not
always be within human powers to do so. Likewise, abnormal psychology and demonology
need to address abnormal human thought and behavior, not only to aid in restoration of
normal function, but also to help us assess the degree to which moral responsibility for
behaviors may be diminished.
Many complain about the "medicalization" of evil in modern psychotherapy and psychiatry;
by letting people off the hook as diseased, some say, we miss the very real evil that infects
their behavior. If, however, we are to make the relevant moral judgments reliably, we must
take special care that a demon-based purpose-directed explanation is given due
consideration. For example, in the heart-rending case of the two Abner Stolzfus's, as
described at the beginning of this section, one may wish to consider the possibility that this
apparent failure of family values to take appropriate hold in these young men is really the
work of the devil. (This is not to imply that the religious views of the Amish themselves would
require consideration of this possibility.)
For application, there are carefully specified procedures for exorcisms, religions’ main
therapy for demonic possession. These procedures include detailed guidelines on
differential diagnosis: it is crucial to determine if the person presenting with undesirably
deviant behavior is suffering from a physical or mental disease, or if the person is in fact
possessed. In one especially careful article on Multiple Personality Disorder (MPD), at his
web site on trauma and religious issues, Father Joseph Mahoney137 recommends the
following precautions:
If someone is diagnosed as having MPD based on other personalities, a demonic
presentation should be presumed to simply be another personality, unless clearly
demonstrated otherwise.
An MPD personality will have an identifiable time of formation and functional role
within the personality system consistent with the trauma as it was occurring. MPD
specific therapy will result in psychologically consistent change in that personality, with
improvement over time in the presence of a healthy therapeutic alliance.
As the personality is worked with, emotions such as rage will be clearly "human" in
origin, and if the personality is more developed there will be the clear existence of
state-dependent learning. Knowledge possessed will be appropriate to the role and
function of the personality.
Unusual phenomena will be those seen and reported in at least some other patients
clearly diagnosed with MPD, and will follow the general patterns for those phenomena.
Unusual phenomena consistent with true possession would be clearly outside the

"natural order" and/or would be situations not reasonably accounted for by science.
There are strange phenomena that are frequently seen and observed in MPD patients.
They are accepted as MPD dynamics, and are often present with some personalities
and absent with others. In different patients, they may or may not be linked with a
subjective sense of "being evil" and are clearly linked both to trauma and attempts at
adapting to that trauma. These phenomena would include at least the following:

Susceptibility to hypnosis and an unusual ability to cause others to enter
hypnotic or trance states.
Body memories having physical characteristics. These are re-enactments of past
trauma and follow the same characteristics as the classic stigmata phenomena.
They may appear and disappear without external manipulation and may include
rashes, welts, cuts, burns, blood, swelling, and significant physiological changes.
Apparent telepathy, clairvoyance, and unexplained knowledge. These may
reflect hyperacute senses, such as hearing thoughts reflected in the movement
of the larynx. Photographic state-dependent memory, extremely acute
awareness of others' body language and visual cues, and unusual mental feats
are also common.
Physical strength beyond ordinary perceptions of what is humanly possible.
Highly accelerated healing, control of bleeding, and ability to regulate
physiological states in ways not normally considered possible or under
conscious control.
Ability to cause in an observer a sense of cold, evil, or threat.
Acts of self-harm and extreme self-mutilation, hatred of God and religious
objects.
Ability to go for long periods with neither food or sleep.
Selective, personality specific anesthesia and the blocking of normal pain
stimuli.

It should be noted that many unusual phenomena that are considered in popular
culture to be "psychic" or otherwise unexplainable are often based on the skills and
illusions of magicians, the use of various forms of trance states, suggestibility,
intentional fraud, etc.
These, of course, are just the sorts of guidelines that we'd hope would be carefully observed
when demonic hypotheses about deviant behavior were also being taken seriously.138
Historians of medicine have recounted demonic explanations offered in past centuries of
long past plagues. But what were once seen as incidents of demonic possession are now
understood as the result of simple physical ailment, such as poisoning or epilepsy. Hence
differential diagnosis will be difficult and important.
There is even some limited clinical evidence for efficacy of exorcism, where it may seem to
work when standard therapy and medication did not. Here, too, we must be careful to
