A Second, Broader Problem with Separation by Reference

Even if we put aside these observations about the difficult-to-observe and alleged
unobservables, there is something far more serious that is wrong with Separation by
Reference. We can see what it is by pressing the second question about Separation by
Reference: which subject is alleged to be off-limits by the proposal?
If we confine our attention to the most popular religions in the US, then we can give an agreed
upon answer. According to the typical understanding of the main Western religious
traditions, Christianity, Judaism and Islam, God has at least seven defining characteristics,
most of which should be familiar from our discussion of the Problem of Evil in “Angelic
Science.”
The first four derive from God’s supremacy in four respects. God is said to be supreme in
knowledge, power, goodness and existence. So God is, according to the main Western religious
traditions, necessarily:
•
•
•
•

omniscient (knows all truths at all times),
omnipotent (can do anything consistent with his nature),
omnibenevolent (supremely good - there couldn't be a better being, morally), and
necessarily existent (depending on nothing and no one for His existence).

In addition, these religious traditions say that God must be:
•
•

creator of the universe (is causally, as well as morally, responsible for its existence),
incorporeal (is not like a large astronomical body in outer space, but a purely spiritual
being)

and, finally, a characteristic that I suspect you've been taking for granted but which is quite
distinctive of these religious traditions:
•

there's exactly one God.

Christianity, Judaism and Islam are, after all, monotheistic.
But, of course, as indicated in the “Introduction,” it would be the height of bigotry, narrowmindedness and provincialism to confine our attention to the most popular religions in the
US. There is enormous diversity of religions and religious beliefs. If one counts religious
sects, some of which differ sharply, even violently, on matters of religious doctrine, then
there are thousands of religions now in existence. A crude grouping might reduce the number
to a few score, but no lower. (See the “Introduction.”) Among the matters these religions
disagree about is the proper object of worship. The word "god" (or "God" or some translation
thereof) may be used, but what is expressed by it can differ radically from religion to religion.
There are religions according to which there are many gods, and not only one - polytheistic
rather than monotheistic religions. Polytheistic religions have one advantage over
monotheism: they can readily account for evil and disorder in the world as the result of
disagreement among less-than-supreme deities; monotheism has a tougher time with the
Problem of Evil (see “Angelic Science”). Supremacy of other sorts is denied to their object(s)
175

Separation by Reference
of worship by other religions. There is one Christian sect which maintains that God is a
physical object - a certain body. There are religions with hundreds of millions of adherents
according to which there are no gods at all. As theologian Rita Gross remarks,
[A] standard dictionary definition of 'religion' as recognition by human beings of a
controlling superhuman power entitled to obedience, reverence and worship
applies to the main Western traditions, but not Buddhism, Taoism, Shintoism or
many others.161
So a fatal defect of Separation by Reference is that it ignores the diversity of religions.
Now that we have noted this fact, I am not going to harp on it, but you should keep it in mind
nevertheless. Obviously, we cannot accept a Separation Principle that consigns to oblivion
the religions of billions of people. Just because certain religious groups are in the majority in
this country, those in the local majority are not entitled to ignore large religious groups
elsewhere. Inevitably, the fact of religious diversity will make finding a satisfactory
separation principle far more difficult, because we'll need to say, for example, what it is that
Judaism, Buddhism and Jainism have in common in virtue of which they are distinct from
science. But there's no point in pretending that our task is any simpler than the relevant facts
demand. (We’ll return to this important issue in “School Board Problems.”)

According to the reference work Guide to the Gods (also cited in the Introduction), there are
over 18,980 deities that have been supposed to exist by various religions of the world. But
this understates the possible variety in religious beliefs, because some religions suppose that
there are many deities and there could be an extremely tolerant, all-embracing religious view
that accepts all possible combinations of the 18,980, a very large number.
Still, these enumerations and classifications omit, over-simplify and gloss over deep differences within the categories labeled - and they are only the existing religions. There are other
less popular religions and probably infinitely many other possible religions.
Costly Confusion #4: Science is about only what can be directly observed

During Fall 2010 – Spring 2015, over 2400 students enrolled in PHI 340 responded to surveys asking for their opinions on issues discussed in the course.162 The same survey was
given at the beginning and at the end of each term. One of five responses was allowed for
each of the fifty survey items: strongly agree, agree, neither agree nor disagree, disagree,
strongly disagree. For six of the survey items, there was a significant negative relationship
between strength of agreement on the end-of-term survey and the grade-determining
course score. Each of the six items expressed a common mistake argued against in the
course. Students who persisted in making these mistakes thus tended to perform less well.
One of the items was:
In order to be scientific, a statement must be capable of direct experimental test.

162 You can find a summary of survey results for 1986 -2015 in the Getting Started in PHI 340 folder on the

course web site home page. There are many interesting patterns in the responses.
178

Separation by Reference

179

Separation by Attitude
Separation by Attitude
If you watch movies online, you can usually find one of those old SciFi B-movies from the
50's or 60's somewhere. They are a marvellous repository of stereotypes. Stereotypes are
often caricatures, but some caricatures carry a bit of truth - or so some people think. Science
and scientists are caricatured in those old movies, and we may be able to find something
useful for our present task if we see what they have to say about science.
One of the stock characters is The Mad Scientist. Typically a white, middle-aged, balding male
whose costume is a long white lab coat, The Mad Scientist has no interest in the things that
give us humanity, is indifferent to natural beauty and cares (if he's even capable of such
emotion) only about the acquisition of knowledge for the sake of power. He is conducting a
very dangerous experiment that threatens to End the World - and with it, his stereotypically
beautiful, young female lab assistant (whose own lab coat is noticeably shorter than his). She
tries to stop his nefarious schemes, and is rewarded for her efforts by being tied to a large
piece of lab equipment by The Mad Scientist (his only sign of possible interest in her). Not to
worry, though. In comes the tall, dark, stereotypically handsome (and thus square-jawed)
young male scientist or reporter, sees to it that the Mad Scientist is trapped by his own evil
devices, and rescues the young female (damsel in distress) at the Last Possible Moment (a
fairy tale for modern times).
The image of the cold, calculating scientist is an influential and prevalent one. It is, of course,
a gross distortion of reality. But it's often thought to contain a grain of truth at least about
Science if not about scientists. Science is interested in Nature, but its attitude towards Nature
is a 'purely factual' one. In this sense, science is cold - indifferent to the values that color the
world (or so many have said).
The picture we find in religion, stereotypical and actual, is quite different. The literature of
religion is full of eloquent expressions of awe, wonder and appreciation of Nature,
considered as a product of divine creation. A religious believer coldly indifferent to
underlying harmony, beauty and order in Nature would be a very peculiar person, and we
might well be skeptical about whether or not such a person was capable of truly religious
sentiment.
Canticle of the Creatures
Praised be you, my Lord, with all your creatures,
especially Sir Brother Sun,
who is the day
and through whom you give us light.
And he is beautiful and radiant
with great splendor;
and bears a likeness of you, Most High.
Praised be you, my Lord,
through Sister Moon and the stars,
in heaven you formed them clear
and precious and beautiful.
Praised be you, my Lord,
through Brother Wind,
and through the air, cloudy and serene,

and every kind of weather
through whom you give sustenance
to your creatures.
Praised be you, my Lord, through Sister Water,
who is very useful and humble
and precious and chaste.
Praised be you, my Lord, through Brother Fire,
through whom you light the night,
and he is beautiful and playful
and robust and strong
Praised be You, my Lord, through our Sister Mother Earth,
who sustains and governs us,
and who produces various fruit with colored flowers and herbs.
in Francis of Assisi (1182-1226): Early Documents (1999) 113-114.
For from the greatness and beauty of created things comes a corresponding perception of their
Creator.
Wisdom of Solomon 13:5
Whenever, in the course of the daily hunt the red hunter comes upon a scene that is strikingly
beautiful or sublime - a black thundercloud with the rainbow's glowing arch about the mountains,
a white waterfall in the heart of a green gorge; a vast prairie tinged with the blood-red of sunset
- he pauses for an instant in the attitude of worship. He sees no need for setting apart one day in
seven as a holy day, since to him all days are God's.
Charles Eastman (Ohiyesa), The Soul of the Indian (Houghton Mifflin, 1911).

"What a difference a difference in attitude makes." So, let's try:

181

Separation by Attitude
Separation by Attitude
Religion does, but Science does not, express the awe-and-wonder attitude
towards Nature.
According to this proposal, religion and science do not differ so much in their subject - in
what they talk about - than in how they talk about a common subject, Nature. Before we see
whether or not this proposal is true, we need to get clear on what it means. We need to find
out which attitude is supposed to be distinctive of religion but missing from science. But even
before we do that, we have to guard against a misunderstanding of Separation by Attitude.
To find out whether or not Separation by Attitude is true, we do need to look at what some
scientists have said about their own attitudes towards Nature. But we have to be careful not
to confuse their attitudes towards science with the attitudes, if any, expressed by science
itself. Our question is not whether, as a matter of fact, individual scientists differ in their
attitudes from individual believers of one religion or another; clearly, they may differ. What
we are concerned about is whether there are particular attitudes built into the very natures
of religion or science, attitudes that are essential to what they are, and to how they differ.
What individual scientists have to say gives us some evidence relevant to answering this
question, but it is science and not them on which we have to remain focused.
Separation by Attitude is also meant to be indifferent to the reasons for which an attitude is
held. The reasons may turn out to be important, but, for the sake of clarity, I am going to split
our job into simpler parts. So, if there is a religious attitude naturally associated with the
Argument from Design and purpose-directed explanation, we will, for now, pretty much
ignore the argument and attend to the attitude. (See “Separation by Explanation.”)
So is the proposal true? One problem with it is that it is not clear that there is a single attitude,
or kind of attitude, that is essential to all religions. Religious writers have been among the
most eloquent and passionate in their expressions of admiration for what they take to be the
creator(s)' handiwork, but given the enormous diversity of religions on Earth (I know, I
promised not to harp on this, but it's important), there may be no one emotional thread
running through them all. If some sort of admiration is what is essential, then there is a
second problem with the proposed separation principle.
On the Importance of Beauty in Assessing Scientific Theories

It is clear from the writings of scientists that aesthetic virtues are very important in science
in judging the relative merits of scientific theories. This often comes as a great surprise to
non-scientists, but it is a fact nonetheless. Generally, the greater the scientist, the more highly
developed her aesthetic sensibility. Often, when scientists write about preferring the simpler
theory, about simplicity as a virtue of theories, what they have in mind may better be
expressed by saying that the most beautiful (or, elegant) theory is preferable. It is not
possible to give you a deep sense of these virtues -beauty, elegance, harmony, symmetry, etc.
- and for their importance in doing science, without going through some scientific theories
in much greater detail than I have space for. Usually, it takes a lot of advanced training in a
branch of science before you're in a position even to recognize the relevant characteristics,
and they're often expressed in complex mathematical forms. I wouldn't even try to
accomplish all that here. So I will do what is a distant second best, and rely on the testimony
of some great physicists.
182

Separation by Attitude
Nature has a simplicity and therefore a great beauty.
--Richard Feynman (developed quantum electrodynamics, Nobel Laureate)
All great scientists are inspired by the subtlety and beauty of the natural world that they
are seeking to understand. Each new subatomic particle, every unexpected astronomical
object, produces delight and wonderment. In constructing their theories, physicists are
frequently guided by ... concepts of elegance in the belief that the universe is intrinsically
beautiful. Time and again this artistic taste has proved to be a fruitful guiding principle
and led directly to new discoveries, even when it at first sight appears to contradict the
observational facts.
--Paul Davies ($1,000,000 Templeton Prize Winner)
It is more important to have beauty in one's equations than to have them fit experiment.
--Paul Dirac (developed relativistic quantum mechanics, predicted anti-matter,
Nobel Laureate)
Physics is a form of insight and as such it's a form of art.
--David Bohm (developed deterministic interpretation of quantum mechanics)
All of these endeavours are based on the belief that existence should have a completely
harmonious structure. Today we have less ground than ever before for allowing ourselves
to be forced away from this wonderful belief. Equations of such complexity as are the
equations of the gravitational field can be found only through the discovery of a logically
simple mathematical condition.
--Albert Einstein (who speaks with some authority because he discovered those
equations)
The beauty in the laws of physics is the fantastic simplicity that they have ... What is the
ultimate mathematical machinery behind it all? That's surely the most beautiful of all.
--John Wheeler (Feynman's dissertation director)
My work has always tried to unite the true with the beautiful and when I had to choose
one or the other I usually chose the beautiful.
--Hermann Weyl (mathematical physicist and colleague of Einstein’s)

Weyl did not intend to be taken literally, but he did mean to emphasize the importance of
beauty in science. He expressed a view common among scientists: that beauty may help
guide theory choice because Nature is profoundly beautiful, and a theory that matches
Nature in this way is better than a mismatch. We could find many more such remarks by
scientists in every branch of science. There is no reason to doubt either the sincerity or the
accuracy of their statements. Although one could write a long book on the 'equation' of
beauty and simplicity, it is, I think, clear enough that awe and wonder may be as much a part
of science as they are of religion. Certainly, religion has no corner on that market, just as
science has no corner on the market of cognitive standards.

183

Separation by Personal Improvement
Separation by Personal Improvement
What may underlie the attraction that many feel for Separation by Standards and Separation
by Attitude is a perception that religion is personal in a way that Science is not. Science, some
say, has as its goal the systematic acquisition of knowledge about nature, based on careful
observation and experiment. The discussion of other separation principles reminds us that
religion, too, seeks knowledge about nature, based on careful observation and experiment.
[As I reported in discussion of Leibniz and Scientific Creationism, some deeply religious
scientists see their systematic search for such knowledge as an investigation of a divine plan;
as we'll find when we get to Separation by Explanation and Arguments from Design, the
knowledge gained from the search can be put to good theological use.] But when religion
seeks knowledge it is also concerned, for example, with securing the individual believer's
proper personal relationship to God (or gods), and these seem to be very different sorts of
goals. Because, presumably, a religion will seek to make this relationship better than it would
otherwise be, let's consider:
Separation by Personal Improvement
Religion aims for personal improvement, but Science does not; Science seeks
knowledge for its own sake.
Although this proposal seems quite promising, I think that it is wrong.
One kind of improvement is the acquisition of knowledge, because having knowledge has, in
addition to secondary effects, intrinsic value: it is valuable for its own sake. It is a kind of
improvement that is, one might say, the heart and soul of most religions because growing
spiritually is a kind of learning. Of course, religions aim for lots of kinds of improvement, but
a lot of what they aim for fits neatly under the heading of "knowledge," though they differ in
the methods they recommend for achieving such improvement, and in their advice about
exactly what knowledge to acquire. If, as this suggests, learning is a kind of improvement,
then to seek knowledge for its own sake is to seek a kind of improvement, and religion and
science don't differ in this way.
What about the proposal's emphasis on the personal? This emphasis is a defect because goals
in religion and science are often communal goals. To see what this means, we need to talk
about what kinds of things religion and science are.
I've already emphasized the difference between a theory and people who advocate or apply
it (or, in parapsychology's case, the difference between this branch of inquiry as
characterized by certain questions and individual parapsychologists’ characters). The
difference is obvious and important, but I don't want to overemphasize it. There is a great
deal more to science than the theories it deals in. There are also the collective practices that
constitute those dealings. These include devising and evaluating theories, extending the
frontiers of ignorance by finding new phenomena to explain, and communicating the results
of all this activity, none of which is typically done by lone individuals.
"Theology" means the systematic study of religious belief, but the term is also used to
indicate a particular body of religious beliefs, e.g., Catholic theology or Japanese Buddhist
theology, that may be the object of study. In the latter sense of the term, a theology is a theory
about whatever the religion in question is concerned with, often, the origin and nature of the
184

Separation by Personal Improvement
universe, including the nature of morality. But just as there is more to science than its
theories, there is more to religion than its theologies. There is a complex relationship
between theology and the normal practice of the religion, as evidenced, for example, in
prescribed religious rituals.
A 1996 US court decision (US v. Meyers, 95 F. 3d 1475 - Court of Appeals, 10th Circuit 1996)
gives a helpful list of ten "external signs" that are supposedly symptomatic of, if not essential
to, religion (see the Appendix to "School Board Problems" for more information).
Paraphrasing the decision, the signs are: being wholly founded or significantly influenced by
a deity, teacher, seer, or prophet who is considered to be divine, enlightened, gifted, or
blessed; embracing seminal, elemental, fundamental, or sacred writings; designating
particular structures or places as sacred, holy, or significant (where these sites often serve
as gathering places for believers, including physical structures and natural places); having
clergy, ministers, priests, reverends, monks, shamans, teachers, or sages (by virtue of their
enlightenment, experience, education, or training, these people are keepers and purveyors
of religious knowledge); including some form of ceremony, ritual, liturgy, sacrament, or
protocol (where these acts, statements, and movements are prescribed by the religion and
are imbued with transcendent significance); having a congregation or group of believers who
are led, supervised, or counselled by a hierarchy of teachers, clergy, sages, priests, etc.;
celebrating, observing, or marking "holy," sacred, or important days, weeks, or months;
prescribing or prohibiting the eating of certain foods and the drinking of certain liquids on
particular days or during particular times; prescribing the manner in which believers should
maintain their physical appearance or clothing; thinking that they have something
worthwhile or essential to offer non-believers, and so attempting to propagate their views
and persuade others of their correctness - sometimes called "mission work," "witnessing,"
"converting," or proselytizing.
With the possible exceptions of regulation of diet and dress (which are not necessary
features of religion, either), there are striking similarities with science: embracing seminal
or fundamental, writings.; designating particular structures (labs) or natural places (the
field) as significant; having (tenured) teachers who, by virtue of their experience, education,
or training, keep and purvey knowledge; including some form of ceremony, ritual or protocol
(PhD orals, grant-seeking, peer evaluation); having a group which is led, supervised, or
counselled by a hierarchy of teachers (as in universities); observing or marking important
days, weeks, or months (the Annual Professional Convention, the BRAIN initiative, the
International Geophysical Year, etc.); thinking that they have something worthwhile or
essential to offer non-scientists, and so attempting to propagate their views and persuade
others of their correctness (in teaching and through popularization and lobbying). Even diet
and dress can be remarkably uniform within a scientific specialty, especially in large
gatherings (e.g., professional meetings) - so much so that even if there are no explicit
regulations, discipline-specific social forces do seem to be at work. How all these activities
should be carried out is closely related to, but not determined by, the particular theories
current in science or a branch of science.
There appear to be deep, complex similarities between scientific theory and (normal)
scientific practice, on the one hand, and theology and (prescribed) religious behavior, on the

185

Separation by Personal Improvement
other. When a particular theology incorporates a specific scientific theory, it will be the
"deepest similarity," namely, identity.
Because of these shared complexities, it can be very difficult to figure out exactly what a
theory (or theology) and its associated norms of evaluation and application are. Is Professor
Smythe really applying special relativity? misapplying it? applying another theory? Is
Reverend Browne really a Southern Baptist? a member of distinctive subgroup? a heretic?
You need to find representatives whose activities, thoughts and behavior accord with the
relevant norms since the norms are rarely spelled out. Even when an attempt at authoritative
codification is made, you've got to be able to find the authority and to learn how to read their
code, and since you're trying to discover what the norms and theories are, this can be pretty
tricky. Your task is to find a good theory about what the theory is, so you face all the usual
difficulties in finding explanations for complex phenomena, where part of the task is to
distinguish more from less representative examples. As usual, if you're not careful, you'll end
up generalizing from the acts of unrepresentative individuals, and biased stereotypes can
result. (In the US, public debate about religion has been skewed by taking a few vocal
individuals as representative of a larger group - taking conservative, Orthodox Christians as
paradigmatic Christians or, more broadly, religious believers, for example.)
Acknowledging these complexities makes the study of religion and the study of science
("Religious Studies" and "Science Studies") difficult, but not impossible. Such studies would
miss their targets if they focused on one of theory or practice to the virtual exclusion of the
other.
Talk of "community" in religion and in science can thus describe these important
complexities characteristic of the kinds of things they are.
Is it even possible for there to be a religion that's thoroughly "anti-community," perhaps even
a religion of one (person) forbidden to seek interpersonal relations of any sort (-Ultimate
Hermitism?). Even if there could be a "religion of one," it's clear that for some religions, the
basic unit is a group (e.g., the congregation, the adherents to the faith) and the religious
group's well-being and improvement is paramount. The ideal may also be for members to
benefit from their relationship to the group, but some religious ideals require self-sacrifice
in practice. And if one person suffers or dies so that the others may live or live better, she
might, according to her religion's tenets, achieve excellence or salvation or eternal reward.
But everyone's benefiting is consistent with the purpose of the martyr's actions being the
improvement of the group's status. A necessary consequence of the community's
improvement might be the improvement of the individual, without the latter's being the
purpose of the improvement.
Science seems about as personal as religion is in this way. Learning (including learning
what's unknown) is the improvement that the scientific communities seek.
Individual scientists may benefit or may make great sacrifices in the process, but the purpose
is to increase the community's knowledge. A manic-depressive psychiatrist who tests a
potentially toxic, new-found cure on herself risks sacrifice for the sake of knowledge and
personal improvement. Acting in accord with a scientist's professional obligations, she seeks
to improve the state of knowledge.
Now that we have reminded ourselves that religion isn't cognitively empty, that choosing
186

Separation by Personal Improvement
what to accept on faith must not be arbitrary, that deities are not necessarily off-limits in
science, that science isn't blind to beauty, that religion has communal goals and that gaining
scientific knowledge is a kind of personal improvement, we can turn to more plausible
separation principles.

187

Separation by Fact and Value
Separation by Fact and Value
We now come to our first example of a potentially insightful Separation Principle. (It seems
to be endorsed by no less an intellect than Einstein, in his "Science and Religion.")
Separation by Fact and Value
Religion does, but Science does not, try to say what morally ought to be (values);
Science tries to say only how things actually are (the facts).
Notice first that this proposal does not claim that religion alone tells us about values; it allows
for the existence of other sources. It does say that science is different from religion in this
respect.
Second, we have to understand the word "ought" (or "value") correctly here: there are
several different uses of it, only one of which is relevant.
One kind of "ought" is an "ought" of practical advice, a prudential ought: "if you want to rob
a bank, then you ought to use Uzi's," "if you want the house to be well-insulated, then use 2
x 6's, and not 2 x 4's." We mean simply to give a bit of practical advice about the best means
for achieving a certain goal, and intend no evaluation of the goal itself.
Another kind of “ought” is an etiquette “ought”: "You ought to place the fork on the left of the
dinner plate," "You ought not belch loudly after meals." This kind of "ought" tells you how to
conform to local customs. (I'm told that in some locales, belching loudly after meals is taken
as a compliment to the host. I know some people who should consider moving.) Maybe it is
a kind of prudential "ought."
There is also a thought "ought" - an "ought" of 'right thinking': "you ought to rid your
(arithmetic) beliefs of inconsistencies", "if you have no independent evidence for a belief,
then you ought not hold it."
These three contrast with the moral "ought": "You ought not torture innocent infants to
death just for fun." "You ought to do unto others as you would have others do unto you
(unless you're a sadomasochist)." "One ought to treat each person with respect and dignity."
"You ought to keep your promises, but if you break a promise, then you ought to apologize."
To evaluate Separation by Fact and Value fairly and thoroughly, we need first to get rid of
some common, very persistent confusions about the nature of knowledge and morality,
confusions that lead some to deny knowledge of objective moral truths and to adopt an
utterly absurd, incoherent, self-contradictory view, Naïve Moral Relativism. In the next four
sections, I will (i) offer some common sense reminders about the fallibility of knowledge; (ii)
offer strong evidence that Naïve Moral Relativism needs to be refuted; (iii) explain why
believing contradictions is not a good idea; and will then (iv) present and decisively refute
Naïve Moral Relativism.
Reminders about Knowledge and Fallibility

You and Sam the Skeptic (SS) are standing in a classroom. You see a red chair in the front row
of the classroom.

188

Separation by Fact and Value

You are
• looking straight at the chair which is a few feet away,
• nothing obscures your view,
• the lighting is good,
• you are not distracted,
• nor do you have any eye defects (at least, none uncorrected by, say, wearing contacts
or glasses),
• and there are no magicians, experimental psychologists or other nefarious individuals around playing tricks.
You say about the chair, attentively looking straight at it under the aforementioned conditions, “That’s red.” And you’re correct: you know that’s red.
SS Objection 1: So, Know-it-all, you think you’re infallible. But you aren’t infallible. You’re
sometimes wrong about what you (think you) see. So, you don’t know that’s red.
Reply 1: I am fallible, and I realize that. But I am not always wrong, and I sometimes
see how things are – for example, right now I see that’s red.
SS Objection 2: You’re an arrogant fool. What do you know about photochemistry or the
neurophysiology of vision? You know next to nothing about how your eyes work or how your
central nervous system processes visual information. You have no theory of human vision
that explains how people see “how things are.” So, you don’t know that’s red.
Reply 2: I don’t know much about the theory of vision. I don’t even know if anyone has
a good theory of vision. But finding such a theory could not even begin if it did not
start with examples of seeing how things are (as well as examples of making visual
mistakes.) And I don’t have to know exactly how in general eyes work to know that’s
red.
SS Objection 3: Your aim’s way off. You say, “that’s red,” but you’re not really talking about
a chair. You’re really talking about your EXPERIENCE. Experiences are all in your MIND. So
even if you know that something – your experience - is red, you don’t know that (the chair)
is red.
Reply 3: I’m not always “on target,” but sometimes my aim’s good. I do sometimes
think about my experiences and thoughts, but I don’t generally confuse my experiences and thoughts with what they are about. That would be a symptom of a truly
bizarre mental disease or malfunction that I’m not suffering.

189

Separation by Fact and Value
In this case, I am having an experience – which by the way is not itself red: how could
part of my mind be red?! – and it’s an experience of that red chair. And I know that’s
red.
Three general points are illustrated by these exchanges with SS:
•
•

You can know S even if you’re not infallible about S or similar statements.
You can know S even if you have no theory about how it is that you know S and similar
statements.
• You can know S even if you must also have an experience of what S is about to know
S.
The same holds for moral truths: If S is a moral truth, then sometimes you can know that
S is true even if:
•
•
•

you sometimes make mistakes about moral issues;
you don’t have a complete theory of moral right and wrong that explains why S is true;
your opinion (perhaps widely shared) is that S is true, but S is not about your (or
anyone else’s) opinion.
Why Bother to Refute Naïve Moral Relativism?

Suppose that you’d read (say, in the Technician) about the results of a recent survey of over
1800 undergraduates, where the sample was statistically representative of all undergraduates at NC State, and part of the summary was the following:
Survey item
% Accept
I am > 9’ tall.
63%
I am < 1” tall.
55%
I am neither > 9’ tall nor < 1” tall.
60%
That would be pretty disturbing, wouldn’t it? But there’s more:
% Accept
> 9’
< 1” neither > 9’ nor < 1”
45% Accept Accept
-37% Accept
-Accept
33%
-Accept
Accept
26% Accept Accept
Accept
Those additional results would be very disturbing, wouldn’t they?
Why would they be disturbing? It’s worth being clear on that. The problem is that anyone
who had gained admission to a university should know that each of “I am > 9’ tall” and “I am
< 1” tall” is false, and that they are also mutually inconsistent. That does require some
knowledge about human heights and about numerical measures, but it’s pretty basic
knowledge that any high school senior ought to have. Even less basic knowledge is required
to know that
“I am neither > 9’ tall nor < 1” tall”
is inconsistent with each of:
“I am > 9’ tall”
190

Separation by Fact and Value
and
“I am < 1” tall.”
For the latter, one needs only a basic knowledge of English - mainly, how “neither … nor __”
works. (Of course, we could dispense with “neither … nor ___” and use two “not”’s with an
“and” instead: “I am not > 9’ tall and I am not < 1” tall.”)
If you found yourself at a school where about a quarter to two-thirds of your fellow students
were so badly confused as the above suggests, then, even if you were not involved in much
group work, you might want to consider a rapid transfer.
It turns out that you are at a similar school, except that the sample was of 1819 students
(from 2012-2015 in PHI 340) in which juniors and seniors from the Colleges of Engineering
and Science were over-represented and the survey items were not about human heights but
about moral issues.
Here are the relevant, disturbing survey results:
Survey item
% Accept
What’s morally right and wrong is wholly and completely determined by cultural
63%
opinions. [“Cultural”]
55%
What’s morally right and wrong is wholly and completely determined by individual opinions. [“Individual”]
60%
What’s morally right and wrong is neither wholly and completely determined by
cultural opinions nor wholly and completely determined by individual opinions.
[“Neither”]
These three items were part of a longer survey (about a dozen topics) for which students
received extra points if they completed it at the beginning and the end of the term. These
items all occurred in a row, one right after the other, within the survey. Extensive checks on
internal consistency and attentiveness showed that, except for these three items focused on
moral issues, inconsistent responses were very rare, and respondents were highly attentive.
In effect, Cultural is the absurd claim that no culture can ever make a mistaken moral judgment; and Individual is the absurd claim that no one can ever make a mistake in moral judgment. Anyone who knows that individuals sometimes disagree with cultures should not accept both Cultural and Individual. Neither denies both of these absurd claims of infallibility in
moral judgment and the statement Neither is therefore true. The very disturbing survey results were:
% Accept Cultural Individual Neither
45% Accept
Accept
-37% Accept
-Accept
33%
-Accept
Accept
26% Accept
Accept
Accept
The results are for the beginning of the term, but, alas, the end-of-term results were much
the same for these items – also a very disturbing outcome.
191

Separation by Fact and Value
(By the way, about 20% of the respondents reported having very strong religious beliefs that
led them to reject one or the other or both of Cultural and Individual and to accept Neither at
higher rates than the rest, but they didn’t do all that much better at maintaining consistency
in their beliefs about moral issues.)
So, that’s evidence that we should be as clear as possible about why no one should believe
contradictions and why the naïve kinds of “moral relativism” expressed by Cultural and Individual are false. Clearly, the result should be:
% Accept
Cultural
0%
Individual
0%
100%
Neither
And why were moral issues and no others affected by such massive confusion? Perhaps we
can find out. (Obviously, the answer can’t be, “Because morality is all just a matter of opinion”
- that would be to accept an absurdity like Cultural or Individual.) “Social signaling” probably
plays some role, but there are surely better ways to let others know that one regards tolerance as a virtue than by embracing absurdities and logical incoherence. After all, if there are
literally no limits at all to tolerance, then tolerance would not be a virtue.
What’s Wrong with Believing Contradictions?

Fred says,
“I believe that every time it rains, the ground gets wet. And I believe that it’s now
raining. But I believe that the ground’s not wet.”
Fred is speaking honestly, sincerely, reflectively and is a fully competent speaker of English.
Fred knows what he believes now. Yet it is logically impossible for all his beliefs to be true;
that is, Fred believes a logical contradiction. Fred has a very serious problem. Here’s why.
Let’s look at Fred’s beliefs more closely. Abbreviate Fred’s beliefs so that the contradiction is
vivid:
R = It’s now raining.
W = The ground’s wet now.
Fred said that he believes all three of these statements:
1. If R, then W.
2. R
3. Not-W.
If “If R then W” and “R” are true, then W would have to be true:
If R, then W.
R.
Therefore, W.

192

Separation by Fact and Value
It wouldn’t matter what “R” and “W” abbreviated. If two statements are structured as (1) and
(2), then whatever “W” abbreviates will follow. Fred’s thus committed himself to all four of:
1. If R, then W
2. R
3. not-W
and
W.
Hence, Fred is now committed to accepting
W and not-W.
Is there something wrong with that? There is something very seriously wrong with that.
(If you already think that believing a contradiction is a serious mistake, then you could stop
here, but you probably shouldn’t. If you need persuading, or are just curious, you should definitely read on.)
To see what’s wrong, notice that the following is a clearly correct way of reasoning:
P or Q
Not-Q
Therefore, P.
[For example, suppose that
P = You don’t have to work on Monday.
Q= You don’t have to work on Tuesday.
Your boss says, “You don’t have to work on Monday, or you don’t have to work on Tuesday.
Oh, but I see that we’ll be short-staffed on Tuesday, so you can’t take Tuesday off.” In effect,
your boss has just given you Monday off:
You don’t have to work on Monday, or don’t have to work on Tuesday.
Not-You don’t have to work on Tuesday.
Therefore, you don’t have to work on Monday.
This way of reasoning can be trusted because of the way that the word “or” works. If two
statements are joined with “or”, then the result will be another statement that is true if and
only if at least one of the joined statements is true:
P or Q is true if and only if at least one of P, Q is true.
That, after all, is what “or” means! Back to Fred.]
Fred believes at least one contradiction: W and not-W. Because Fred is committed to believing W, he is also committed to believing
W or T

193

Separation by Fact and Value
whatever T might be. Why is that? The reason: if Fred is committed to believing W, then he
has committed himself to believing whatever follows logically from W. And if W were true, then
W or T would have to be true, too. So, Fred is committed to believing: W or T.163
Thus, Fred’s committed to believing:
W
W or T
Not-W
But
W or T
Not-W
Therefore, T
as we saw in the example of not working on Monday. And T could be any statement at all.
Hence, if Fred accepts any one contradiction, then Fred has also committed himself to believing every statement. From a contradiction, everything logically follows.
That’s not good. There are lots of statements that Fred does not believe, would not believe,
and would instead heatedly reject; for example:
T = Fred should be boiled in oil now.
T = Fred owes everyone else in the universe $1,000,000,000 now.
T = Fred has long devoured cute things for breakfast.
T = Fred is now a poached egg.
T = A huge meteor will wipe out all life on Earth in one minute and Fred will be hit
and die in agony, but he will survive long enough to see his loved ones roast.
T = A huge meteor wiped out all life on Earth seventy years ago, including Fred.
T = 1+1 = 953,994,478,847.5114,408,746
T = Fred will spend eternity listening to static-filled recordings of elevator music.
and so on and on and on and on … . The phrase “on pain of contradiction” is apt.
This objection to believing contradictions does depend on the assumption that a person commits herself to believing whatever follows logically and obviously from what she believes.
But without that assumption, we’d never be able to hold anyone responsible for following
through on what they say. And without any possibility of responsibility, there could be no
trust. While no one is wholly and always trustworthy, never being able to trust anyone about
anything at any time would be awful.

163 Maybe Fred can’t believe statements he hasn’t thought of. That’s fine. We can help Fred out by suggesting
various candidates for T. See below. Besides, there are plenty of things that Fred has thought of, and that will
be enough to make the point below.

194

Separation by Fact and Value
No one should ever believe a contradiction. If you slip up in reasoning – maybe you don’t
notice what follows logically from your beliefs, even though the inference is an obvious one
– then as soon as you discover the mistake, you should give up at least one of the contradictory beliefs.
If you find yourself in a discussion with someone else who has contradictory beliefs and who
refuses to give up at least one of them, then you should heed the advice of the great, ancient
Greek philosopher and biologist Aristotle who suggested that trying to talk with someone
who believes contradictions is like talking with a vegetable.164 The mental lives of vegetables
are so limited that the conversation is bound to be unproductive. You’d be better off poaching
some eggs.
Summary:
1. W and non-W
2. Therefore W.
3. Therefore, W or T
4. Therefore T.
-where T can be any statement whatsoever.
Thus, to believe any one contradictory statement (e.g., 1., above) is to believe everything.
No one should do that. Believing contradictions should be avoided.
With those preliminaries taken care of, we can turn to objections to naïve versions of moral
relativism.
Naïve Moral Relativism Rejected

Naïve Moral Relativism (NMR) says that moral right and wrong, good and bad are relative
to individual or collective beliefs.
The “relative to” relationship is supposed by NMR to be a very strong one: for any action or
object that has a moral value, the relationship is: the belief(s) makes it the case that the action
or object has whatever moral value it has. Here are two versions of NMR:
Cultural NMR: For any culture and any action: if a culture believes that an action is
morally right then the action is morally right, and for any culture and any action: if a
culture believes that the action is not morally right then the action is not morally right.
Individual NMR: For any person and any action: If a person believes that an action is
morally right, then that action is morally right, and for any person and any action: if a
person believes that the action is not morally right, then the action is not morally
right.
Here are some obvious facts about beliefs (aka “opinions,” “views,” etc.):

164 Aristotle spoke Ancient Greek and the word he used [φῠτόν] is sometimes translated as “tree” instead of

“vegetable.” It doesn’t much matter for understanding his advice which of those is used in the translation. See
Aristotle, Metaphysics, Book Four, 1006a. “… such a person … is really no better than a vegetable.”
http://data.perseus.org/citations/urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0086.tlg025.perseus-eng1:4.1006a
195

Separation by Fact and Value
We can tell them apart and can thus count them.
They change over time.
Different minds sometimes have different beliefs, and sometimes have some of the
same beliefs.
Sometimes, we know what someone believes (and this includes ourselves).
The most straightforward way to reject the versions of NMR above is to endorse:
Moral Objectivism: There are moral facts (=objective moral truths) about which actions are morally right and about which actions are not morally right, and these moral
facts are not wholly determined by individual beliefs and these moral facts are not
wholly determined by cultural beliefs.
A lot of people talk as if they hold some version of NMR. Indeed, over half of the students
who’ve taken this course have said that they agreed with Cultural and Individual NMR! Even
worse, about a quarter of them said that they agreed with all three of Cultural NMR, Individual NMR and with Moral Objectivism, which is explicitly to accept a contradiction!
Why is NMR so popular? The main reasons for its popularity seem to be these two:
(i) NMR seems to account for the existence of moral disagreement;
(ii) NMR seems promotes the virtue of tolerance by taking into account the relevance of
complex psychological facts about the people involved in actions.
But there is no need to accept NMR to account for the existence of moral disagreement, and
NMR does nothing to promote tolerance and can require intolerance. There are some other
reasons that some claim to find NMR attractive, but they are even worse reasons than the
previous two:
(iii)

NMR is encouraged by a tendency to confuse, “I believe that P” - or, colloquially,
“P is true for me,” - and “P is true.”

(iv)

NMR is encouraged by a desire to make it easy to know what is morally right or
wrong, good or bad.

On (i): The fact that there is deep, long-standing disagreement about some moral issues is no
more reason to think that there isn’t an objective truth of the matter about moral issues than
the fact that there is deep, long-standing disagreement among religions is reason to think
that there is no truth of the matter in religious matters; and similarly for perceptual beliefs,
as already pointed out. Moral disagreement is readily accounted for without accepting NMR:
there are disagreements on matters of moral principle, and even when all parties agree on
the relevant moral principles, they may disagree about how they apply in a particular kind
of case. There may nonetheless be a moral fact of the matter about which principles are correct and about how to apply them.
On (ii): Suppose that according to the culture of Blabistan, everyone morally ought to be tolerant of the culture of Crabistok, which disagrees with Blabistan about many moral issues.
Suppose also that according to the culture of Crabistok no one morally ought to be tolerant
of any culture that disagrees with Crabistok. Then according to NMR, tolerance of Blabistan
by Crabistokians is immoral. NMR does not make tolerance into a universal moral virtue –
196

Separation by Fact and Value
quite the opposite. Obviously, a precisely similar argument can be given against Individual
NMR. Naïve Moral Relativism cannot promote tolerance; it undermines tolerance.
But this understates the problem for NMR. Again, there are two versions:
Cultural NMR: For any culture and any action: if a culture believes that an action is
morally right then the action is morally right, and for any culture and any action: if a
culture believes that the action is not morally right then the action is not morally right.
Individual NMR: For any person and any action: if a person believes that an action is
morally right, then that action is morally right, and for any person and any action: if a
person believes that the action is not morally right, then the action is not morally
right.
Given what they say, it follows from each that if anyone disagrees with either someone else
or a culture, then anyone who accepts either must believe contradictions. This is easy to see:
As already noted, disagreement about moral issues is common. So, suppose that the infamous serial killer John Wayne Gacy165 believes that it is morally right for him to rape and
murder boys. Suppose also that you disagree: you believe that it is not morally right for John
Wayne Gacy (or anyone else) to rape and murder boys. Then the following two statements
follow from Individual NMR:
M = It is morally right for John Wayne Gacy to rape and murder boys.
Not-M = It is not morally right for John Wayne Gacy to rape and murder boys.
Therefore, if Individual NMR is true, then the statement M and not-M is also true. Anyone
who believes Individual NMR is thus committed to believing contradictions, given the painfully obvious fact of moral disagreement.
A similar argument shows that Cultural NMR is similarly afflicted. You have probably heard
that some cultures endorse killing of women who have been raped – so called “honor killings” – and that some of the rape victims disagree with their cultures. Therefore, if Cultural
NMR is true, it is and it is not morally right for some raped women to be killed because they
were raped.
But that’s not all that’s wrong with NMR. Yet another defect is hidden in the two versions
presented: NMR’s devastating vagueness. Let’s suppose that it’s clear enough what a person
is (though the chapter “Personal Identity” will raise some questions about even that seemingly safe assumption). Cultures are somehow made up of persons. But how are cultures
“made up of” persons? Is there some minimum number of persons required (e.g., two)? What
kinds of relationships between the persons must there be for them to “make up” a culture?
Is there some minimum length of time that those relationships – whatever they might be –
must hold among the persons who “make up” the culture? Does a culture require a connected
location? A discernible origin in either time or space? Could you and one person from each
capitol of each of the world’s current nations “make up” a culture? How about persons from
each of the past ten centuries in, say, New Delhi or Caracas? Or all but the North Carolinians
from one dormitory building on campus? What would give such collections of persons any
165 https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Wayne_Gacy

197

Separation by Fact and Value
moral authority, according to Cultural NMR? Could the inmates of a maximum-security federal prison comprise a culture and determine that rape, murder, extortion and kidnapping
even when combined are morally right? Any form of relativism must be very clear and specific about what its basic elements are: to what the property of interest is supposed to be relative? NMR thus says nothing clear or specific about what moral properties are relative to. In
a sense, there is no view NMR. “It” – NMR – dissipates in a conceptual fog.
(iii) above was the claim that NMR is encouraged by a tendency to confuse, “I believe that P”
and “P is true.” There is of course nothing wrong with having a variety of ways of describing
what you believe. “I believe that everyone should eat more lard,” “I think that everyone
should eat more lard,” “Everyone’s eating more lard seems to me to be the way to go, dietwise,” “Everyone should eat more lard – that’s something that I believe,” and “That everyone
should eat more lard is true for me,” can all be used to express the very same notion. But the
last way of expressing the belief encourages an utterly bizarre confusion: “What’s true for
me is true.” Even many of those suffering from severe forms of mental illness or brain malfunction rarely think that they can make anything at all true just by believing it to be so; even
they regard someone else who is so deluded as insane. Perhaps some relatively sane people
are so desperate to have their beliefs treated respectfully, that they cry out, “What’s true for
me is true! – You must take it seriously!!” But here the lesson of Jamie Whyte’s “The Right to
Your Opinion” needs to be kept in mind: in any case where a belief matters and is not a wholly
isolated state of mind utterly without significant consequences, it needs to be supported by
reason and evidence. Almost every belief must earn respect. This fact is in no conflict with
treating people with respect – even those with whom one disagrees or who have demonstrably false beliefs; like the mentally ill, their truly desperate need may be for help.
On (iv), the claim that advocates of NMR want to make it easy to know what is morally right
or wrong, good or bad: desires for simplicity may be part of reality but reality cares little
what any one individual desires. There’s no implicit promise that in every instance gaining
knowledge about some matter will be possible, no less easy. While NMR might make it a bit
easier to discern supposed moral truths by opinion polls, NMR is so defective in so many
ways that any advantage it might afford in this one way is of little or no significance.
Besides, it is sometimes quite easy to see what’s morally right or morally wrong, just as it is
sometimes quite easy to see, hear, feel, etc. – that is, accurately to perceive what’s around you,
as previously pointed out. This allows us to give a clear counterexample to NMR (to pile on
top of all its other difficulties).
After all, one good reason for thinking that there are truths about what we morally ought to
do and about what we morally ought not to do, is that we know some of these truths. We tend
to forget this when we focus, quite understandably, on the unresolved cases. But we can all
think of many clear examples, where it's not just a matter of individual or collective opinion.
Therefore it is relatively easy, though unpleasant, to give a decisive counterexample to NMR,
keeping firmly in mind the analogies presented above between perceptual and moral
knowledge. To be effective, the counterexample must be clear, and to be clear, it must be in
the extreme end of the spectrum of moral wrongs. No normal person enjoys contemplating
such examples. Ridding oneself of NMR once and for all may take some courage.

Separation by Fact and Value

How We Can Fight Child Abuse

by Andrew Vachss, Esq.

… Predatory pedophiles are clever, calculating criminals. They stalk their victims with great care, working
themselves into positions of trust. They study children as carefully as any psychologist, and their camouflage
is our unwillingness to see the shark in our swimming pool.
The consequences of their depravity can be found in our psychiatric wards, our prisons and our
graveyards: The runaway who turns to child prostitution, the violent juvenile criminal, the teenage suicide
... all too many members of this army of victims can be traced to a predatory pedophile's original attack. …
Today's Victim Could Be Tomorrow's Predator
If we really want to fight crime, we must start at its root—the abused or neglected child.
by Andrew Vachss, Esq.
http://www.vachss.com/av_dispatches/disp_9006_a.html
Originally published in Parade Magazine, June 3, 1990
… I have known many children who did survive [the] torture [of child abuse]. Adam was one of them—…
handsome …, blond, polite and soft–spoken. Every Saturday, he would dress in an immaculate sailor suit
and go to a suburban mall. He would position himself carefully outside the entrance to the rest rooms and
patiently wait. Sooner or later, a young woman would approach, holding a little boy by the hand, consternation on her face. Adam knew what she was thinking: Her child was too old to take with her into the ladies'
room and too young to venture by himself into the men's room. Adam would approach, smiling gently. "Do
you want me to take him inside with me, ma'am?" he would ask. "I always take my baby brother." Relieved
and grateful, the young woman would entrust her child to this charming little boy. She would wait outside.
And wait. Finally, when too much time had passed, she would either ask a passing man to check on her son
or, on some occasions, rush inside herself. Her child would be found on the floor of the rest room, crying.
Slap marks on his face, his clothes in disarray. The only sign of Adam: the open window he'd used to escape.
I was Adam's lawyer. He readily acknowledged his crimes, displaying no consciousness of guilt
whatsoever. When I asked him why he did such things, he looked at me as if I was out of touch with reality.
Why should anyone feel sorry for his victims? Nobody had felt sorry for him.
Later, we learned that Adam had actually served an apprenticeship in sexual assault. His home had
been the classroom. The therapists told us that Adam was trying to master his own sense of powerlessness—to overcome oppression by imitating his oppressors.
Adam told me he liked doing what he did. It made him feel strong. Special. In control.
