166 More on Andrew Vachss: http://www.youtube.com/user/burkesdogpansy/videos ,The Zero:

http://www.vachss.com/index.html , http://www.facebook.com/andrewhvachss
199

Separation by Fact and Value
Here then is a very clear counterexample to NMR, involving a possibility that morally ought
never occur:
Predatory Pedophile Paradise is a causally isolated island society where for a very
long time human infants have been bred for the purpose of being tortured and
killed just for the fun of its adult residents. All the adults there are convinced that
torturing and killing innocent infants just for fun is morally right.
But of course
It is morally wrong to torture and kill innocent human infants just for fun.
That’s one of the objective moral truths that you know. You can think of many other examples. The Nazi extermination by torture of over six million human beings, including many
thousands of infants and children, was clearly morally wrong. You are not inclined to excuse
them by saying, "Although I don't go in for smashing infants’ skulls in front of their parents,
Hitler and those who ran his death camps were of the opinion that it was right - it fit so well
with their 'value system' - so it was what they ought to have done. OK for them, not-OK for me.
But we're all OK. Can’t we all just get along?!"
NMR is false.167
Thus, there are objective truths about morality, and we sometimes know them, even in some
cases when we have no clearly correct theory to explain them. This is not, of course, to say
that we get it right most of the time, that it is easy to get it right, or that we tend to answer
the important moral questions correctly. Nor is it to say that finding a correct theory of
morality – right and wrong, good and bad, etc. – isn’t an excellent goal, useful, or in more
complex, less obvious cases, indispensable.
One final comment on a kind of behavior common among those who endorse some form of
naïve moral relativism. If naive relativism about X is adopted, then it will no longer be possible to defend X by arguing that it is objectively true, or more likely to be true. After all, X is
just a matter of opinion and not-X is equally just a matter of opinion, according to naïve relativism about X. That leaves only dogmatic insistence – re-asserting the opinion, without any
supporting evidence or argument – as the only way to press for an opinion.168 And if X is
thought to be important, that insistence may well become vicious because fear will lead to
an increased sense of urgency to force acceptance of X. Isn’t just such vicious dogmatism
what one finds in, e.g., online exchanges – they can’t really be called “debates” – with extreme
relativists? Oddly, naïve relativists tend to be among the most intolerant.
An Obvious Problem for Separation by Fact and Value Easily Resolved

The two assumptions - there are truths about morality, and we sometimes know them -which
ought to be uncontroversial, create a problem for the separation principle. A statement is
167 There are non-naïve versions of moral relativism, but what makes them non-naïve is that they place strict
constraints on the kinds of relativity that are allowable. At least some of the non-naïve versions would more
aptly be called, “Complex Moral Objectivism.” See, e.g., http://www.3ammagazine.com/3am/the-pluralist-2/ ,
http://duke.academia.edu/DavidWong and https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/moral-relativism/ .
168 See: Samantha Wakil, “Objectivity and orgasm: the perils of imprecise definitions,” Synthese: 1-19
(10/1/2020 pub online) DOI 10.1007/s11229-020-02886-8.

200

Separation by Fact and Value
true if it accurately describes a fact. So, if there are (known) truths about morality, then there
are facts about morality; and if there are facts about morality, then they are parts of how
things actually are. A list of facts about the world might contain statements like these:
Sugar dissolves in water (under the right conditions).
NCSU's basketball team won the championship in 1983.
The Nazis ought not to have tortured millions of people to death.
Cobalt-60 is a radioactive isotope of cobalt.
etc. ...
But Separation by Fact and Value says that science attempts to discover facts, and now we
are reminded that among the facts are moral facts.
There's an obvious response to this obvious problem: what's clearly intended by advocates
of Separation by Fact and Value is a distinction between two kinds of facts, moral and nonmoral, where science is supposed to be concerned with non-moral facts alone, and religion
is supposed to be concerned with both kinds of facts. This is a good first response, but it has
to be treated carefully. We don't want to render Separation by Fact and Value empty by
simply stipulating
Science = the study of only non-moral facts
so we need some independent way to characterize the overall difference between moral and
non-moral. Let's look at two contrasting approaches to the analysis of the meaning of moral
terms. Then we'll be able to see the real weakness of Separation by Fact and Value.
Two Approaches to Analyzing Moral Terms169

It will be convenient if we focus on some one moral term. It's grammatically simpler to talk
about "good" than to talk about "ought", so that's what I'll do. The two approaches to
analyzing moral facts are Ethical Naturalism and Ethical Anti-Naturalism. Oddly, it's easiest
to present the "anti" view first.
Let's begin by distinguishing between two different sorts of characteristics. Suppose I ask
you to list some properties that a textbook has. You're likely to mention its shape, weight,
color, perhaps something about the materials in it (paper, ink, etc.). If you want to be very
thorough, you might do some chemical tests on the ink, or even check for radioactive
isotopes. In doing all this, you are using your five senses or are extending their reach with
scientific equipment, to discern what you can about the object. Call something a natural
property if it's a characteristic that you can detect by using your five senses, possibly
augmented by extra equipment.
According to Ethical Anti-Naturalism, goodness is a real, objective property of objects and
actions, but there is no scientific or observational method that can even so much as help us
to determine whether a particular thing or action has it. Goodness is not a natural property,
according to Ethical Anti-Naturalism. Nor is it analyzable as some cluster of natural
properties. According to this view, if two individuals disagree about whether or not a certain
thing or action is good, they may be unable to come to any agreement, but exactly one of

169 -based on the superb textbook, Fred Feldman, Introductory Ethics (Prentice Hall, 1978).

201

Separation by Fact and Value
them is right, though there is simply no way empirically to determine who it is. According to
Ethical Anti-Naturalism, goodness is indefinable, unanalyzable, and undetectable.
Ethical Naturalists find this view unacceptable because it makes moral knowledge
impossible in two ways.
We want children to acquire the concept of goodness. The psychology of learning and
concept acquisition is a big, complicated subject, but in many cases, we begin by giving a
child some examples. If you want your daughter to know what "red" means, you'll show her
lots of red things (and tell her they're red) and you'll show her lots of nonred things (and tell
her they're not red), and, with amazing speed, she'll pick up on the relevant difference (if you
choose your examples judiciously; obviously, if every red thing you show her is also round,
you're going to confuse her). Nothing like this can work if goodness is a nonnatural property,
since nothing your daughter can do with her senses (even if she drags in equipment from the
lab) will allow her to detect the goodness in the fifty thousand examples you show her. So, if
Ethical Anti-Naturalism were correct, no one could learn what goodness is. That’s the first
way in which it makes moral knowledge impossible.
[I've sometimes been offered the Revelation Defense of Ethical Anti-Naturalism: No one can
know any moral truths if they are not revealed, directly or indirectly, by God. Anyone who
makes this claim has at least two questions to answer. First, isn't it possible that an isolated
community, deprived of contact with the Bible or other scriptures, could develop some
appreciation of moral truths; e.g., mightn't they come to see that the wanton torturing of
infants is wrong? Second, even supposing that some fundamental moral principles are
revealed or instilled (in all human beings?) by God, we will need to be able to apply them to
individual cases. Won't that require an ability to perceive goodness in particular things?
Unless a special mental power of moral intuition is built into us by God, it seems that we must
be able to perceive goodness in things and acts. If there is such a power of moral intuition,
how does it work, why does it so often fail so miserably, and why is there so much
disagreement about moral matters?]
So, a second way in which Ethical Anti-Naturalism makes moral knowledge impossible is
this: if, by some miracle, you (or God) could get the concept of goodness into your mind,
there'd be no way for you to apply it to particular cases to tell whether it was present or
absent.
Ethical Naturalists claim instead that goodness can, after all, be tied down to what we can
perceive or feel. The Ethical Naturalists claim that goodness can be explained by the use of
terms that each express an ordinary, observable property. Different Ethical Naturalists have
different proposals about how exactly to define or analyze goodness, but they all agree that
it can be analyzed, and analyzed purely in terms of natural properties.
There are many good books on ethics, and I leave it to them to delve into the many Ethical
Naturalist analyses. For our limited purposes, it will be enough to examine just one
suggestion about how science might be a source for knowledge of moral truths.
Science, Normal Function and the Well-Being of Human Beings

Given two highly plausible premises, we can argue for the moral relevance of science.
(i) What is good for an individual, what constitutes her well-being, what she ought to do, and
202

Separation by Fact and Value
what she ought to be, all depend directly upon her nature. It is almost a commonplace that
what is good for human beings depends on human nature, that their well-being is
determined by what kind of being they are. For example, what kind of government and
society human beings ought to have depends upon what their natures are. Or, to take a more
obvious case, torturing people is not good because it harms them; and it harms them because
of the nature they have. We can put this same homey truth in other words: there are certain
conditions and states it is normal for a human being to be in, states and conditions conducive
to their well-being; some departures from normal function are not good, and ought to be
avoided if well-being is to be maintained. (Let's put aside questions about supernormal
function.)
(ii) It is possible to discover, by means and methods familiar in science, what is and what is
not normal for a human being. How an organism functions normally is something that
biologists are often quite successful in discovering. There seems to be no reason to believe
that medical doctors and psychologists have not or cannot discover a great deal about
normal human function, or human well-being. I must stress here that I am not talking simply
about average function. There is a difference between normal and average: even if a whole
population of organisms suffered from some sort of harmful disease, (e.g., an unusually bad
world-wide flu epidemic) we would say that, on the average, their function was then below
normal. It is normalcy in this optimum, well-being sense (in addition to statistical averages)
that science can discover.
Based on these two plausible assumptions, we can give an argument for the moral relevance
of science:
The Moral Relevance of Science
1. Science (medicine and psychology) can discover normal human function (wellbeing)
2. Discovering normal human function (well- being) is discovering some truths
about what is morally good (for human beings).
3. Therefore, Science can discover some moral truths
In the second premise, some form of Ethical Naturalism is assumed to be correct. It is a
substantive assumption. But it certainly seems to be true. It may even be difficult to imagine
how human well-being could otherwise be discovered. Nevertheless, I would mislead you if
I didn't tell you that this argument, and the Ethical Naturalism it is based on, are
controversial. So I won't pretend to have refuted Separation by Fact and Value decisively.
But I think that I have raised some serious questions about its adequacy.
[For Optional Final:] What of Einstein's proposal? (Do I think I'm smarter than he was?!) He
took the rather strong position that conflict between religion and science is impossible;
here's his reason:
... science can only ascertain what is, but not what should be, and outside of its
domain, value judgments of all kinds remain necessary. Religion, on the other
hand, deals only with evaluations of human thought and action: it cannot
justifiably [i.e., properly] speak of facts and relationships between facts. ... the

203

Separation by Fact and Value
well-known conflicts between religion and science in the past must all be ascribed
to a misapprehension ... (108)
He does, however, see the need for "strong reciprocal relationships and dependencies":
Though religion may be that which determines the goal, it has, nevertheless,
learned from science, in the broadest sense, what means will contribute to the
attainment of the goals it has set up. But science can be created only by those who
are thoroughly imbued with the aspiration towards truth and understanding. This
source of feeling, however, springs from the sphere of religion. To this there also
belongs the faith in the possibility that the regulations valid for the world of
existence are rational, that is, comprehensible to reason. I cannot conceive of a
genuine scientist without that profound faith. The situation may be expressed by
an image: science without religion is lame, religion without science is blind. (1089)
What is Einstein proposing here?: he is proposing a redefinition of religion and science, how
he thinks they should be seen, and not primarily how they are or have been seen (-as he
implies in his historical remarks, 109). He wants to redraw the map in a way the he finds
personally attractive.
His proposal is not without its odd consequences. On his view, Marxism, with its emphasis
on higher goals, would be at least part religion. He also proposes a redistribution of labor
and resources: to remove from religion much of its traditional explanatory role, and to give
that job to science alone.
So, at best, Einstein is offering a personal vision of science and religion, and proposals for
changes in, and reconciliation between, the two. (His vision is not unique to him, as he
notes.170) There is nevertheless reason to treat his proposal with some caution: there may
be more fact in function (goal and purpose) than he allows. And even if the job of explaining
is divided between the two, religions will still want to say a great deal about "facts and
relationships between facts", on any reasonable interpretation of "fact".
We need to keep looking for an adequate separation principle.
Next, everyone's favorite: religion is based on faith, but science is not.
Mathematical and Moral Knowledge: Can Ethical Anti-Naturalism Be Saved?

Ethical Anti-Naturalism was rejected because it makes knowledge of any moral properties
impossible. Even if some knowledge of moral properties is innate, it was argued, Ethical AntiNaturalism could not explain how particular instances of those properties could be detected.
But there are other properties that we clearly can know about and that seem to share a key
feature with moral properties as characterized by Ethical Anti-Naturalism. The other properties are mathematical properties, and they, too, are not necessarily qualities that can be
170 A much more sophisticated version of Einstein’s proposal is defended at length by Robert John
Ackermann, Religion as Critique (University of Massachusetts Press, 1985). Ackermann accepts the
consequence that Marxism is a religion and recognizes that he is not simply giving a previously accepted
definition of “religion.” https://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/catalog/NCSU1513240

204

Separation by Fact and Value
perceived.
This may sound somewhat surprising because we can, for example, count objects and perceive their shapes, and that seems to involve numbers (counting) and geometry (shapes).
Aren't we using our senses in counting and measuring objects that we perceive? Perhaps so,
but the fundamental features of numbers and geometrical objects are not necessarily qualities that are perceptual. Here are two of many possible arguments for the latter claim:
(i) Even if there were an upper bound, N, on how many actually perceived (=detected by the
senses) objects there actually are, there could have been more (M > N) objects, and the truth
that
There could have been M objects, where M > N = the actual number of objects.
which involves the number M and thus can't be about a property possessed by any actual
collection of perceived objects because by hypothesis, there wouldn't be enough of them.
(ii) Even if there were a perfectly spherical perceived object, there might not have been any
such object. Yet we could still speak intelligibly about it's being possible that there was one
in the absence of such an object. You might even say, wistfully, "I sure wish there were some
perfect spheres. I'd like to have one." So, the truth that
There could have been some perceived object that is perfectly spherical, but there is
no such object.
can't be about just the property that characterizes an actually perceived perfectly spherical
object because there is by hypothesis no such object.
Generally, and even more obviously, we can't prove mathematical truths concerning mathematical properties by doing experiments. Only someone who was very confused would suggest conducting an empirical investigation into whether it must be true that, e.g., all bachelors are unmarried! Similarly, only someone very confused would propose scientific experiments - i.e., an empirical investigation - to show that it must be true that 12+97=109. This
helps to emphasize that mathematical properties are not necessarily perceptual qualities.
Call the view that mathematical properties are not necessarily perceptual qualities, "Mathematical Anti-Naturalism."
We can also easily avoid two other mistakes about mathematical knowledge.
While some suggest (incorrectly, as argued in "Separation by Fact and Value") that moral
truths are "just a matter of (collective) opinion" and/or "no one can know what's really true,
morally speaking," the parallel claims about mathematical properties are utterly ridiculous.
As has been widely recognized for over 2500 years, our knowledge of mathematical truths
(about mathematical properties) is some of the best and most secure knowledge that anyone
could ever have. (Short of what's classically self-evident – see “Separation by Foundation” –
there is little knowledge more secure than basic mathematical knowledge.) Thus, what we
might call, “Naïve Mathematical Relativism” should be rejected out of hand.
A second kind of mistake about mathematical knowledge results from confusing the names
of numbers - i.e., numerals - with the numbers that are named. If someone were confused in
that way, it might lead them to say silly things like, "Well, 12+97 doesn't have to be 109
205

Separation by Fact and Value
because we could use binary code instead ("1101101"), or we could even call 109 'Griselda,'
and then 12+97=Griselda!" But that mistake is easily avoided. You, for example, are not the
same thing as your name, so if your name were “Samantha” then you = Samantha ≠ “Samantha.” More generally names are not the same as what they name, so even if the names that
we give the numbers themselves are arbitrary, none of the arbitrariness ‘infects’ the numbers themselves. We can't change the essential nature of the number designated (in base 10)
by "109" by giving that number a novel or unusual name.
If mathematical properties are knowable and not necessarily perceptual qualities, then how
do we know mathematical truths about mathematical properties? Would it help to suppose
that knowledge of mathematical properties is innate, in parallel with the Revelation Defense
of Ethical Anti-Naturalism's claims about moral properties? How would that help in the
mathematical case if it didn't help in the moral case?
Should we just postulate an innate capacity of Mathematical Intuition (to apply this supposedly innate mathematical knowledge), along with an innate capacity of Moral Intuition
(to apply the supposedly innate moral knowledge)?
No, we should not postulate such innate capacities unless there are more powerful arguments offered that compel us to do so. Doing so would have, as the philosopher Bertrand
Russell once remarked in another context, “all the advantages of theft over honest toil.” It
would be cheating. Furthermore, there is in this case a difference between mathematical
properties and moral properties: even if mathematical properties are not in any straightforward way sensory qualities, we can in some cases discern their presence or absence by using
our senses (perhaps extended by test equipment); but Ethical Anti-Naturalism says that
moral properties can never be discerned by using our senses. Because there is this difference,
more powerful arguments that take this difference into account are needed.
Ethical Anti-Naturalism remains an unacceptable view that makes moral knowledge impossible.
Costly Confusion #5: In moral matters, it’s all just a matter of collective opinion.

During Fall 2010 – Spring 2015, over 2400 students enrolled in PHI 340 responded to surveys asking for their opinions on issues discussed in the course.171 The same survey was
given at the beginning and at the end of each term. One of five responses was allowed for
each of the fifty survey items: strongly agree, agree, neither agree nor disagree, disagree,
strongly disagree. For six of the survey items, there was a significant negative relationship
between strength of agreement on the end-of-term survey and the grade-determining
course score. Each of the six items expressed a common mistake argued against in the
course. Students who persisted in making these mistakes thus tended to perform less well.
One of the items was:
For any culture and any action: if a culture believes that an action is morally right then the
action is morally right, and for any culture and any action: if a culture believes that the action
171 You can find a summary of survey results for 1986 -2015 in the Getting Started in PHI 340 folder on the

course web site home page. There are many interesting patterns in the responses.
206

Separation by Fact and Value
is not morally right then the action is not morally right.

207

Separation by Foundation
Separation by Foundation
We come now to the most popular separation principle of all.172
Separation by Foundation
Religion is, but Science is not, based on faith; Science is based on proof and
observation.
Many religious writers have said that religion requires a 'leap of faith', that religious belief is
religious precisely because it is held in the absence of proof or supporting observations; in
fact, it may be held so strongly as to persist despite a great deal of contrary evidence. We
might even say, echoing some of these writers, that a faith untested by reason is a faith not
worth having.
In our examination of this most popular proposal, we must check on three things: first, we
have to be clear on what it is to have a belief on faith; then, second, we need to ask why faith
is thought to be essential to religion; and third, we need to make sure that the principle really
marks a difference between religion and science. We have already found reason to think that
the proposal is overstated. In our discussion of Scientific Creationism, we were reminded
that inconclusive justification is the best that is even hoped for in science - so it does not
seem to require proof. Also, given how indirect the connection between theory and
observation is, given how remote observational evidence may be from many theoretical
claims it helps to justify, there may be more room for observation in religion, and more
similarity between it and science, than a distorted picture of science would allow. Let's drop
the second part of Separation by Foundation and focus on faith.
The Evidentialist Objection to Religious Belief

It's useful to consider an old, familiar objection to belief in the existence of God. For the sake
of simplicity, I will now narrow the focus to the God of Western theology. Let's begin with an
analogy.
Consider the peculiar case of Mr. Maxwell Jones, multi-millionaire. Mr. Jones has become
fantastically wealthy by playing the stock market. Beginning with an investment of $25, he
has amassed a multi-million dollar fortune. How did he do it? What is the secret of his
success? He offers to share his secret with you. "It's all in this book" he says, the Book of
Fluctuations. The Book, he tells you, was a gift from beings from another planet, a race of
supereconomists who amuse themselves by observing the economies of other worlds and
finding the fundamental principles that govern them. They themselves are too rich to care
about making more, and it pleases them to give whatever they find out to a Select Few on
each planet they study. They came to Max one day in a vision on Cablevision, and soon after
the Book arrived in the mail, post-paid. Ever since, he's been on easy street. You are pretty
skeptical - after all, all you've got is Max's word on this - but he is very rich, so you decide to
take a look at the Book. What you find is deeply disappointing: a bunch of vague financial
platitudes, open to a variety of interpretations.
Instead of something really useful, like "Buy IBM at 104 on October 15, 2023; sell at 108 on
172 -and the most difficult material in this book, largely because the concept of classically self-evident belief is

difficult to apply.

208

Separation by Foundation
October 23, 2023.", you find instead such statements as "Buy low, sell high", "Avoid
excessively large risks" and "High tech stocks are risky during the end of the 21st century."
All good advice, but not the detailed stuff of real get-rich-quick schemes. Max, you say,
money's not the only thing you're full of.
As you first listened to Max, you had clearly in mind what appears to be an eminently sensible
principle for the conduct of your cognitive affairs:
Principle of Minimum Evidence: If you have no evidence for a statement, S, then
you should refrain from believing S.
But you soon brought in another principle which seems equally worthy of assent:
Principle of Evidence for Extraordinary Claims: If a statement, S, conflicts with
previously well-justified beliefs, then you should not believe S unless there is
overwhelming evidence for statement S.
Both of these principles seem eminently sensible. And they direct you not to believe Max's
story. It's more reasonable to suppose that he's a rich nut.
Mustn't the same principles apply to the claims of religious believers? Surely, one might say,
it is the height of irrationality to accept claims for which one has no evidence. And if the
claims are both vague and extraordinary, as some of the Bible's claims are, isn't it your
epistemic duty to demand a great deal of evidence before you believe what it offers? There
is, I think, a case to be made for the extraordinariness of the belief that God exists. We are
used to this belief, the overwhelming majority of us accept it, and even the atheists and
agnostics among us are quite familiar with the belief. Suppose, however, that a visiting
Martian inquires173 about your religious beliefs if any, and you reply, "I believe that there's
one and only one omniscient, omnipotent, omnibenevolent, necessarily existent, incorporeal
creator of the universe." The curious Martian would of course want to know why you hold
this belief and is sure to ask you what evidence you have for it. This helps to motivate the
following argument:
The Evidentialist Objection to Belief in God
1. It is irrational to believe (extraordinary) S if one lacks (overwhelming) evidence
for S
2. There is not (overwhelming) evidence for believing that God exists.
3. Therefore, it is irrational to believe that God exists
This is an agnostic's favorite argument; atheists find it too weak. Coupled with the principle
that one ought not believe what is irrational to believe, this argument would lead to the
conclusion that one ought not believe that God exists.
There are two premises here, and both have been attacked. I am going to wait until
“Separation by Explanation” to attack the second premise, though attack it I will. Here, the
flaws in the first premise are relevant.
Is it irrational to believe where one lacks evidence? It is often irrational to do so. The story of
Maxwell Jones reminds us of this. But there are homier examples, too. If I get it into my head
173 See A Useful Method: Taking a Martian Perspective, in the “Introduction.”

209

Separation by Foundation
to believe that wearing white socks leads to success, without having any evidence at all, then,
surely, I am being irrational. But is this always the way it is?
Let's consider a third example. I meet with one of the students about an exam score, and I
come to believe that he is angry. "Why do you think he's angry?" you ask, in a skeptical and
challenging mood What's your evidence? I respond by reporting the usual sorts of evidence:
he shouted obscenities at me and made unkind remarks about my parentage, he waved a
baseball bat in my direction, he hopped up and down, etc. My report is, of course, usually
enough to settle the matter; usually, no further evidence is demanded. But, pressing me
further, you ask about my claim that he shouted at me. I respond that I heard him shouting,
and he mentioned my name, and there was no one else around, and I remember all this
clearly and vividly. "What makes you think that you heard him shouting?" you ask. I respond
that it seemed then to me that I heard a voice emanating from the neighborhood of his mouth,
and have a clear, present memory impression of that audio-visual perception. "What makes
you think that that is how it now seems to you?" ask you. At this point, I get angry, hop up and
down etc.. You have outrun my patience. "I don't have any further evidence," I say. "What's
more, I don't need any: this is how things now seem to me to be; and for my belief about how
things now seem to me to be, I need no justification. Beliefs like these are self-evident, and
are not the sort of thing about which I could be mistaken, so I don't need evidence for them.
Your last request is not reasonable."
This story brings out two important points:
First, justification must come to an end somewhere. This is not just because we get tired, or
our patience runs out. It is because there must be beliefs that need no independent
justification, if any justification is to be possible. Such beliefs we can call foundational,
because they lie at the foundation of all our knowledge and rational belief. It is on such a
basis that all of our other beliefs rest: all the beliefs, that is, that are justified or known for us
at any one time, all beliefs that we are rational in holding on to. It is because the 'higher'
beliefs are supported in the appropriate ways by the lower ones that we can be justified in
believing anything at all. This view is, naturally enough, called "Foundationalism," and has a
long history, dating back about 2500 years. It is difficult to resist.
The second point of our story is this: whatever else the foundation includes, it will surely
include beliefs about how things then seem to the believer. In order to get clearer on what
this means, I want to say a bit about the most famous argument in philosophy. It was given a
few hundred years ago by Descartes. He himself did not buy the argument's conclusion, but
he thought that it was useful for getting at some truths about Foundationalism.
First, however, I need to run a sanity check on you. I'd like you to bring to mind someone you
really care about, whose well-being really matters to you. Now, what do you think of the
following scenario: you arrive home one day to find your loved one (I'll write "she") deep in
anguished thought. She says that she's now convinced that it's all just a dream. "Sure, it looks
like I'm here on the couch, talking with you, but I'm really back in my room, fast asleep, in
bed, dreaming it all up. And it's been that way for quite a while. You're just a figment of my
imagination." Wouldn't you take that as an emergency, and try to get help for your loved one?
After all, such a belief is obviously crazy. Anyone who takes it seriously does not deserve
points for philosophical insight; they need, perhaps, a long rest in a quiet place. Consider a
210

Separation by Foundation
slightly different scenario. This time, your loved says she thinks it all might well be a dream
- "maybe, maybe not - I'm no longer sure." Even this much of a loss of one's grip on the
difference between dreams and reality is a sign that your loved one needs help. This, too, is
a sign of serious mental malfunction. I hope all that seems right to you.
Of all the sources of information about the world - testimony of others, memory, present use
of the senses - the last is clearly the most trustworthy. Among our least trustworthy sources
of experience are dreams. If we think that an experience is just dreamed up, we know not to
trust it as a guide to how the world is then. But, Descartes asks, how can you now be sure
that your present experience isn't just dreamed up? And if you can't be sure, then there is no
reason to trust it as a guide to how the world is now. Because present sensory experience is
rendered suspect in this way, and it is the most trustworthy source of information, it seems
to follow that we can never know anything. Obviously, it would be cheating to rely on less
reliable sources - memory and testimony - to shore up the most reliable source, so relying
on those sources is 'against the rules'.
Descartes' Dream Argument (Quick Version)
1. If I don't know with certainty that I am not dreaming now, then I don't know
anything
2. I don't know with certainty that I am not dreaming now
3. Therefore, I don't know anything
As Descartes observed, the first premise is overstated and is thus false. There are things
about which you can be certain even if you are crazy enough to be convinced that your whole
life's a dream. Even if you think that you’re just dreaming up this whole book - what a
nightmare!- and so don't know that there really is a book that you are now seeing, you can
still be absolutely certain that it now seems to you that you are seeing a book. In general,
certain statements about how things seem to you to be now will be self-evident to you. Let's
define
The Definition of Classically Self Evident Statement [CSE] (always indexed to
particular person and time)
A statement S is classically self-evident for me now =def. necessarily: if I think that
S is true, then S is true.
Statements that are in this way self-evident you can make true simply by thinking that they
are true, and so you could not be wrong about them.174 About the current, conscious contents
at the surface of your own mind, at least, you are infallible - as long as you confine yourself
to mere appearance, and don't stick your neck out on matters of mental causation.
This definition is so strict that even your own body is beyond classical self-evidence. Consider,
for example, your belief (indeed, your knowledge) that you have a nose.

174 For technical reasons, there is an additional condition that is needed in the definition of “classically selfevident belief.” The statements, S, should be limited to contingent – that is, not necessarily true and not necessarily false – statements. Because the definition is already difficult for many to apply, and because knowledge
of necessary statements requires separate treatment, this additional condition will be omitted in what follows.

211

Separation by Foundation
The medical literature is full of case histories of people with nervous system malfunction so
severe that they make mistakes about the presence or absence of parts of their own bodies.
In phantom limb pain, an amputee may continue to feel pain as if it is in her missing limb,
even though she can see quite clearly that the limb is gone. Some stroke victims lose all sense
of ownership and all voluntary control over a limb, sometimes asking for its removal from
the hospital bed because, after all, it must belong to someone else. It's as if the brain
maintained a map of the body that can get out of sync with the actual state of the body.
So imagine that you've been in an accident, and you're just awakening from anesthesia after
surgery. You take inventory: "Two legs, two arms, and, thank goodness, I still have my nose."
But, despite heroic efforts, your nose was too badly damaged to save, and the surgeons were
forced to do an emergency nosectomy. You are now noseless, though you don't yet know it.
The bare conceivability of such mistakenness about your own nose is enough to deprive your
belief "I now have a nose" of classically self-evident status. From the perspective of classical
self-evidence, even your own body is in the external world, outside your mind.

The definition of classically self-evident has two other important consequences.
First, classically self-evident beliefs are only current and thus fleeting; that is, they change
from moment to moment. Although you can remember a past mental state, that would
require using your memory, and that makes error possible. So the definition entails that you
cannot have any classically self-evident beliefs about a past mental state that you know about
only through memory.
Second, classically self-evident beliefs are private in the sense that your classically selfevident beliefs must be different from everyone else’s classically self-evident beliefs. This is
because your classically self-evident beliefs are about your current, conscious mental states
213

Separation by Foundation
and their classically self-evident beliefs are about their current, conscious mental states.
You can and do know many things about your past mental states and the mental states of
others, just as you can and do know many things about your body and its parts. But much
more is required for classically self-evident belief than is required for knowledge. (See Belief,
Knowledge, Classically Self-Evident Belief and Foundational Belief, with its diagram, below.)
Avoiding Common Confusions That Make It Impossible to Apply the Definition of "Classically
Self-Evident" (CSE) Correctly

In most ordinary conversational contexts, these two forms of expression are used as if they
conveyed the same information:
I think that S
S
For example, suppose that you were asked,
Q1: Is there any ice cream in the freezer?
You might answer with either of two sentences:
A1: Yes, there's ice cream in the freezer.
or
A2: Yes, I think that there's ice cream in the freezer.
Similarly, if the question were instead phrased as:
Q2: Do you think that there's any ice cream in the freezer?
you could appropriately reply with either A1 or A2. So, in many contexts, A1 and A2 can be
pragmatically equivalent, as linguists say.
But for our present purposes, we must have some way of clearly marking the distinction
between statements about the mind and statements not solely about mental subject matters.
And in English it's difficult if not impossible to do this without insisting on a difference in
meaning between such sentences as
I believe that S (I think that S, it seems to me that S, It appears to me that S, etc.)
on the one hand, and
S
on the other. And that’s what we’ll do. From now on,
S ≠ I think that S.
Because, however, our deeply ingrained linguistic habit is to treat these forms of expression
as equivalent, it is especially easy to misapply the definition of "classically self-evident" and

214

Separation by Foundation
to misclassify "there's ice cream in the freezer" as classically self-evident (adding, " ... but
that's what I think, so it's true for me.").175
Candidate CSE Statement

Non-CSE Component

I believe that God exists.

God exists.

It seems to me that I have two hands.

I have two hands.

I think that Afghanistan is next to South Africa.

Afghanistan is next to South Africa.

It seems to me that: there's a dog barking outside There's a dog barking outside my winmy window.
dow.
I believe that: my hearing a dog's bark is caused by My hearing a dog's bark is caused by a
a real dog's barking.
real dog's barking.
It’s my opinion that: my obsession with cleanli- My obsession with cleanliness was
ness was caused by overly strict toilet-training. caused by overly strict toilet-training.
Plantinga’s Refutation of Classical Foundationalism

Among your foundational beliefs are surely all of your classically self-evident beliefs. But do
any other beliefs deserve to be foundational as well?
Classical Foundationalism takes the highly - maximally - restrictive view that the classically
self-evident statements are the only ones that belong in the foundation.
Classical Foundationalism (CF): The foundational beliefs for a person at a time are
all and only the classically self-evident statements for her at that time; these and
any derivable from these, with certainty, are known or justifiedly believed.
Classical Foundationalism thus sets the highest possible standards for the foundational and
other beliefs: they must be wholly immune to any possible doubt or error.
Classical Foundationalism has been the object of many attacks. Alvin Plantinga presents one
of them in "Is Belief in God Properly Basic?" He says that Classical Foundationalism is selfreferentially incoherent. His objection is an ingenious one.
Consider the statement (CF), which is Classical Foundationalism itself. Presumably, its
proponents hold that it is possible to know that their view is correct. But then, according to
Classical Foundationalism, (CF) must be either self-evident or derivable with certainty from
self-evident statements. It is clearly not self-evident, nor does there appear to be any hope
of deriving it from a bunch of statements about how things now appear to me to be. So,
according to Classical Foundationalism, it itself is unknowable or unjustifiable.
Classical Foundationalism is Self-Referentially Incoherent
1. If Classical Foundationalism is true, then a statement is justifiable only if it is
175 Also remember the warnings in “Separation by Fact and Value,” about using “S is true for me” to say, “I

think that S is true” or “I believe that S.”

215

Separation by Foundation

2.
3.
4.
5.

either classically self-evident or derivable with certainty from such statements
CF is not classically self-evident
CF is not derivable with certainty from classically self-evident statements
It is irrational to hold unjustifiable views.
Therefore, if Classical Foundationalism is true, it is irrational to hold it.

Of course if Classical Foundationalism is false, and we consider that fact, it would be
irrational to hold it. Either way, Classical Foundationalism seems to be a view that it would
be irrational to hold. It presents us with the very odd spectacle of a theory of rationality that
says to you, "Don't believe me! Why, you'd have to be irrational to believe me!"
This argument, as well as common sense, suggest that Classical Foundationalism is too
restrictive in setting irrationally high standards for rational belief: it shuts the door on any
number of perfectly reasonable foundational beliefs that, although not classically selfevident, are fit for inclusion in the foundation.
So, we might also object to Classical Foundationalism in this second way: There are many
possibilities that might interfere with our justifications of beliefs. The dream hypothesis is
one that we've seen already. You might like to entertain yourself by imagining others. For
example consider the possibility that you are a disembodied brain floating in a vat of
nutrients, and that the Mad Scientist has stuck electrodes in your brain to make you think
that you're embodied and reading a book. Or, you’re a creature of The Matrix.
But unless you have some compelling and specific reason to take that possibility seriously in
a given situation, it should not be allowed to interfere with your justification: some possible
alternatives are normally irrelevant and (thought-) ought to be ignored. It is possible to get
into abnormal situations – you’re suddenly told by a reliable source that the water supply
has been contaminated by hallucinogens, and you begin to wonder how much of the day
really happened - but in such situations we typically know what to do to check on the
abnormalities and to discover when they're no longer an issue.
We began with the question: Do we always need evidence for rational belief? We saw that in
some cases - the classically self-evident ones - independent evidence is not needed. We have
also seen that knowledge and justified belief have a structure described by Foundationalism:
some beliefs are justified because they rest on yet other beliefs, but there must be an end to
requests for justification; they terminate in beliefs that need no independent justification.
And we have discovered that the foundation must contain more than the classically selfevident beliefs. Let's call this, by way of summary, Common Sense Foundationalism.
Common sense beliefs are, roughly, those that it would be truly insane to have doubts about
in a normal situation. Although such “common sense” beliefs are less than classically selfevident, they can nevertheless qualify as foundational. As foundational beliefs, common
sense beliefs will not ordinarily require support by argument or evidence, will be highly
specific and will not be generalizations. So, for example, “Some trees have leaves” would not
express a common sense belief, although “That’s a tree” (said while looking at and focusing
on a particular tree) would express a common sense belief under normal circumstances.
Plantinga also provides us with a helpful way of describing situations in which a belief is
properly taken to be basic (i.e., foundational) when the belief is not classically self-evident.
To introduce his way of describing such situations, consider the following simple example:
216

Separation by Foundation
Sean is a healthy four-year old human being in Raleigh. Sean is looking straight at a
green ball which is a couple of feet away, nothing gets in the way of Sean’s having a
clear view of the ball, the lighting is good, Sean is not distracted and is on the contrary
paying close attention, Sean’s vision is entirely normal, and there is no one attempting
to deceive Sean in any way. Under these circumstances, Sean believes and knows
that’s green (thinking about the green ball).
Jean is a healthy twenty-year old human being in Raleigh. Jean is looking straight at a
green ball which is a couple of feet away, nothing gets in the way of Jean’s having a
clear view of the ball, the lighting is good, Jean is not distracted and is on the contrary
paying close attention, Jean’s vision is entirely normal, and there is no one attempting
to deceive Jean in any way. Under these circumstances, Jean believes and knows that’s
green (thinking about the green ball).
Mr. Gorser, the pushy but well-meaning neighborhood Classical Foundationalist, challenges Sean to justify the belief that’s green by describing fully any relevant evidence.
Sean hears but is unable to understand Mr. Gorser’s demands - Sean is after all only
four years old - and does not respond to them.
Mr. Gorser also challenges Jean to justify the belief that’s green by describing fully any
relevant evidence. Jean hears and understands Mr. Gorser’s demands and refuses to
do as Mr. Gorser demands.176
The circumstances under which Sean and Jean see the green ball and form their belief about
it are called “grounding circumstances” by Plantinga. Thus, Plantinga would describe Sean
and Jean as having the grounded belief about the ball that’s green; under the circumstances
described, that belief is both properly basic for and known by each of them, but it is clearly
not classically self-evident for either Sean or Jean. Their grounding circumstances cannot
be evidence for their belief. Only beliefs can be evidence for beliefs; circumstances are not
beliefs.
Because Sean and Jean are in grounding circumstances for belief about the ball that’s green,
the belief that’s green is grounded, properly basic and does not need to be supported by independent evidence. Simply being in the right circumstances – those that ground the belief –
is sufficient to make it reasonable for Sean and Jean to hold the belief.
Similarly, your belief about the red chair that’s red (see “Separation by Fact and Value”) was
a grounded belief as there described. That’s why the skeptic’s challenges to your belief could
quite reasonably be turned aside without providing any independent evidence.
Plantinga’s Account of Faith

What has all this to do with faith?
To have faith in something is to make it foundational. It is not because we can provide
further evidence for them that we have faith in them. It is because they need no independent
justification. In this way, faith can gain intellectual respectability by associating with all those
176 “Gorser” is “Rogers” spelled inside out. Mr. (Fred) Rogers was neither pushy nor a Classical Foundationalist though he was well-meaning; also, he lived in Squirrel Hill, Pittsburgh, PA. Surely, Mr. Rogers would have
been a Common Sense Foundationalist. How unfortunate it is that he is no longer with us.

217

Separation by Foundation
other common sense beliefs, like "I have a hand" and "this is a page I'm now reading ". For,
as we have already discovered, the foundation must be made thicker if it is to sustain the
weight it needs to bear, and we're going to have to accept some such common sense
statements without justification anyway.
Plantinga suggests that among the beliefs in the thickened foundation of a theist may be some
religious beliefs; e.g., "God forgives me," "God is to be worshiped," "God has created universe
all this around me." etc. As he puts it, belief in God is "properly basic". Let's call his view
“Theistically Thick Foundationalism.”
Of course, on this view, one may not be able to give an airtight proof that God exists - but the
same can be said, for example, of your belief that there are people in the room with you, or
that you are now reading a book. There is little to be done, and normally nothing that needs
to be done, against the Dream Hypothesis except to assume that the properly basic beliefs
somehow get beyond it, and this supposition seems eminently rational.
So the Evidentialist Objection to Belief in God turns out to have a false first premise. In some
cases, believing without proof or evidence is quite rational, and belief in God may be one of
them. Plantinga offers the theist a rather clear way to characterize faith and to make it
rational. He can thus explain why numerous attempts to prove the existence of God are not
necessary: they are attempts to prove what needs no proof. He even gives believers a way of
making good use of religious experience: statements of such experience can be properly
basic as well, fitting as they do with similar beliefs in a theistically thick foundation.
Plantinga's view is not without its problems, and, characteristically, he is among the first to
point them out. The main problem is, how does one rationally decide in general what to include
in the foundations? There must be some constraints, unless 'anything goes' - and that
would be irrational indeed; Plantinga explicitly rejects the idea that any belief someone might
pick qualifies for foundational status. But on what general, principled grounds can one charge
an adherent of Vegetabilism, or Mr. Maxwell Jones, of cognitive irresponsibility, while
keeping foundationalism safe for Western theology? What reason can be given for preferring
the Bible to the Book of Fluctuations, or to Vegetabilist or Pastafarian or Church of the
Subgenius scripture? Plantinga's full response took almost twenty years and is about three
books long, wholly appropriate given the depth and difficulty of the question.177 But because
this difficulty does not threaten Plantinga's proposed characterization of faith, I leave it to
you to read what Plantinga has to say.
Now that we know what faith is, we can evaluate Separation by Foundation, and we can see
quite clearly that it is false. If accepting something on faith is including it in the foundations,
then it is clear that every minimally rational person accepts a great deal on faith, scientists
doing science included. They do, of course, accept the testimony of their fellow scientists on
many matters - not every claim made in the journals gets checked before being accepted.
Even in those cases where checks are made, where, for example, experiments are repeated,
scientists do not allow themselves to be taken in by Classical Foundationalism, not even
when they are doing perceptual psychology. We would rightly think a scientist insane if they
began to raise serious questions about the existence of their laboratory or co-workers.
177Warrant: The Current Debate (Oxford University Press, 1993); Warrant and Proper Function (Oxford Uni-

versity Press, 1993); Warranted Christian Belief (Oxford University Press, 2000).
218

Separation by Foundation
Science may move beyond common sense, but it has no alternative to starting with it.
Scientists, like the other rational believers, have no choice but to accept a great deal without
proof or justification. So accepting things, even important things, without proof, marks no
difference between science and religion.
It may be that what is accepted in this way, on faith, is different in science and religion, but
that's tantamount to admitting that Separation by Foundation is false (-because it says that
it is how statements are accepted, not what they say, that matters), and the difference lies
elsewhere. Perhaps it does. Many theists have thought that the best response to the
Evidentialist Objection is to refute its second premise by producing the evidence. We will
consider what they have to in the next chapter, “Separation by Explanation.”
What is it like to be a Classical Foundationalist?

Despite Plantinga’s refutation of Classical Foundationalism, some remain attracted to this
“Why not the best?” view, and persist in asking annoying questions of the sort, “Well, how
can you really be sure that ….?” So, it might be useful to reflect on what it would be like to try
to live up to the extraordinarily exacting standards of Classical Foundationalism.
Because classically self-evident beliefs change from moment to moment, Classical
Foundationalism would demand that you check on your beliefs at every moment to make
absolutely certain that they were still reasonable.
And any possibility, no matter how absurd, should it occur to you, would have to be ruled out
with absolute certainty before you could reasonably accept any belief with which the possibility could interfere. So, for example, not only would you need to rule out the possibility that
you are dreaming or drugged or a brain in a vat, but you would also need to rule out more
specific possibilities that could interfere with more specific beliefs. After all, you can’t be absolutely certain that ruling out a general possibility takes care of all the more specific ones,
of which there are many, perhaps infinitely many.
Should it occur to you in passing that, say, your pen might be an alien life form that will suffer
horrible agony if its “clip” is flexed on being placed in your bag or pocket, causing you to be
charged with a felony under intergalactic law and triggering an intergalactic conflict in which
quadrillions of life forms might die, then you must rule out that possibility with absolute certainty before you reject that utterly absurd belief.
Of course, it might also occur to you that expending the enormous amounts of time and energy trying to do all that would result in neglect of other duties and obligations to yourself
and others, causing much pain and suffering.
But you really can’t be absolutely certain of that either, so you must make absolutely certain
of all it as well – in the course of which many other possibilities will doubtless occur to you.
And that process will be repeated thousands of times over at every moment of every minute
of every hour of every day, without end, for all the less absurd possibilities that are actually
likely to occur to you.
Repeating that process in that way is however humanly impossible. It far exceeds our cognitive bandwidths, which probably can’t be stretched beyond considering a few things at once.
So, the frantic attempt to deal constantly with the Classical Foundationalist demand perpetually to check with the greatest possible care – care that exceeds what is humanly possible
219

Separation by Foundation
in even the most modest and infrequent circumstances – will instead result in nervous system meltdown.
Perhaps that’s what those who were once called “catatonic schizophrenics” suffered in some
instances: Descartes’ Disease, or End-Stage Classical Foundationalism.
Sometimes, “the best is the enemy of the good.”
The failure of Classical Foundationalism is however no excuse for going slumming in
the cognitive gutter. The challenge is to figure out how to back away from the excesses
of Classical Foundationalism without abandoning the good.
But the next time someone annoys you by pressing questions of the sort,
“Well, how can you really be sure that ….?”
“Can you proooove that ___?”

you can tell them that you have better things to do than to respond to their questions, which
are sophomoric178 in the worst sense of the term.
Although (unlike Plantinga’s argument) these observations about human psychology do not
constitute a refutation of Classical Foundationalism, they might help you to rid yourself of any
lingering attachment to the view.
On The Kinds of Necessity, and CSE Belief

The definition of CSE statements relies on a notion of necessity. There are various kinds of
necessity and only one of them – the strongest - is used in that definition. The kinds of
necessity may be arranged in order of their strength. To each kind of necessity there
corresponds a kind of possibility which is thereby arranged in order of decreasing strength.
It’s sometimes useful to think of necessity as determined by a set of laws or rules.
Some examples:
Parental necessity: what your parents compelled you to do when you were kid. There
were certain rules that they enforced. For example, “Be in bed with the lights out by
9PM if you are under the age of 8 years.” That’s a pretty weak kind of necessity,
because it’s pretty easy to break the rule.
Cultural necessity: what your culture compels you to do. Various activities are
encouraged and discouraged, some very strongly so. “Don’t kill disabled children.”
“Wear some clothing to the office or in school.” “Use bathrooms for eliminative
functions.” “Puke in private.” This seems a stronger sort of necessity than parental. If
someone proposes to go to the office wearing nothing but a hat, the “can’t” in “You
can’t do that!” has a good deal of force.
Physical necessity: what nature compels; what the laws of nature make happen.
“Gravity is an attractive force, proportional to the mass and inversely proportional to
178 -derived from the same Greek root as “sophistry.” For more on the associated practice, see Harry Frankfurt, On Bullshit (Princeton University Press, 2005) http://www2.lib.ncsu.edu/catalog/record/NCSU1845025
; and Gary L. Hardcastle and George A. Reisch, eds., Bullshit and Philosophy: Guaranteed to Get Perfect Results
Every Time (Open Court, 2006) e-book: https://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/catalog/NCSU4212677 .

220

Separation by Foundation
the square of the distance between any two physical objects.” This is a very strong
sort of necessity.
Metaphysical necessity: what must happen, what must be, even allowing for variation
in the laws of nature. It is possible that the laws of nature be different from what they
actually are. There might have been no attractive physical forces, or there might have
been inverse cube forces, or no forces at all, or no physical objects.
Logical necessity: what the laws of logic dictate. Anything but a logical contradiction
(P & not-P) or what would logically entail a logical contradiction is ruled out. Anything
else is allowed. This is the strongest kind of necessity.
(Some argue that logical necessity = metaphysical necessity. We needn’t settle that issue.
Once you get as far as metaphysical necessity you’re dealing with a very strong kind of
necessity.)
A very useful way of thinking of necessity and its twin or dual notion of possibility is by using
the notion of a possible world (universe).
A statement is X-necessarily true if and only if it is true in every X-possible
universe.
So, “Lights out by 9PM for < 8 yrs old” is parentally necessarily true because in every possible
universe governed by the same rules as laid down by your parents, the lights are out by 9PM
for those under the age of 8 years.
And 2+2=4 is logically necessarily true because in every possible world without exception,
2+2=4.179
The kind of necessity in the definition of classically self-evident belief is logical necessity.
Confusing Terms – “Subjective”, “Objective” – Avoid Them

Aside from the phenomenon of pragmatic equivalence, the terminology of “objective” and
“subjective” is a source of a lot of confusion. It is a source of particular confusion when it
comes to applying the definition of CSE.
Here is the definition again:
A belief S is classically self-evident for you now =def necessarily, if you think that S
is true, then S is true.
where “think that” could just as well be “believe that” or “it appears to me that” etc.
Suppose we say that a statement S is objectively true if and only if S corresponds to some
mind-independent fact about the world. That’s often what “objectively true” is used to mean.
For example, in the discussion of naïve moral relativism, when the NMR says there are no
objective truths of morality because when it comes to morality, it’s all just a matter of
(possibly collective) opinion, the phrase “objectively true” must mean something like
179 The logically necessary truth that 2+2=4 is of course not the obviously false claim that it is logically necessary that the sentence of arithmetic, “2+2=4” express the truth that 2+2=4. The sentence and its constituent
symbols didn’t even exist before people began using them, but the truth that 2+2=4 was around long before
people were.

221

Separation by Foundation
opinion-independent truth. And of course opinions are creatures of the mind if anything is;
they are beliefs.
Now suppose that we say that a statement S is subjective if and only if it is wholly about
someone’s mental state. The “wholly” here is intended to limit the scope to mind where the
mental state resides, so a statement about how your current visual experience of black type
is caused by seeing certain real black physical objects would not count as subjective. That
seems to be one meaning of “subjective.”
Using this characterization of “subjective” we can say that a statement S is objectively true if
and only if S corresponds to some fact about the world that is independent of the truth of any
subjective statement.
Suppose now that we ask, Are CSE beliefs objectively true? The answer would have to be
“No.” And given the stipulations that have just been made, that’s fine.
But it’s also likely to mislead, because the stipulations will very likely be forgotten, and what
will remain is a misunderstanding: that CSE beliefs can’t be about solid matters of fact
because they are about mental states. And the latter is dead wrong.
Instead, CSE beliefs are about facts. These facts happen to be facts about minds. But they are
no less factual than facts about chairs and planets. And they are furthermore knowable in
ways that facts about chairs and planets and many other things outside of minds are not
knowable: they are knowable with absolute certainty.
Although they are knowable with absolute certainty only by the minds that have them, they
are knowable in other ways by other minds. It is after all not only possible but easy for others
to know that you have particular beliefs, perceptions and feelings. Well before sophisticated,
high-tech brain-scanning devices were invented, such knowledge was easy to gain (not that
such devices yet give us that knowledge). We get it by talking with people and seeing what
they do; we ask them what they think. Knowledge does not require absolute certainty, as the
refutation of Classical Foundationalism reminds us.
It would be best if we never used the terminology of “objective” and “subjective” at all in
discussing knowledge, belief, and justification.
Belief, Knowledge, Classically Self-Evident Belief and Foundational Belief

To believe a statement, S, is simply to think that S is true. (To say, “My beliefs are true for me”
is to assert the triviality, “I think that my beliefs are true;” that is: “My beliefs are my beliefs.”)
Knowledge requires more than mere belief. To know a statement S is for S to be true and to
have an adequately justified belief that S is true.
Classically self-evident belief that S requires more than mere knowledge. To have at a time
classically self-evident belief that S is to believe S and for it to then to be logically impossible
for S to be false. Classically self-evident beliefs may thus be aptly described as self-justifying,
or as needing no independent justification.
Foundational, non-CSE beliefs are those that are normally beyond rational challenge, but
it is possible (though very unlikely) for them to be false.
The relationship among these four concepts is illustrated below:
222

Separation by Foundation

Belief

Knowledge
Foundational,
non-CSE beliefs
CSE

223

Separation by Foundation
Sentences vs. Statements

Sentences are linguistic tools for expressing statements, making assertions and telling others
what we believe (among other things)180, and it is the statements made that are evaluated as
being about something or other and as being true or false.
Statements are the meanings of sentences. Statements ≠ sentences.
We don't believe sentences; we believe statements that we can express using sentences.
Sentences are not true or false; what they are used to express - statements - are true or false.
Fortunately, it is often feasible to tell which statement someone intends to express by their
use of a particular sentence at a particular time in a particular context. And if you're talking
only to yourself, it should be easy, at least, to tell what statement you yourself intend to be
expressing to yourself!
If someone is not fluent in a language, though, it may be hard work to figure out which statement is being made by a particular use of a sentence.
Here are three different sentences that when used in typical ways express the very same
statement:
Snow is white.
Der Schnee ist Weiss.
La neige est blanche.
It’s obvious that the sentences are different because they are made from different words.
Sentences live in languages, but statements do not. Translations tell us whether different sentences from different languages make the same statement. Some translations are correct and
others incorrect, but that does not depend on the truth or falsity of the statement.
To make things a bit simpler for the purpose of test-taking in this course, the course stipulates that "I think that S" does not have the same meaning as "S" (and the same goes for "I
believe that S" and "S" and ""It seems to me that S" and "S," etc.).
That stipulation is needed because in conversational English, "I think that S" (etc.) and "S"
can be - and often are - used as if they had the same meaning and thus to convey the same
information, as examples previously given remind us.
["I think that S" (etc.) and "S" can be used to convey different information as well. But if you
try to use them to convey the same and different information at the same time, you'll probably fail and will confuse those listening to you. That's why we don't do that.]
There is no simple, linguistic rule that determines whether a sentence expresses a CSE belief.
It is the definition of CSE that determines whether a given statement, S, is or is not CSE. One
must begin, in applying the definition, by asking, What is the statement S about? - that is,
What would have to be true if the statement S were true? Or, equivalently, What are the statement S's truth conditions?

180 For example, sentences can be used to ask questions (“Is the door shut?”), to make requests (“Please shut

the door.”) or to exclaim (“Wow!”).

224

Separation by Foundation
Given the stipulations already made, I'm now seeing some words on this computer screen in
front of me is about the computer screen in front of me, and that statement is true only if I
really am now seeing some words on that computer screen.
I think that I'm now seeing some words on this computer screen in front of me is NOT about
any computer screen; instead, it is about my current visual experiences - events that are occurring now in my mind as current conscious mental states. I think that I'm now seeing
some words on this computer screen in front of me would be true even if I were nowhere
near any computer screens and was instead hallucinating or dreaming of words on the
screen of my computer.
Because of the way in which the definition of classically self-evident belief works, any statement that is NOT about one's own current, conscious mental state will NOT be CSE. That is
to say: all CSE beliefs must be beliefs about one's own current, conscious mental states. (All
non-M's are non-CSE's = All CSEs are M's.) So:
It does not follow that all beliefs about one's own current, conscious mental states are
CSE. That's false. (All M's are CSE's ≠ All CSEs are M's.) (That would be to make mistake #2
of the Four Common Mistakes to be Avoided - see the Separation Principles Matrix.) We all
have lots of true beliefs about how our mental states are caused and no such belief could be
CSE, as Descartes' Dream Argument is intended to remind us.
The following complex statement is NOT CSE:
[The cause of
{I think that I'm now seeing some words on this computer screen in front of me}
is:
{I'm now seeing some words on this computer screen in front of me}]
For that complex, relational statement to be true, there must be a particular kind of causeand-effect relationship between my mental state (visual experience) now and the actual
computer screen; the seeing causes the believing. I can know that the seeing causes the believing, but it cannot be CSE that the seeing causes the believing. Again, the point of Descartes' Dream Argument is to remind us of the latter.
Of course, we also have lots of beliefs about our mental states that don't say anything about
their supposed causes and they, too, can fail to be CSE. (For example, they could be about the
effects of having the beliefs. 'Self-help' books are full of claims about the effects of having
various kinds of beliefs, and many of those claims are doubtful or even known to be false.)
Our beliefs about the causes of our mental states are just especially clear examples of nonCSE beliefs about our mental states.
Some Exercises on Applying the Definition of “Classically self-evident”

Assume that you are in Withers 232A, a 293-seat lecture hall in which all the seats have red
upholstery. Assume that you counted them all. Assume that in the following, Linus is a real
person but do not assume that the Great Pumpkin is real. By now, you have burned the definition of classically self-evident belief (CSE) into your central nervous system.

225

Separation by Foundation
Suppose that you now believe the statement: Known?
1. That chair is red. (pick a red chair)
2. It appears to me that that chair is red.
3. It’s appearing to me that that chair is red
is caused by that chair’s being red.
4. I think that: [it’s appearing to me that the
chair is red is caused by that chair’s being
red].
5. There are more than 237 chairs here.
6. The Great Pumpkin exists.
7. I think that the Great Pumpkin exists.
8. That Linus now thinks that the Great
Pumpkin exists is CSE for Linus.
9. That I now think that the Great Pumpkin
exists is CSE for me. †
Another question to consider:

“Common sense”?

CSE?

Neo, a neon sign, displays, “I am illuminated” when and only when Neo is on. Neo has
been engineered to be extremely reliable, with backups for backups for backups ….
Neo comes with a money-back guarantee for the life of the universe.
But then it’s guaranteed that: if Neo says “I am illuminated,” then Neo is illuminated.
So, I am illuminated is classically self-evident for Neo.
Is that reasoning correct? Is “I am illuminated” CSE for Neo? Why or why not? [Hints: (i) Does
“it is guaranteed” = “necessarily”? (ii) Does “display” = “thinks” (“believes,” “says”)?]
Costly Confusion #6: In doing science, one should never take anything on faith.

During Fall 2010 – Spring 2015, over 2400 students enrolled in PHI 340 responded to surveys asking for their opinions on issues discussed in the course.181 The same survey was
given at the beginning and at the end of each term. One of five responses was allowed for
each of the fifty survey items: strongly agree, agree, neither agree nor disagree, disagree,
strongly disagree. For six of the survey items, there was a significant negative relationship
between strength of agreement on the end-of-term survey and the grade-determining
course score. Each of the six items expressed a common mistake argued against in the
course. Students who persisted in making these mistakes thus tended to perform less well.
One of the items for which the downward trend was strongest was:
In doing science, one never accepts things on faith or without proof.

181 You can find a summary of survey results for 1986 -2015 in the Getting Started in PHI 340 folder on the

course web site home page. There are many interesting patterns in the responses.
226

Separation by Foundation

227

Separation by Explanation
Separation by Explanation
In considering the Evidentialist Objection to the belief that God exists, I presented the
evidentialist premise that there isn't adequate evidence for God's existence, either because
there is no such evidence, or because there is some evidence, but that it is not sufficiently
strong or compelling evidence. I want now to look at a direct response to this premise, one
of the most famous and widely honored arguments for the existence of God: the Argument
from Design, or Teleological Argument. ("Teleological" comes from Greek roots meaning,
roughly, purpose-directed.) According to the various versions of this argument, there is
compelling evidence of God's existence because we can see God's purposes and design in
many parts of the universe, and in the universe itself, considered as a whole. We've already
met this kind of argument in Plutarch's Purpose Argument, concerning extraterrestrial life,
but it's so important, it's worth going over in more detail. Probably the best discussion of the
argument in a religious context was given during the late 18th century by the British
philosopher and historian David Hume, in his beautifully written book Dialogues Concerning
Natural Religion. Here is a brief excerpt, stating a main argument.
Look round the world: contemplate the whole and every part of it: You will find it
to be nothing but one great machine, subdivided into an infinite number of lesser
machines, which again admit of subdivisions to a degree beyond what human
senses and faculties can trace and explain. All these various machines, and even
their most minute parts, are adjusted to each other with an accuracy which
ravishes into admiration all men who have ever contemplated them. The curious
adapting of means to ends, throughout all nature, resembles exactly, though it
much exceeds, the productions of human contrivance; of human designs, thought,
wisdom, and intelligence. Since, therefore, the effects resemble each other, we
are led to infer, by all the rules of analogy, that the causes also resemble; and that
the Author of Nature is somewhat similar to the mind of man, though possessed
of much larger faculties, proportioned to the grandeur of the work which he has
executed. By this argument a posteriori, and by this argument alone, do we prove
at once the existence of a Deity, and his similarity to human mind and
intelligence.182
We can gain a better appreciation of this argument if we begin with bits and pieces of the
universe, and work our way up to the universe itself.
Arguments from Design

While on a walk in the woods with my Beauceron, Roxanne, and my friend, Skeptical Sam,
Sam wasn’t watching where she was going and didn’t notice an object in her path. She tripped
and fell on her face. I retrieved the object and examined it for a few minutes while Sam pulled
herself together. I noted that the object had many parts, among them a viewfinder, a lens, a
focusing-ring, various buttons and levers, etc. After a few minutes of non-expert examination
- I'm no optical engineer - I was able to see the soundness of:

182 http://www.davidhume.org/texts/dnr.html

228

Separation by Explanation

The Camera Argument
1. The pattern of the camera's parts is a purpose-indicator (for picture-taking, as I
can tell in this case).
2. Wherever there is such a pattern, there is very probably an intelligent designer.
3. Very probably, this camera is designed.
This seems a perfectly reasonable way to argue. Precisely parallel arguments could be, and
are, made about any number of other familiar objects. And we have seen similar arguments
before, in Plutarch’s Purpose Argument (UFOlogy) and the discussion of IDC.
Living up to her name, and feeling pretty annoyed about having been tripped, Skeptical Sam
objected in three ways to the argument:
"See this lever on the right. It's twisted and no longer works. The camera has a broken
part, so it is not likely to have been designed." But this is not a good objection; perhaps
the object was broken in the factory, or in shipping, or by being abused by its previous,
obviously quite careless, owner.
"There's a little black button here on the side of the lens. Push it, and nothing happens.
The camera has a useless part, so it is not likely to have been designed." This is not a
good objection, either; perhaps there was an error in the manufacturing process, or
it's the designer's idea of a joke.
"It's all just a cosmic accident. Camera molecules drifting through the atmosphere
happened to glom together here on the forest floor. The camera came to be as it is
where it was purely by chance, so it is not likely to have been designed." Ha! This is
the worst objection of all; the possibility it mentions (if it's even a possibility) is so
unlikely that we need not take it seriously.
