Y

Machine Functionalism

The marvellous thing about the •-game is that, with the right interpretations, it's very useful.
(I-AIR) Let X = NV and Y = V, and we've got the much desired news about bathroom
availability on board the airplane.
(I-OR) Let X = False and Y = True, and we compute the meaning of "or."
(I-AND) With X = True, and Y = False, we compute the meaning of "and."
So despite the obvious differences in these four examples, they are all instances of the same
formal system. The game has two tokens and two 'squares'. Of course, we give the squares
and the tokens different names and meanings in each example, but that is irrelevant to their
identity as formal systems. What makes them formal is precisely the fact that meaning is
irrelevant.
Although the three very simple systems specified above all implement the same formal
system, there are infinitely many other formal systems, as Haugeland explains. Although it’s
not quite right, it can be helpful to think of formal systems as describing a wiring diagram
for a system of components. (The reason it’s not quite right is that formal systems describe
kinds of wiring diagrams and not any particular way of connecting the components. For
example, although Mr. B chose a parallel circuit for AND, he could have used a serial circuit.)
When Machine Functionalism analyzes mental states, the number and the types of basic
components are far greater, and the relationships far more complex - so simple tabular
displays are no longer useful or feasible. (It sometimes helps to think of networks of nerve
cells although nerve cells may themselves need to be modelled as complex networks.) But
the basic idea remains the same. Every mental state can be fully and accurately modeled
as some interpreted formal system.
MF is far richer in its resources than is Behaviorism. While Behaviorism is confined to inputs
and outputs at the boundary between organism and world, MF allows one to look inside and
to keep track of the internal processing. Thus MF adds a third element to the inputs and
outputs of Behaviorism: it adds relations among internal mental states of the subject. Mr.
Waldo and the Unintelligent Waldo Simulator have the same inputs and outputs (over a
rather wide range, anyway), but the computations that occur internally are of very different
sorts. The computations that occur in Mr. Waldo produce understanding, and the
computations that occur in the Simulator do not. Or so MF would claim. MF thus seems to
avoid the problem of pattern poverty: richer patterns are allowed, and we are told, precisely,
of what sort the patterns may be. MF seems to make good on the computer model for the
mind.
A Dangerous Analogy

Behaviorism was based on a 'black box' model of mental processes: we can treat everything
inside the body as if it were in an opaque, unopenable black box, and concentrate on the
patterns of inputs and outputs that the body might exhibit. MF replaces the one big black box
with a series of smaller black boxes, wired together in complicated ways. When we seek to
discern the nature of internal processing that MF says is essential to defining mental states
and processes, we break the process down into simpler sub-processes, much as one analyses
303

Machine Functionalism
a computer program using a flowchart. And just as we define the boxes in a flowchart by
specifying their inputs and outputs, MF tells us to define the sub-processes by their inputs
and outputs. We can go all the way down to the level of nerve cells (or below, if we wish).
When we talks about nerve cells, though, we must treat them as little black boxes which give
a particular kind of output for a specified input: they are functionally specified, as are the
pieces in a chess game, as members of a complex formal system. Where ingenuity is required
is in figuring out how to wire the little boxes together to produce intelligent mental
processing. So, if we are careful not to confuse MF and Behaviorism, it might be helpful to
think of MF as a kind of "Internal Behaviorism", which directs us to look not only at skinlevel behavior, but also at the behavior of the stuff inside.

It is this similarity between the two views that worries many people. How much good does it
really do to increase the number of 'black boxes'?
First of Three Objections: MF versus the Block Heads

Ned Block has given a famous objection to MF. He focuses on a particular sort of mental state:
sensations, like pain or pleasure. Unlike, say, belief or knowledge, sensations have a
characteristic feel. There is no particular way it feels to believe that 2+2=4, or to know that
there is a book in front of you. But having a toothache produces a very definite, all too familiar
sort of sensation, toothache pain, and its essential feature is that it hurts. In general, what
makes a sensation the mental state that it is, is precisely the way that it feels.
Suppose that at some time in the not too distant future, psychologists have discovered
enough about people to draw functionally accurate flowcharts for toothache pain. It is
plausible to assume that 4 billion nerve cells are involved in producing that unpleasant
sensation. Each of them has a certain array of inputs and outputs that contribute to toothache
pain. MF says that all there is to toothache pain is the pattern of inputs and outputs involving
the nerve cells wired together. The flowchart for toothache pain is therefore a certain formal
system. This system, like any other such system, is capable of many different sorts of physical
realizations. There is nothing in the nature of the system that says it must be built out of
small, squishy organic parts. Any network of components that obeys the rules will suffice.
304

Machine Functionalism
Drawing on these features of MF, Block asks us to perform a thought experiment. Imagine
that we convince the government of China that it would greatly enhance its international
prestige to participate in a great experiment in Cognitive Science. We equip each of the 4
billion or so individual members of the Chinese population with two-way radios, linked via
satellite to Cognitive Science Central in Cambridge, MA. Each of them receives instructions
about how to play the toothache pain game, each individual playing the role of an individual
nerve cell. (It's a game with 4 billion pieces. To avoid costly litigation, we give them all
anesthetic, so that they won't feel anything if they trip and fall during the simulation.) And,
for the period of an hour, the Chinese population obediently and flawlessly plays the
toothache game, just as it is played by your nerve cells.
About such a situation, MF must say that the Chinese population, considered as a whole,
suffers from toothache during this time. Just as the flight attendants could substitute for the
electrical components in the airplane bathroom status display, the Chinese people can
substitute for your nerve cells. But something can suffer toothache only if it hurts! So MF
entails that during the hour of the simulation, China hurts. That, says Block is absurd. You
cannot produce that sensation by such functional simulation. So MF is wrong. We can
summarize as follows:
Block's Absent Quality Objection to MF
1. If MF is true, then China hurts if it runs your toothache program.
2. China does not hurt if it runs your toothache program.
3. Therefore, MF is not true.
There is a common misunderstanding of this objection that we must avoid. Block is not
claiming that participation in the experiment would produce pain in the individual players
(-remember that they're full of anesthetic, anyway!). MF does not say that. Rather, it is the
whole system - the group that is the entire Chinese population - that has the pain and hurts,
not the individual components. MF must say that the system hurts. And that is what Block
finds absurd. Pain (and other sensations), Block says, are partly a matter of functional role,
but they also have an essential element of felt quality (sensation = functional role + felt
quality), and this latter element is what MF leaves out. If Block is right, MF is bedevilled by
pattern poverty, too: both external and internal behaviorism share a failing, if he is right.
MFs have often responded to this argument by claiming that because there is no more to
mental activity than functional role, the roles imagined in this odd experiment do produce
the sensation of pain. It sounds odd to say, "China hurts" because the whole situation is odd
and unfamiliar; sometimes, however, the truth is odd. Some of the oddity can be dispelled by
indulging in another science-fictional thought experiment, which I heard described in a talk
by one of the best and funniest philosophers of science, Clark Glymour.207
We already have artificial substitutes for hearts, lungs, kidneys, joints and other organs or
body parts. Some of these replacements don't work as well as the originals, but it's a matter
of time before better designs are produced. While no neuroscientist would claim to know
exactly how brain cells work, a great deal has been learned, and it is similarly a matter of
time before we are able to produce artificial neurons; they'll be something like the
207 http://philsci-archive.pitt.edu/141/1/Silicon_Reflections.rtf

305

Machine Functionalism
microchips of today, though much smaller and perhaps more complex ("silicon patches").
What they will do is to replicate the patterns of signal processing in wet, squishy organic
neurons; the patches will be functionally identical to biological brain cells, though made of
different (perhaps more robust) material.
I'd like you to imaginatively project yourself into the future era when such patches are
commonplace; I'd bet that younger readers will live in this era. Suppose that after years of
hard work, you are ready for a retirement full of the sports you never had time for, say, pingpong. But your years of alcohol consumption have pickled the brain cells that control parts
of your hands, and they no longer work as well as they once did for either pinging or ponging.
Your neighborhood neurosurgeon offers you hope: "We can remove the pickled cells and
patch in some functionally good-as-old silicon substitutes. It's a quick, virtually no-risk, office
procedure done under local anesthetic, and your health insurance will pay for it. It'd be no
worse for you than having a cavity filled by your dental robot. I've done thousands of these
procedures myself with no problems at all. You can call my patients to check up on me. Are
we going ahead, or are you going to sit by the side-lines and watch enviously while others
enjoy themselves at the table?" (When I've presented this thought experiment to people, only
about fifty percent say they want to go ahead at first. Then, I reassure them about the safety
of the procedure and remind them health insurance will take care of the bill, and the
percentage jumps to over ninety percent.)
Suppose that you have the work done and are once again able to win at ping-pong. Years pass
and you pickle more cells, which you also have replaced. Finally, you're a silicon head: all of
the old neurons have literally gone down the drain and you have functionally perfect
artificial neurons instead. In some ways, you're better off: you used to have to worry about
being hit in the head with your partner's paddle, but now, the paddle breaks. From the inside,
so to speak, things have remained as always, because the input-output patterns and the
pattern of intercellular connections have all been carefully preserved. If all that sounds right
to you, then you are having strong machine functionalist intuitions because all that's been
preserved are the relevant functional roles, and that seems to be enough.
Block has however a possible rejoinder, based partly on a rare but real medical condition:
[Ashlyn Blocker,] the girl who feels no pain was in the kitchen, stirring ramen
noodles, when the spoon slipped from her hand and dropped into the pot of
boiling water. It was a school night; the TV was on in the living room, and her
mother was folding clothes on the couch. Without thinking, Ashlyn ... reached her
right hand in to retrieve the spoon, then took her hand out of the water and stood
looking at it under the oven light. She walked a few steps to the sink and ran cold
water over all her faded white scars, then called to her mother, “I just put my
fingers in!” ...At school, she was once asked if she was Superman. Could she feel a
punch to the face? Could she walk across burning coals as if she were walking on
grass? Would it hurt if she were stabbed in the arm? The answers are no, no, yes,
no. She can feel pressure and texture. She can feel a hug and a handshake. She
felt her best friend, Katie, paint her toenails. “People don’t get me!” she said one
night while we played checkers on her iPod. “Everyone in my class asks me about
it, and I say, ‘I can feel pressure, but I can’t feel pain.’ Pain! I cannot feel it! I always
306

Machine Functionalism
have to explain that to them. ”The implications of her condition [congenital
insensitivity to pain] were profound. She was an anomaly of nature. Over the next
few years, [doctors] tested Ashlyn’s genetic material and eventually found two
mutations in her SCN9A gene. That same gene, mutated in a different way, led to
severe pain and chronic pain syndromes. She could feel tickles and pressure and
distinguish a soft touch from a pinprick, but she couldn’t perceive extremes of
temperature. [Her doctor] also gave her a range of psychological tests to
determine if she could feel emotional pain and empathy, and he found her to be
a bright and friendly child [capable of empathy].
“… what goes through your mind when you see someone hurt?” [her father]
asked her.
“I feel bad for them,” she said. “Because they go through the pain and I don’t. I
would help them.”
“When you see someone else in pain, what do you associate?”
“That must really hurt.”
“What is 'hurt'?”
Ashlyn squinted her eyes, as if in deep thought. She couldn’t answer him.208
Suppose that bioengineers were able to develop a system for those who suffer congenital
insensitivity to pain that gave them immediate reports on significant local tissue damage,
e.g., the system might say in a way audible only to the person, "Severe damage to left index
finger." (The system might even provide useful monitoring, of a sort that normal humans
lack, to alert to all significant damage to tissues, internal or external.) Thus, Ashlyn would
know immediately if she suffered a bad burn or cut, even though she would continue to feel
no pain at all. Assuming that the monitoring system could be developed and used as described, the example of ‘assisted Ashlyn’ would provide support for a distinction between (i)
the information that tissue damage has occurred in a particular bodily region and (ii) the
way that it feels when that damage occurs (it hurts!). And if information is not the very same
thing as the associated felt quality [(i) ≠ (ii)], then Block's Absent Quality Objection may well
succeed.
The debate may seem to end in a draw. There is in any case another objection that may prove
more serious.
An Objection to MF, On Purpose

Even if Block's objection were satisfactorily answered, there is another, even deeper
objection that the MF faces. The objection centers on the question of interpretation.
Recall the example of the "and" and "or" circuits, and the Bathroom Availability Display. In a
sense we made clear, all of these were physical realizations of the same formal system. But
this is not to imply that there is no difference in meaning among "and", "or" and "bathroom
available". It is just that these differences do not emerge until we say how the elements of
the system are to be interpreted, or assigned meaning. If we let the absence of current mean
false, then the circuit represents "or"; if the absence of current mean true, then the circuit
represents "and". Without an interpretation, the formal system is, strictly speaking, a
208 http://www.nytimes.com/2012/11/18/magazine/ashlyn-blocker-feels-no-pain.html

307

Machine Functionalism
meaningless game. It would make no sense to ask about a circuit, "What does its output
mean?" if the formal system it realizes had not been given an interpretation.
The same holds for MF in its attempt to give an account of mental processes. They are not
just automatic formal systems. They are automatic formal systems with an interpretation.
But now an important question arises: what is it about the internal and external behavior of
a physical system that determines which interpretation is the correct one? Suppose that Mr.
Waldo is doing a proof in a mathematics class and thinking a thought of the form "p or q". He
is not thinking of "and" and he is not on his way to the bathroom. Pretend that there is a
parallel circuit in his brain that executes the relevant operation. If it is to be that thought, and
not "p and q" (or "bathroom status"), then there must be some way of nailing down the
interpretation of the physical states of the circuit. But there pretty clearly is nothing about
the circuit that decides the matter: just as it is, it could (and does, according to our definition
of "physically realizes") equally well realize "p and q" or "bathroom status". Why isn't it doing
all three simultaneously?
Such questions are familiar in computer science. And there, they have familiar and
reasonable answers: it is relative to the purposes of the designers and the programmers, who
decide what the circuits are to represent. One and the same sequence of circuit flip-flops
might be used to make weather predictions on Monday, and on Tuesday to simulate battle
logistics in WWII. It all depends on what purpose the programmer had.
What happens if we try the same tactic with the thinking things that we are supposed to be?
The Machine Functionalist would then say that "S believes that p or q" means that S's body
(brain) executes the belief program relative to purpose P; or B(S,P). We introduce an extra
parameter and always relativize mental states interpretations to the purpose parameters.
The parameter, P, is supposed somehow to supply the missing interpretation. To make this
a bit less abstract, suppose that Mr. Waldo is doing his math homework and is proving a
theorem that requires him to consider two possibilities: the relevant numbers are odd or
they are even. He is thus having an “OR” thought, not an “AND” thought, and certainly not a
thought about finding a vacant airplane bathroom. According to Machine Functionalism.
there must be some portion of his central nervous system that is then functioning just like
an “OR”-circuit, though its physical constitution will be quite different. Just to have a label for
the relevant neural sub-system, call this chunk of brain tissue, which might be consist in
connected neurons scattered throughout his brain, the “OR-module.” We can even pretend
that OR-module contains a small parallel circuit because, regardless of what it does actually
contain, whatever is in the OR-module will on this occasion function just as a parallel circuit
in the Ms. A example. What the Machine Functionalist must explain is how the “OR-module”
circuitry comes to be interpreted with the meaning of “or” and not “and” or anything else. Of
course, Ms. A is not lurking in Mr. Waldo’s brain to do the job. Suppose instead, as seems
natural, that Machine Functionalism postulates a PURPOSE-module that on this occasion is
another chunk of brain circuitry that supplies the missing purpose parameter, P, relative to
which the OR-module gets the needed interpretation. That PURPOSE-module plays the same
role as did Ms. A without being a person with purposes and other mental states. But to supply
an interpretation-determining purpose-parameter, the PURPOSE-module can’t be engaged
in nothing but meaningless electrochemical activity; its activity, too, must implement an
interpreted formal system. Only if its activity is interpreted can it supply an interpretation to
308

Machine Functionalism
the OR-module.
But now an obvious question arises: where does the interpretation of the PURPOSE-module
come from? If Machine Functionalism postulates another module – the PURPOSE2-module?
– the very same question will arise about that third module as well. And the series of
questions will continue until Mr. Waldo (or more accurately, Machine Functionalism) runs
out of brain without ever having answered the first question: where does the interpretation
of the OR-module come from?

This objection presents a very serious problem for Machine Functionalism, and it’s of much
broader scope than Block’s Absent Quality Objection. It concerns all mental states, not just
sensations.
It thus seems that as long as Machine Functionalism does not “go outside the head” it cannot
account for the interpretations in IFS’s. It becomes nothing better than an elaborate
mathematical apparatus for describing literally meaningless electrochemical brain activity.
That is not a theory of mind or thought. This then is An Argument against Machine
Functionalism, on Purpose.
Because the problem results, it seems, from the limited material inside of thinking things,
one might try to address the objection by removing those limits and availing oneself of a
wider range of material. There is after all a great deal more to the universe than the inside of
a human head (or body): there is the rest of the universe! Perhaps by extending its reach,
Machine Functionalism will be able to exploit the many relationships between the inside of
the head and the wider universe to supply the missing purpose parameters and thus to
secure application of the concept of an IFS.
309

Machine Functionalism
There are several responses to this argument that are worth considering, mainly because
they help to show how deep the problem is.
First possible response: Suppose that we say, on behalf of the Machine Functionalism, that
it is God, our designer, who determines the interpretations of our brain function. We can call
this Theological Functionalism. [As a matter of sociological fact, no proponent of Machine
Functionalism is going to want to take this route, because Machine Functionalism is meant
as a way of saving a materialistic view of the mind; bringing God in at this point will seem (at
best) like a cheat to the proponent.] Anyway, it won't do any good. Presumably, one of God's
mental activities is having purposes. If there is significant similarity between his
purposiveness and ours, then Machine Functionalism will apply to his as well, and the
problem arises all over again (only now we're dealing with an incorporeal being, and it's
pretty obscure how the computer model is supposed to apply there). If, on the other hand,
God's being in that mental state is entirely dissimilar from any mental state any human being
could have, then we will have no idea how to understand such religious claims as "God
purposefully designed the Universe". Such claims play an important role in many religions,
and in attempting to persuade people to worship God. If making such claims strictly
unintelligible is the price of Theological Functionalism, then no religious believer will want
to endorse the view; and that leaves no one else to endorse it.
A second, more popular, response is Evolutionary Functionalism. In a metaphorical sense,
human beings are 'designed by Nature': their behavior is shaped by the forces of biological
evolution, as described by evolutionary theory, and these forces in turn shape our brains and
somehow supply the missing purpose parameter. Because these forces work primarily on
behavior, Evolutionary Functionalism is Behaviorism augmented by a distinction between
adaptive and non-adaptive behavior. Given what a miserable failure Behaviorism was, we
ought to be skeptical about Evolutionary Functionalism. Is it really plausible to insist that all
distinctions in thought must be tied to this distinction? Much of what we value most in
human life - advanced science, art, music, literature - seem to have no direct bearing on
biological survival, and some of it arguably gets in the way.
One standard objection to this view is the "Swampman [better: Swampperson] Objection."
Suppose that a molecule-for-molecule exact replica of you were to form accidentally out of
"swamp gas." It seems that they would surely be in the same mental states as you were at
the time of formation and would even believe, for example, that she was you and would have
many classically self-evident beliefs that paralleled yours. But Evolutionary Functionalism
says that, lacking an evolutionary history, Swampperson has no mental states at all, a wildly
counter-intuitive result, to say the least!209 If Evolutionary Functionalism were to retreat to
saying that all that’s necessary for being a thinking thing is to have some history or perhaps
to be somehow related to evolutionary histories, then it would be intolerably vague and
would also risk classifying far too wide a range of physical systems as thinking things.
[If the accidental formation of an atom-for-atom match for you seems unlikely, you’re surely
correct. But it is logically possible for such a thing to occur, and it may well be physically
209 For one of the few attempts to defend this result, see: Karen Neander, A mark of the mental: in defense of
informational teleosemantics (The MIT Press, 2018) https://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/catalog/DUKE008187405

310

Machine Functionalism
possible. Remember that we are looking for an analytical definition of “thinking thing,” and
thus the characterization of mental states that any theory of mind provides must be immune
to any possible counterexample. See the beginning of the “Meaning of ‘Life’,” above, on
definition and analysis. This requirement is imposed because the concepts defined must be
capable of guiding us in situations that we have not, and probably could not, anticipate, and
thus must be subject to the most severe ‘stress-testing’. Allowing counterexamples that are
‘merely’ logically possible helps to ensure that the stress is maximal.]
A third possible response to the argument is to reject the claim that interpreted formal
systems are sufficient to describe and explain all mental states and activities. That would be
to reject Machine Functionalism. We might make an exception for the missing purposeparameters, and thus endorse a version of Purpose-Directed Functionalism instead. We
would need to defend that move, but we have the materials for a defense at hand. In effect,
our entire discussion of theories of thought serves as a kind of argument for PurposeDirected Functionalism.
To be honest - the best policy - Purpose-Directed Functionalism is better described as a kind
of Dualism.
It turns out that Dualism comes in two varieties: there's Substance Dualism, according to
which mental activity requires a special, nonphysical substance; and there's what I call
Conceptual Dualism210, according to which there is at least one concept unique to psychology
that's needed to state and explain the facts about certain complex physical systems, namely,
the fact that they think.
Purpose-Directed Functionalism is a brand of Conceptual Dualism. It shares with Cartesian
Dualism the insight that physics won't suffice, but it rejects the reliance on soul-stuff.
Although Purpose-Directed Functionalism has its attractions, it might be subject to a version
of the Problem of Other Minds: how can you know about a particular physical system (say,
your best friend) that a basic notion of purpose applies to its “circuits” - given that this notion
cannot be analyzed in terms of concepts from physics, chemistry or computer science, useful
though their concepts remain?
These worries aside, our investigation of attempts to reduce biology and psychology to
physics provide strong support for the claim that some sort of purpose-directed explanation
is needed in each. If that's right, then our last, best separation principle, Separation by
Explanation, fails.
Another Objection to Machine Functionalism

Saul Kripke is well-known among mathematicians and philosophers for (among other
things) having developed a branch of mathematical logic that had during the previous 2500
years been unable to make much progress. So, it seems safe to assume that Saul Kripke is
pretty good at math and that he can, for example, add and multiply numbers. (He did a lot of
other important work in both math and philosophy. He’s still alive and working.)

210 This is also called “Property Dualism.” See William G. Lycan, “Is Property Dualism Better Off Than Substance Dualism?” Philosophical Studies v164 n2) (2013) 533-542.

311

Machine Functionalism
Kripke has argued that Machine Functionalism is a very bad model of the human mind. What
follows is a simplified account of how Kripke argued (in a talk that he gave at Duke in 1987,
which I attended) that Machine Functionalism is useless for modeling the human mind. 211
Some interpreted formal systems are Turing machines. Turing machines are infinite automata and can have infinite memories. Turing machines can add and can multiply. Interpreted
formal systems that can be implemented by human beings are all finite automata - mathematical descriptions of machines with finite memories. It is a somewhat surprising theorem
about finite automata that they can add but cannot multiply. 212 (Assume the theorem in what
follows.)
Kripke’s Refutation of Machine Functionalism
1. If Machine Functionalism is true, then every human thinking thing is a finite automaton.
2. Saul Kripke is a human thinking thing and can multiply.
3. No finite automaton can multiply.
4. So, not every human thinking thing is a finite automaton.
5. Therefore, Machine Functionalism is not true.
Reply 1: While the difference is not trivial, the use of Turing machines to model human
minds may nonetheless be defensible as an instance of idealization in scientific modeling.
Idealizations can be false but useful and can have explanatory power.

211 For a full presentation of Kripke’s argument, see: Jeff Buechner, “Kripke's critique of functionalism,” in
Alan Berger, ed., Saul Kripke (Cambridge University Press, 2011) 343-367. https://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/catalog/NCSU2461902 (Not long after he developed the branch of mathematical logic, Kripke – who was still living in his hometown of Omaha, NE - was offered a professorship by Harvard University. He turned down the
offer, saying that he thought he ought first to finish high school. He was 15 years old at the time he did the
work in logic. Saul Kripke can indeed multiply. https://www.gc.cuny.edu/faculty/core-bios/saul-kripke )
212 Here is a simple proof sketch, that finite automata cannot multiply, supplied by Dr. Shay Logan, Philosophy,
Kansas State University:
Suppose, without loss of generality, that the machine accepts binary inputs and is deterministic. The following
argument explains why the machine cannot compute the square of an arbitrary whole number.
Suppose the machine has n states. Let m > n. Feed the machine 2m in binary -- this is a 1 followed by m
zeros. As you feed this in, the machine, if it's "maximally efficient", will produce a string of m 0's (if it doesn't
do this, the problems pointed out below just get worse). Once you stop giving it input, the machine must then,
on its own, produce an additional m 0's and then a 1.
But this is impossible. Why? Because m > 0. As it spits out the 0's, the machine goes through state
changes. But because m>n, it must repeat some state. So it goes through a loop. And, because it's not getting
any more input, it will continue to traverse this loop over how ever many steps it has left in the calculation. But
on its first time through the loop it has to spit out a zero at every step. So because it's deterministic, it spits out
a zero at every step in the loop no matter how many times we go through it. So it can't ever spit out the 1 we
need it to spit out to get the squaring right.

312

Machine Functionalism

In her provocatively titled book, How the Laws of Physics Lie,213 philosopher of science Nancy
Cartwright argued that the supposed laws of particle physics (often expressed as differential
equations) do not actually state universal laws of nature but idealizations that, taken literally, are false. Unless we want to accuse physicists of lying, we should not, therefore, take
these “laws” as laws but as idealizations useful in explanatory models – which is exactly what,
she reports, physicists do (and say they are doing). (Kitcher’s discussion of auxiliary hypotheses is also relevant here. On Cartwright’s view, auxiliary hypotheses tell a scientist how
properly to use the false idealizations provided by a theory to describe actual phenomena.)
There are many other kinds of examples of the defensible use of false idealizations. Here are
five additional examples of useful, false idealization:
● There are very likely no perfect rectangles, but that doesn’t mean that Euclidean
(plane and solid) geometry is useless in housebuilding; carpenters quite sensibly
measure the diagonals of a rectangular structure to make sure that they match and
thus help to ensure that a foundation is “square” and not skewed.
● Chaos theory provides examples more nearly like the Turing machine case: fractals
are infinite sets where the structure of the whole is reflected in the structure of their
parts, no matter how small the parts are. Very likely, there are no fractals in nature,
but that doesn’t mean that fractals are useless in describing the chaotic dynamics of
physical systems.
● Anyone who has taken a physics course at any level has relied on idealization as
well: there are no frictionless surfaces in our daily lives, but friction can sometimes
be ignored (idealized away) in practical problem-solving (and sometimes it can’t –
but that’s irrelevant here).
● Physicians use arithmetic in measuring the course of a patient’s fever even though
temperatures are always rough averages for fever activity.
● Engineers often use linear approximations for non-linear phenomena.
Just as we make allowances for physicists’, carpenters’, physicians’ and engineers’ uses of
false idealizations we should make similar allowances for psychologists’ use of interpreted
formal systems (i.e., finite automata). There will be an overlap between the finite automaton
used to model Kripke and some Turing machine that can multiply: the finite automaton can
be thought of as an initial, finite segment of the Turing machine.
Rejoinder 1: “But there isn’t just one Turing machine of which the finite automaton
is the initial segment. There are infinitely many such Turing machines, different in
213 https://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/catalog/NCSU605098

313

Machine Functionalism
infinitely many other ways. Machine Functionalism has no way of choosing one (or
one sub-class) of those infinitely many false idealizations as the preferred model of
me.”
Which of the infinitely many false idealizations should be used to describe human mental lives? Machine Functionalism suffers an “embarrassment of riches.” It’s as if someone claimed that geometry is useful in house building even though no house has the
very same shape as any geometrical object, and you could never figure out which of
any infinitely many geometrical shapes the building is best described as having. This
sort of radical indeterminacy would show that Machine Functionalism is not even a
good model of the human mind.
Kripke favors and has argued for Conceptual Dualism (if not Substance Dualism), so
he needn’t worry about Machine Functionalism’s failure. But functionalism is still
popular with many cognitive scientists. Is there any strategy that they could use to
defend functionalism against Kripke’s refutation?
Reply 2: There are computers and calculator apps that we use to do lots of arithmetic, multiplication included. Many of us rely on such finite automata because we find them more reliable than we are at doing multiplication. No reasonable person would doubt that calculator
apps can best be modeled as doing multiplication. So, there’s no real problem for Machine
Functionalism raised by Kripke’s argument because we already “solve the problem in practice.”
Rejoinder 2: Reply 2 misses the point. It is not effective as a response to Kripke’s
refutation of Machine Functionalism because calculators lack original intentionality.
They have merely derivative intentionality because human beings design and use
them for doing arithmetic. Kripke’s refutation of Machine Functionalism targets Machine Functionalism’s account of the original intentionality of human beings.
Reply 3: If there is to be any hope of rescuing Machine Functionalism from Kripke’s objection, there must be some principled way of narrowing the range of false idealizations. Some
defenders of Machine Functionalism suggest asking, "What purpose does a person have in
describing this human brain as multiplying 3 and 2 to get 6?" thus recommending the “design
stance.”
Design-Stance Functionalism says that although calculator apps, as implementations of finite
automata, can't really multiply, we can describe them as doing so because that's useful, given
our purposes, in explaining their behavior. The same strategy should apply, says DesignStance Functionalism, to our descriptions of human beings who are, on their view, sophisticated calculators.
Rejoinder 3: There is a weak analogy between Design-Stance Functionalism and each of:
● Theological Functionalism – in Design-Stance Functionalism, it’s as if each of us
played the role of a mind-designing minor deity.
● Evolutionary Functionalism – in Design-Stance Functionalism, each designer is
herself the product of evolution.
Theological Functionalism and Evolutionary Functionalism are highly questionable.
These analogies should worry us.214
214 Purpose Directed Functionalism ≠ Design-Stance Functionalism. Design-Stance Functionalism applies a
basic concept of purpose to entire human beings - the ones who attribute mental states - rather than small
portions of human brain circuitry. Purpose Directed Functionalism applies a basic concept of purpose to small

314

Machine Functionalism
But there is a deeper question about Design-Stance Functionalism: Why would the mental state that you are in – the thoughts you are thinking, the sensations you are having, the
emotions you are feeling – depend (in the way required by Design-Stance Functionalism)
on the reasons someone else had in describing your mental life?! Could someone else literally change your mind for you – perhaps from a distance?!?
Jill: ‘Help me out here. I can’t shake the feeling that eating this entire chocolate cake now
would be a great idea.”
Jack (Skype-ing from 1000 miles away): “Glad to oblige – in line with my purposes, I
hereby attribute to you thinking that spaghetti should be used as a replacement for surgical scalpels.”
Jill: “Thanks! [eats entire cake] Well, I must be going now – I’m due in the operating room
to remove my patient's brain tumor. I hope they’ve sterilized the spaghetti.”

Perhaps there are some constraints – meta-purposes? – on the range of purposes that are
allowable according to Design-Stance Functionalism. (Haugeland gives some hints, but
no more than hints.) And perhaps the Tooth Fairy will repay your student loans. One can
always hope. But hoping does not yield a good theory of mind. Design-Stance Functionalism doesn’t deserve its own row in the Mind Matrix.
The investigation in this book leaves us with no compelling reason to think that there is any
fundamental difference between religion and science. We therefore find ourselves on a level
playing field where it would make as much sense to criticize a proposal or practice as either
pseudoreligious or pseudoscientific.
So, the next time someone offers you an explanation, no matter what the subject (e.g., "Here's
why you should live your life in this way ...."), don't waste your time finding out whether its
origin has been labelled “religious” or “scientific,” because those labels carry little if any
information that will help you to evaluate the explanation. Instead, evaluate the explanation
on its own merits, using the known, universal criteria that embody the highest reasonable
standards of thought. Why settle for less?

portions of human brain circuitry to explain the mental states of entire human beings, their purposes among
the mental states.

Some Common Mistakes made about Exam #3 material
Dualism
•

Aside from some religious beliefs, there’s no good reason to take mind-body dualism seriously.
o No, there’s Descartes’ Argument for the Distinctness of Mind and Body

•

If two things interact causally, then they must come into direct contact.
o No, causal interaction may be mediated by an unbroken chain of direct contacts.

•

“Non-physical” means: not solid.
o No, “non-physical” means: necessarily, takes up no space.

•

Someday, physics will discover how mind-body causal interaction occurs.
o No, physics can’t discover what’s logically impossible.

•

The Problem of Other Minds says that according to dualism, “there are no minds other than mine.”
o No, it says “no one can know ‘there’s another mind’.”

•

“I take dualism on faith, so I have no good reason to defend it against any objections.” (“blind faith
reaction”)
o No, the blind faith reaction is very costly because it treats important moral beliefs as weightless and worthless.

•

‘Identical’ twins have exactly the same characteristics.
o No, ‘identical’ twins are merely genetically very similar.

•

MCPI4 says that it’s impossible for a person to be ‘duplicated’.
317

Mind Matrix
o

No, it says that if (as is possible) a person were duplicated, then the original person would
cease to exist and two new, non-identical but exactly similar persons would ‘replace’ the
original.

Behaviorism
•

Any disposition that defines a mental state must be exhibited in behavior for the person to be in that
mental state.
o No, the dispositions of things never need to be exhibited – but they still have them.
• Behavioral dispositions that define mental states are simple: one stimulus, one response.
o No, behavioral dispositions that define mental states are extremely complex; their specifications must say under exactly what circumstances the disposition would be exhibited.
• The Waldo-strings in the memory of the Unthinking Waldo Simulator are the sentences that it responds with.
o No, each Waldo-string is a possible hour-long conversation (hour-long sequence of sentences) that could occur in talking with Mr. Waldo.
Machine Functionalism
•

•

All formal systems are formally equivalent.
o No, there are infinitely many formal systems. (Formal systems are like possible “wiring diagrams” that say how basic components are connected to one another.)
According to Machine Functionalism, every interpreted formal system defines some mental state.
o No, the converse is claimed by Machine Functionalism: every mental state is definable as
some interpreted formal system.

•

If Machine Functionalism and Behaviorism are false, then Dualism is true.
o No, there are other possible theories of mind, some not functionalist and not dualist.
School Board Problems
•

Everyone knows that there’s a big difference between science and religion. [‘Gilkey’s Arrogance’]
o No, none of the reasons offered by US courts and none of the other reasons commonly given
yields a correct separation principle.

•

No belief accepted on faith needs any defense.
o No, if a belief has any possible influence on behavior that would be morally right or morally
wrong, then its acceptance on faith needs to be defended, especially given that there are infinitely many things that could be accepted on faith. (You’re not entitled to your opinion unless perhaps your opinion has no weight.)

318

School Board Problems
School Board Problems
"Our cases interpreting and applying the [religious] purpose test [to legislation] have
made such a maze of the Establishment Clause that even the most conscientious
governmental officials can only guess what motives will be held unconstitutional."
Justice Antonin Scalia, Dissent in Edwards v. Aguillard 107 S. Ct. 2573

Congratulations to all of you on your election to the School Board! I'm the attorney for the
school system, and I'd like to welcome you to what I'm sure will be an interesting first
meeting. I was delighted to learn that you've all just completed a course of study of Abusing
Purpose, and you've been re-examining your views on religion, science and public education.
You'll certainly need a clear head on these matters because we have several petitions from
parents and employees that concern these matters directly. That's why Superintendent
Boinkles, who's away at a three-week conference in Belize, has asked me to attend this
meeting.
Before we get down to business, I thought that you might enjoy reading over a couple of court
decisions that talk about the definition of religion in our legal system. I'll just pass them out,
and you can read them over after the meeting.
[As a courtesy to the reader of these minutes, excerpts from these decisions are appended.]
Let's get started with the business of this meeting. The first petition concerns prayer in the
schools. It has verified signatures from 95% of the parents in the district, and asks that each
day, each class begin with recitation of a non-denominational prayer asking the Supreme
Being for guidance in the day's activities. I think that we can deal with this quickly. The US
Supreme Court has ruled that such an activity is a clear breach of the constitutional doctrine
of separation of church and state. So you can say to the parents and voters that, however
much you would have liked to comply with their well-intentioned wishes, the inevitable law
suit would be very expensive and the district would certainly lose. Is that alright with all of
you? Yes? Good.
Is there a question? Could we rewrite the prayer to delete reference to "the Supreme Being"
to accommodate the polytheists among us? I suppose so, but then there are the animists, the
secular humanists, the atheists, and the Extreme Physicalist Reductionists (some of whom
feel very strongly about the dispensability of purpose-directed explanation - I've seen their
protest marches). If we dilute the prayer enough to satisfy everyone, we'd be reduced to
something like, "We ask guidance (or we don't) from some purposive being or beings (or
not)," and I'm not sure that such an invocation would contribute much to the development
of students’ moral characters. Arguably, it'd be a waste of school time. Can we move on now?
Our second petition is submitted by 5% of the parents and science teachers. They want
included in every science course that discusses evolutionary theory a theory they call
"Scientific Creationism," according to which the universe was created suddenly out of
nothing; there has been no change in the kinds of living things on Earth since creation; there
was a Great Flood that ordered the fossil record; and the universe is less than 20,000 years
old. This is a pretty easy case, too, because the US Supreme Court has found this is nothing
but religion masquerading as science and so violates the constitutional doctrine of

319

School Board Problems
separation of church and state. Same story for the voters - though there are far fewer voters
to worry about in this instance.
Our third petition presents a novel situation, on which the law and court cases give us far
less guidance. About half of the parents and science teachers want the science curriculum,
including, of course, biology courses where evolutionary theory is discussed, to cover the
similarities and differences between purpose-directed explanation as it is deployed in
Satanic Science, in Arguments from Design for the existence of deities and in purposedirected explanation as it is deployed in biology, psychology and physics. The other half say
this is smuggling religion into the science curriculum and that it will confuse students who
are already having a tough time with the material they already have to cover. For a short
time, there seemed to be a compromise in the making when a rather idealistic parent
suggested that what should be avoided throughout education is indoctrination. The courts
make it clear that government indoctrination of children in the tenets of a particular religion
is against the law. But some teachers and parents pointed out that public schools feel an
obligation to indoctrinate young children in the values of American democracy, and, even
more obviously, in the truths of arithmetic. (It didn't help to point out that the latter are
necessary, as opposed to contingent, truths, because many theists regard their fundamental
beliefs, for example, that God exists, as necessary truths, too.) They've tried talking it out and
coming to some other compromise, but with no success, and plenty of residual bad-feeling
on both sides. One of the opponents to this expansion of the science curriculum claimed that
the view of the Benighted Half, as he uncharitably called them, has all the marks of
pseudoscience. I'll read you his prepared statement, on which he obviously spent a lot of
time.
What the members of the Benighted Half are trying to force on us in the Science
Department is a system of statements and practices makes reference to uncontrolled,
unobserved things, encourages selective expression of emotion, expresses an attitude of
awe and wonder towards Nature, seeks to discover some moral truths, is based partly on
faith, explains in terms of purposes and intentions, originated in ancient times, contains
vague terms, focuses on phenomena for which there are no fully understood physical
mechanisms, and has been misused by many evil people who were also utterly dogmatic
in their attitudes towards it.

I'm sure that you realize that given this much information and no more, what's being
described could be a respectable psychological theory, a mainline religion or
pseudoscientific, pseudoreligious drivel. So even if all this is an apt description of the
proposed curricular changes, it doesn't advance the debate.
Until I re-studied Justice Scalia's dissent, I thought that one of the Supreme Court decisions,
Edwards v. Aguillard, might also guide us with this third petition. Having studied his dissent,
I'm not so sure. The majority opinion was the one I relied on in advising you on the second
petition, and our second situation is close enough to the one the court actually considered
that I remain confident of my advice. But the third petition seems to present a significantly
different situation because the role of religion in it is less prominent, so far as I can tell. One
of the questions that Justice Scalia raised in my mind is, Just how deeply do we have to look
into the motives of the petitioners? And anyway, why should their motives be relevant? No
one - certainly not the courts - objects to teaching our children about, or having the
320

School Board Problems
government act on, (for example) religiously inspired Abolitionist objections to slavery. Nor
is it always against the rules for the government to help religion out; for example, Justice
Scalia writes,
… few would contend that Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, which both forbids
religious discrimination by private-sector employers ... and requires them reasonably to
accommodate the religious practices of their employees, ... violates the Establishment
Clause, even though its 'purpose' is, of course, to advance religion, and even though it is
almost certainly not required by the Free Exercise Clause.

To make things even more difficult, consider this explicit contrast between Scientific
Creationism, the focus of Edwards v. Aguillard, and "Intelligent Design theory," as some call
one alternative:
... the ... content of design theory differs significantly from that of scientific creationism.
Scientific creationism is committed to the following propositions: ...
1. There was a sudden creation of the universe, energy, and life from nothing.
2. Mutations and natural selection are insufficient to bring about the development
of all living kinds from a single organism.
3. Changes of the originally created kinds of plants and animals occur only within
fixed limits.
4. There is a separate ancestry for humans and apes.
5. The earth's geology can be explained via catastrophism, primarily by the
occurrence of a worldwide flood.
6. The earth and living kinds had a relatively recent inception (on the order of ten
thousand years).
These six tenets taken jointly define scientific creationism for legal purposes. The Court
in Edwards ruled that taken jointly this group of propositions may not be taught in public
school science classrooms. ...
Design theory, on the other hand, asserts the following:
1. High information content (or specified complexity) and irreducible complexity
constitute strong indicators or hallmarks of past intelligent design.
2. Biological systems have a high information content (or specified complexity) and
utilize subsystems that manifest irreducible complexity.
3. Naturalistic mechanisms or undirected causes do not suffice to explain the origin
of information (specified complexity) or irreducible complexity.
4. Therefore, intelligent design constitutes the best explanation for the origin of
information and irreducible complexity in biological systems.
A comparison of these two lists demonstrates clearly that design theory and scientific
creationism differ markedly in content. Clearly, then, they do not derive from the same
source.
from: David K. DeWolf, Stephen C. Meyer, Mark E. DeForrest, "Intelligent Design in Public
School Science Curricula: A Legal Guidebook."

So this hot potato lands in the Board's collective lap. I'm really looking forward to your wise
counsel on this one. Maybe you shouldn't wait to read those decisions that I passed out at
the beginning of our meeting. Then again, maybe we should ignore them.
No, the Board doesn't usually take a break this early in its meetings. Yes, some meetings have
run all night.
