School Board Problems
Appendix: Two Court Decisions on Defining Religion, with Commentary

I. Smith v. Board of School Commissioners of Mobile County 1987 (US District Court for the
Southern District of Alabama, Southern Division 655 F.Supp. 939)
Judge Brevard Hand on religion and secular humanism:
For first amendment purposes, the commitment of humanists to a non-supernatural and
non-transcendent analysis, even to the point of hostility towards and outright attacks on
all theistic religions, prevents them from maintaining the fiction that this is a non-religious
discipline. This Court is concerned with the logic and consistency, the rationality, one
might say, of Dr. Kurtz's contention that secular humanism is not a religious system, but
science. Secular humanism is religious for first amendment purposes because it makes
statements based on faith-assumptions. To say that science is only concerned with data
collected by the five senses as enhanced by technological devices of man's creation is to
define science's limits. These are the parameters within which scientists function.
However, to claim that there is nothing real beyond observable data is to make an
assumption based not on science, but on faith, faith that observable data is all that is real.
A statement that there is no transcendent or supernatural reality is a religious statement.
A statement that there is no scientific proof of supernatural or transcendent reality is
irrelevant and nonsensical, because inquiry into the fundamental nature of man and
reality itself may not be confined solely within the sphere of physical, tangible, observable
science. [NOTE: The fact that such inquiry can be, and often is, conducted by systematic
and quantifiable methods does not make the inquiry itself one belonging to the physical
sciences.]
To demand that there be physical proof of the supernatural, and to claim that an apparent
lack of proof means the supernatural cannot be accepted, is to create a religious creed. It
is not scientific to say that because there is no physical proof of the supernatural, we must
base moral theories on disbelief and skepticism. [NOTE: In particular, morals are
inevitably based on theories of man's nature and ideas about his purpose and destiny. As
science does not inquire whether man has a supernatural nature or not, the foundation
for a moral theory cannot be represented as having a scientific basis. Therefore, it is
specious and false to represent as a scientific and non-religious statement the claim that
"morals must not be based on supernatural beliefs."] If there is no evidence, the theory,
one way or the other, has nothing to do with science. Religious persons can and do
conduct rational and systematic debate on matters of faith. The physical sciences do not
preclude religion and religious faith. They examine other areas of inquiry, and are
unconcerned, yet compatible with, religious inquiry. [NOTE: Dr. Charles Rudder, a witness
proffered by the State Board of Education testified that it was not rationally possible to
"defend the claim that on the basis of what we know about the natural, we know anything
about the supernatural or can draw any conclusions one way or another about the
supernatural. It would appear to me that if the supernatural exists, we can be informed
about the natural from the supernatural, but we can't inform ourselves about the
supernatural from the natural....] The Court is holding that the promotion and
advancement of a religious system occurs when one faith-theory is taught to the exclusion
of others and this is prohibited by the first amendment religion clauses.
Dr. Kurtz's testimony that secular humanism has no religious aspect is not logical. For
purposes of the first amendment, secular humanism is a religious belief system, entitled

322

School Board Problems
to the protections of, and subject to the prohibitions of, the religion clauses. It is not a
mere scientific methodology that may be promoted and advanced in the public schools.

(The US Court of Appeals for the 11th Circuit, 827 F.2d 684, disagrees with Hand's
characterization of secular humanism as a religion.)
Judge Hand is not unusual in his reliance on the notion of the supernatural. The term "supernatural" is used by Judge Jones in Kitzmiller v Dover (and in precedents that he cites) in arguing that the relevant kind of Creationism is not science, but religion masquerading as science. (And there was Gilkey’s ill-fated use of the term.) It is often unclear however what the
term "supernatural" means in these decisions and in other critical discussions.
One meaning that seems to be attached to it is:
"supernatural" means, by definition: is nonphysical; acts on its purposes; and causes
changes in physical things.
God, the supreme being, would be supernatural in the sense just defined. At least some court
US court decisions then appear to argue as follows:
1. Creationism (of the relevant kind) explains using supernatural causes.
2. Any explanation that uses supernatural causes is not testable.
3. All scientific explanations must be testable.
4. All explanations that are not scientific are religious.
5. Therefore, creationism is religious and not scientific.
In his criticism of Judge Jones’ decision, Plantinga argued against this use of ‘supernatural’.
“Separation by Immaterial Causation” gave a different argument against the use of supernatural’. Can these arguments be evaded by substituting a different notion for ‘supernatural’? In
a recent book215, sociologist Christian Smith offers a definition of "religion" that is somewhat
novel:
Religion is ...best defined as a complex of culturally prescribed practices that are based on premises about the existence and nature of superhuman powers. These powers may be personal or
impersonal, but they are always superhuman in the dual sense that they can do things humans
cannot do and that they do not depend for their existence on human activities. Religious people
engage in complexes of practices in order to gain access to and communicate or align themselves
with these superhuman powers. ... Key to this definition is ... emphasis on ... superhuman powers,
which distinguish it from other approaches that focus on ... the supernatural, sacred, transcendent, divine, or ultimate concern (rather than superhuman powers). This emphasis helps to avoid
problems that plague other ...[definitions] of religion." (page 3, emphasis added)

In the remaining 270 pages of his book, Smith provides a lot of necessary detail, but the passage just quoted is an accurate summary. Smith makes it clear in the rest of his book that
superhuman is a far broader notion than supernatural, so that whatever is supernatural is
superhuman, but not vice versa. As reported below in "Evolution of Opinion about Evolution:
US and NC State 1982-2017," research in psychology has shown that it is common for members of the main Western, monotheistic religious traditions (Christianity, Islam, Judaism) to
think of God not as their theologians do but as a superhuman being; that is, it is common for
these people to regard God not as supernatural but as superhuman in Christian Smith's sense.
So, there is some empirical evidence for Smith's novel definition.
Suppose that US courts were to adopt Smith's definition of religion instead of the
215 Religion: What It Is, How It Works, and Why It Matters (Princeton University Press, 2017), https://cata-

log.lib.ncsu.edu/trln/DUKE008165435 .

323

School Board Problems
characterization implied by the argument given above ("1. Creationism (of the relevant kind)
explains using supernatural causes.," etc.). What effect would that have on the attempt to
show that Creationism (of the relevant kind) is religious and not scientific?
Smith’s characterization of superhuman as opposed to supernatural would seem to imply
that belief in Santa Claus, Superman or the Marvel Comics superheroes would be religious
belief. It would thus not be plausible to assume, "any explanation that uses superhuman
causes is not testable" and would thus make it far more difficult for the US courts to find that
religious beliefs are not scientific. And it would be misleading to characterize the relevant
kinds of Creationism as holding that there are superhuman powers that created and shaped
the universe, because it would omit the divine nature of the creator, as specified in the attributes of incorporeality, omnipotence, omniscience, etc. So, US courts are unlikely to adopt
Smith’s suggestion, whatever its other merits may be.
II. US v. David Meyers (US Court of Appeals for the 10th Circuit 1996 U.S. App. LEXIS 23363
September 6, 1996, Filed)
There is no dispute that Meyers' beliefs are sincerely held and that they are substantially
burdened by 21 U.S.C. @@ 841 and 846 and 18 U.S.C. @ 2. The issue is whether his
sincerely held beliefs are "religious beliefs," rather than a philosophy or way of life. In
analyzing this issue, the district court examined the cases that have delved into the
question of "what is religion" and catalogued the many factors used to determine
whether a set of beliefs is religious in nature. [NOTE omitted] Meyers, 906 F. Supp. at
1501. The court then used its list of factors to examine Meyers' beliefs to determine if his
beliefs fit the factors sufficiently to be included in the realm of "religious beliefs
Keeping in mind that the threshold for establishing the religious nature of his beliefs is
low, the court considered the following factors:
1. Ultimate Ideas: Religious beliefs often address fundamental questions about life,
purpose, and death. As one court has put it, "a religion addresses fundamental and
ultimate questions having to do with deep and imponderable matters." .... These matters
may include existential matters, such as man's sense of being; teleological matters, such
as man's purpose in life; and cosmological matters, such as man's place in the universe.
[See: Separation by Explanation, Separation by Fact and Value, discussion of Gilkey's
criticism of Scientific Creationism]
2. Metaphysical Beliefs: Religious beliefs often are "metaphysical," that is, they address a
reality which transcends the physical and immediately apparent world. Adherents to
many religions believe that there is another dimension, place, mode, or temporality, and
they often believe that these places are inhabited by spirits, souls, forces, deities, and
other sorts of inchoate or intangible entities. [See: Separation by Immaterial Causation,
Separation by Explanation, discussion of Gilkey's criticism of Scientific Creationism]
3. Moral or Ethical System: Religious beliefs often prescribe a particular manner of acting,
or way of life, that is "moral" or "ethical." In other words, these beliefs often describe
certain acts in normative terms, such as "right and wrong," "good and evil," or "just and
unjust." The beliefs then proscribe those acts that are "wrong," "evil," or "unjust." A moral
or ethical belief structure also may create duties -- duties often imposed by some higher
power, force, or spirit -- that require the believer to abnegate elemental self-interest.
[See: Separation by Fact and Value]
4. Comprehensiveness of Beliefs: Another hallmark of "religious" ideas is that they are
comprehensive. More often than not, such beliefs provide a telos, an overreaching array
of beliefs that coalesce to provide the believer with answers to many, if not most, of the
324

School Board Problems
problems and concerns that confront humans. In other words, religious beliefs generally
are not confined to one question or a single teaching. ... [See Separation by Explanation]
5. Accoutrements of Religion: By analogy to many of the established or recognized
religions, the presence of the following external signs may indicate that a particular set of
beliefs is "religious":
a. Founder, Prophet, or Teacher: Many religions have been wholly founded or significantly
influenced by a deity, teacher, seer, or prophet who is considered to be divine,
enlightened, gifted, or blessed.
b. Important Writings: Most religions embrace seminal, elemental, fundamental, or
sacred writings. These writing often include creeds, tenets, precepts, parables,
commandments, prayers, scriptures, catechisms, chants, rites, or mantras.
c. Gathering Places: Many religions designate particular structures or places as sacred,
holy, or significant. These sites often serve as gathering places for believers. They include
physical structures, such as churches, mosques, temples, pyramids, synagogues, or
shrines; and natural places, such as springs, rivers, forests, plains, or mountains.
d. Keepers of Knowledge: Most religions have clergy, ministers, priests, reverends, monks,
shamans, teachers, or sages. By virtue of their enlightenment, experience, education, or
training, these people are keepers and purveyors of religious knowledge.
e. Ceremonies and Rituals: Most religions include some form of ceremony, ritual, liturgy,
sacrament, or protocol. These acts, statements, and movements are prescribed by the
religion and are imbued with transcendent significance.
f. Structure or Organization: Many religions have a congregation or group of believers
who are led, supervised, or counselled by a hierarchy of teachers, clergy, sages, priests,
etc.
g. Holidays: As is etymologically evident, many religions celebrate, observe, or mark
"holy," sacred, or important days, weeks, or months.
h. Diet or Fasting: Religions often prescribe or prohibit the eating of certain foods and the
drinking of certain liquids on particular days or during particular times.
i. Appearance and Clothing: Some religions prescribe the manner in which believers
should maintain their physical appearance, and other religions prescribe the type of
clothing that believers should wear.
j. Propagation: Most religious groups, thinking that they have something worthwhile or
essential to offer non-believers, attempt to propagate their views and persuade others of
their correctness. This is sometimes called "mission work," "witnessing," "converting," or
proselytizing. ... [See Separation by Personal Improvement]
The district court emphasized that "it cannot rely solely on established or recognized
religions to guide it in determining whether a new and unique set of beliefs warrants
inclusion" and that "no one of these factors is dispositive, and that the factors should be
seen as criteria that, if minimally satisfied, counsel the inclusion of beliefs within the term
'religion.'" ... However, in accord with Yoder, the court noted that "purely personal,
political, ideological, or secular beliefs probably would not satisfy enough criteria for
inclusion." Id. at 1504. See Yoder, 406 U.S. at 216 (philosophical and personal beliefs are
secular beliefs); Africa, 662 F.2d at 1036 (finding beliefs are secular not religious); Berman,
156 F.2d 377 at 380-81 (beliefs which are moral and social are not religious); Church of
the Chosen People, 548 F. Supp. at 1253 (beliefs which are sexual and secular are not
religious).

325

School Board Problems
After carefully examining Meyers' beliefs derived from his testimony, the district court
concluded that his beliefs were secular and, thus, did not constitute a "religion" for ...
[Religious Freedom Restoration Act] purposes. ... The court concluded that:
Marijuana's medical, therapeutic, and social effects are secular, not religious. . . . Here,
the Court cannot give Meyers' "religious" beliefs much weight because those beliefs
appear to be derived entirely from his secular beliefs. In other words, Meyers' secular and
religious beliefs overlap only in the sense that Meyers holds secular beliefs which he
believes so deeply that he has transformed them into a "religion."
While Meyers may sincerely believe that his beliefs are religious, this Court cannot rely
on his sincerity to conclude that his beliefs rise to the level of a "religion" and therefore
trigger ... [the Religious Freedom Restoration Act's] protections. Meyers is, of course,
absolutely free to think or believe what he wants. If he thinks that his beliefs are a religion,
then so be it. No one can restrict his beliefs, and no one should begrudge him those
beliefs. None of this, however, changes the fact that his beliefs do not constitute a
"religion" as that term is uneasily defined by law. Were the Court to recognize Meyers'
beliefs as religious, it might soon find itself on a slippery slope where anyone who was
cured of an ailment by a "medicine" that had pleasant side-effects could claim that they
had founded a constitutionally or statutorily protected religion based on the beneficial
"medicine." ... Finally, the court noted that "Meyers' professed beliefs have an ad hoc
quality that neatly justify his desire to smoke marijuana." ...
We agree with the district court. Under the district court's thorough analysis of the indicia
of religion, which we adopt, we hold that Meyers' beliefs more accurately espouse a
philosophy and/or way of life rather than a "religion." The district court did not err in
prohibiting Meyers' religious freedom defense.

In Chapter 7, “The Definition of Religion,” of Boris I. Bittker, Scott C. Idleman, and Frank S.
Ravitch Religion and the state in American law (Cambridge University Press, 2015), a standard law school textbook, the authors write,
As it turns out, "religion" appears to be one of the few key terms of the First Amendment, if not
the only key term of that provision, that the contemporary Supreme Court has not authoritatively
defined in a comprehensive or systematic manner. … Even among the multitude of other Article
III judges past and present, only a modest number have ventured into this definitional abyss and
yielded substantial (and sometimes quite different) definitions. … The lack of an authoritative and
meaningful definition of "religion" - a key constitutional term that has been part of extensive litigation for decades - is, as suggested earlier, rather anomalous.

They then outline some criteria that have been used to guide the search for a definition:
1. Original Intent or Meaning - what the authors of the US Constitution had in mind when
they used the word “religion” in writing the First Amendment;
2. Comparative Similarity –features that the most widely accepted examples of religion have
in common;
3. Functional Equivalence – “focuses … [on a] belief[‘s] … actual role or function in the life of
an adherent and specifically whether it functions with a depth, intensity, or totality often
associated with genuine religious devotion.” “The fundamental focus is … how meaningfully
the beliefs shape and direct the adherent's life as opposed to what they proclaim or how
they. are externally manifested.”
326

School Board Problems
4. Subjective Characterization – “…as long as … beliefs are sincerely held and are, in the …
[believers] '''own scheme of things, religious [then the beliefs are religious] the requirement of sincerity can become the pivotal, if not exclusive, inquiry in the analysis, with the
element of religiousness turning essentially on the [… believer’s] characterization of his or
her own beliefs.”
On original intent:
As Professor Daniel Conkle has observed, "for the Founders and for generations that followed,
the defining characteristic of religion was easily stated and utterly uncontroversial: it was the performance of duties owed to God. This understanding encompassed not only the various strands
of Protestant Christianity, but also other traditional religions, including Catholic Christianity and
Judaism.”

But by the mid-20th century, this relatively narrow definition had been abandoned by the
courts to recognize Buddhism, Taoism, various forms of paganism, Ethical Culture and Secular Humanism as religious.
United States v. Meyers exemplifies the comparative similarity approach, which the authors
of the text favor most among the four above (and which resembles Prothero’s characterization from God is Not One).
The functional approach was taken by courts in allowing conscientious objector status to
registrants for the military draft who rejected conventional religious bases for pacifism.
About the subjective characterization, Bittker et al say,
Were this not an approach potentially embraced by a growing number of courts, it might not
deserve mention in a chapter on defining religion if only because, in a sense, it is not an effort to
define religion.

By giving sincerity so much weight that it is not only necessary but nearly sufficient, the First
Amendment could be “exploited” by anyone “trying to elude the strictures of nonestablishment,” the authors say.
The authors final suggestion is to improve the comparative similarity in the following five
ways:
First, the Meyers framework presumably should be enhanced, in a balanced way, with more …
functionalism …. Second, whatever approach is developed, it should … [give “religion” about the
same meaning in both] the Free Exercise and Establishment Clauses. Third, it should be assessed
using a variety of real and hypothetical test cases, such as the lists of included and excluded beliefs
found in Meyers. … the lists it provides seem not to have been constructed through a rigorous use
of the factors, and it was not always obvious why certain items were on one list rather than the
other. Fourth, … [stipulate] that no single factor should be deemed dispositive. [Fifth] … the factors probably should not remain unranked or ungrouped in terms of importance … and there
should be some indication as to how many factors ordinarily will be sufficient to qualify under the
definition.

327

School Board Problems
Aside from its rejection of the subjective characterization, this is not much more helpful than
United States v. Meyers in arriving at a workable definition of religion.216
It does not even attempt to address the deeper question about who or what is authoritative
about defining religion.
The answer cannot be: let the courts stipulate a definition of religion. The courts are no freer
simply to stipulate a definition of religion than they are free simply to stipulate a definition
of automobile or magnesium or chair, although the courts might reasonably be called upon
to tidy up those concepts in practically relevant ways for legal purposes. All those concepts
suffer from some vagueness in potentially legally relevant ways and drawing some conceptual boundaries in a way that allows application of laws is reasonable. (“I agreed to buy a
chair and the store sent me a sword, claiming that because someone could sit on it, the sword
was a chair. Well not just anything you can sit on is a chair! I want my money back! I’m suing.”) Simply stipulating a definition is not reasonable (“All chairs must have exactly four legs
and be made out of wood” would take care of some cases but decide many others incorrectly.)
Those who spend the most time and expend the most effort studying religions are of course
Religious Studies researchers! It might therefore be a good idea to see what they have to say
about defining religion. What they say does not support optimism about the prospects for a
definition.
At most, religious studies scholars would agree to a weakened version of the comparative
similarity approach, and they appear to allow for much greater emphasis on subjective factors than do Bittker et al. Notice that the criticism of subjective characterization by Bittker
et al said little more than: it won’t allow us to define religion in the way that we think is
needed for legal purposes. But that doesn’t show that the approach is incorrect. Winnifred
Fallers Sullivan is unusual among researchers in Religious Studies because she is also an attorney who has served as an expert witness in federal cases that raise the question of how
to define religion. She argues forcefully and persuasively217 that giving heavy weight to a
subjective component together with a functional characterization may be the only way to
give appropriate recognition to religious beliefs and practices that are unconventional and
not covered by the original meaning of “religion.” Peter B. Clarke and Peter Byrne argue218
that religion is best regarded as a family resemblance concept (as Prothero also suggests),
which would make finding anything like a clear boundary for the concept of religion impossible. Timothy Fitzgerald219 goes further and argues that there are no pervasive and stable
similarities that religions share and that it is only ignorance of religious history and a lack of
imagination about possible future developments that allow us to kid ourselves into thinking
216 For a very up to date review of the case law and legal scholarship, see Courtney Miller, “’Spiritual but Not

Religious’: Rethinking the Legal Definition of Religion,” Virginia Law Review v102 (2016) 833-894. It begins by
discussing the case of spiritual counselor ‘Psychic Sophie,’ Moore-King v. County of Chesterfield 708 F.3d 560,
564, 571 (4th Cir. 2013). Miller also favors something like the approach in United States v. Meyers.
217 The Impossibility of Religious Freedom (Princeton University Press, 2005)
http://search.trln.org/search?id=UNCb4736782
218 Religion Defined and Explained (St. Martin's Press, 1993) http://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/record/NCSU925650
219 The Ideology of Religious Studies (Oxford University Press, 2000) ebook
http://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/record/NCSU3584511
328

School Board Problems
that religions form a loose “family association” with identifiable resemblances. The brilliant
and influential Jonathan Z. Smith argued that the concept of religion was a 19th century invention:
...while there is a staggering amount of data, phenomena, of human experiences and expressions
that might be characterized in one culture or another, by one criterion or another, as religion —
there is no data for religion. Religion is solely the creation of the scholar’s study. It is created for
the scholar’s analytic purposes by his imaginative acts of comparison and generalization. Religion
has no existence apart from the academy.220

More recently Brian Leiter has argued221 that religion is definable in a way that makes it clear
that religious beliefs deserve no special legal protection, though he does agree that First
Amendment protections for many deeply held beliefs, religious or otherwise, are important
to retain. His four-part definition is roughly this:
For all religions, there are at least some beliefs central to the religion that
1. issue in categorical demands on action – that is, demands that must be satisfied no matter what
an individual’s antecedent desires and no matter what incentives or disincentives the world offers up; and
2. do not answer ultimately (or at the limit) to evidence and reasons, as those are understood in
other domains concerned with the world. Religious beliefs, in virtue of being based on “faith,”
are insulated from ordinary standards of evidence and rational justification, the ones we employ
in both common sense and in science. (33-34)
3. religious beliefs involve, explicitly or implicitly, a metaphysics of ultimate reality (47) where “ultimate reality is what is most important for valuable/worthwhile /desirable human lives, whether
that concerns the transcendent well-being of the “soul,” or the moral value of life in this, the
material world (48); and
4. render intelligible and tolerable the basic existential facts about human life, such as suffering
and death (“existential consolation”). (52)

All these authors are attempting to address the question, Who or what has the authority to
define religion? If religions are, or even might be, part of a “package deal” that claims to include that authority, then defining religion for legal or many other purposes may be an impossible task. And Leiter’s definition seems to suggest that religion is just such a package
deal.222
Is there then any principled basis on which to argue that, say, Pastafarianism223 is not already
a religion? And if for some reason it is not already a religion, what could stop it from becoming one?

A Model for Predicting Growth in Worldwide Coverage of Pastafarianism

A May 1, 2016, search of the Lexis/Nexis Academic database (which covers
hundreds of news sources worldwide, law journals and corporate profiles
among many other sources) for all occurrences of “Pastafarian,” “Pastafarianism,” and “Flying Spaghetti Monster” yielded the article counts per year summarized below. (For reasons that will become clear presently, only currently
available article counts are depicted in the graph.)
A variety of models, linear and non-linear, were fit to the data and the best fit
was to a model of exponential growth. That model is specified below. (Estimated confidence intervals are omitted as they are irrelevant in light of prior
confidence.)
Using the model, it can now be predicted with confidence that by the year
2050, there will be about 95,858 articles on Pastafarianism and by 2100,
there will be about 627,712,417 articles on Pastafarianism.

Some Common Misconceptions
Some Common Misconceptions
If you have read this far, then there are over twenty common, influential mistakes about science, religion and other matters that you can (and should!) avoid. Only the bold-faced items
in the list below are correct claims; the rest are false.
i.
ii.
iii.
iv.
v.
vi.
vii.
viii.
ix.
x.
xi.
xii.
xiii.

xiv.

xv.

xvi.
xvii.

During the past one hundred years, there have been some well-designed, properly
analyzed experiments that have shown that some people have some sort of 'ESP'.
Astrology reliably gives accurate predictions of daily events in individuals' lives.
Astrology typically gives accurate descriptions of peoples' characters.
Anyone who studies evolutionary theory in any way is inevitably morally corrupted
by that study.
Modern evolutionary theory attempts to explain the origin of the universe.
Modern evolutionary theory attempts to explain the origin of the first living things on
Earth.
The main and defining aim of science is to find facts.
The First Amendment to the US Constitution states the Doctrine of Separation of
Church and State, which forbids any discussion of religious beliefs in US public school
classes.
Only science tries to meet high standards in giving explanations.
Religious doctrines cannot change.
The only religions are: Christianity, Islam and Judaism.
Science must be based on observation and thus may talk about only what can be observed with the five senses: what can be seen, heard, felt, tasted or smelled.
For any person and any action, if a person believes that an action is morally right then
that action is morally right, and for any person and any action, if a person believes
that an action is not morally right then that action is not morally right. (slogan: "All
morality is ultimately and wholly determined by individuals' beliefs.”)
For any person and any action, if a culture believes that the person's performing the
action is morally right, then the person's performing the action is morally right; and
if a culture believes that the person's performing the action is morally wrong, then
the person's performing the action is morally wrong. (slogan: "Morality is ultimately
and wholly determined by a culture's beliefs.”)
For any person and any action, there are moral facts about which actions performed by the person are morally right and about which actions performed by
the person are morally wrong: and these moral facts are not ultimately and
wholly determined by individual beliefs and these moral facts are not ultimately and wholly determined by cultural beliefs. (The meaning of the word
"fact" does not imply being known. There are many facts that remain as yet undiscovered and there are many facts that will never be known by any human being.)
“I never think that something is true when it isn't really true.” [say this to yourself].
[Equivalently: "I never have any false beliefs."]
“I never think that something is true about my mind when it isn't really true about my
mind." [say this to yourself] [Equivalently: "I never have any false beliefs about my
mind."]
332

Some Common Misconceptions
xviii.

Because numbers can be referred to in many ways (e.g., base 10, base 2, base 16, Roman numerals) it's not necessarily true that the number 6 is an even number.
xix. Because numbers can be referred to in many ways (e.g., base 10, base 2, base
16, Roman numerals) it's not necessarily true that the sentence, "6 is an even
number,” expresses a truth.
xx.
In doing science, one should never accept any statements on faith or without proof.
xxi. There is some evidence that the universe is the product of design by one or
more intelligent beings.
xxii.
There is no argument or evidence for the claim that there are non-physical minds
("souls"); and thus the claim that there are non-physical minds ("souls") must be believed on faith if it is believed at all.
xxiii.
“Anything (even a computer) that always behaved as intelligently as I do, would have
to be intelligent.”
xxiv.
One of the best ways to show that an explanation is wrong is to show that its proponents are typically immoral.
You might recognize a few of these statements from exams. It’s worth reviewing the reasons
for accepting the truths (in bold) above and for rejecting the falsehoods. I’ll add some comments.
During the past one hundred years, there have been some well-designed, properly analyzed
experiments that have shown that some people have some sort of 'ESP'.
One of the problems with this claim was pointed out in “Parapsychology”: it is not clear what
“extrasensory perception” (ESP) means. When suitably clarified and used to make testable
claims, the results of well-designed and properly analyzed experiments over the past 130
years have all been null: not a single instance of confirmed ESP.
David Marks, “Investigating the Paranormal,” Nature v320 n6058 (March 13 1986)
119-124.
The reasons for the tendency of many to believe that such “paranormal phenomena” occur
are many; some of the most powerful reasons stem from the persistent tendency of even
well-trained people to reason incorrectly (and wishfully or fearfully) about the likelihoods
of events, as massively documented in:
Daniel Kahneman, Thinking, Fast and Slow (Farrar, Straus & Giroux, 2012)
DH Hill Stacks (3rd floor) BF441 .K238 2011 [NY Times Bestseller list]
https://www.nytimes.com/2011/11/27/books/review/thinking-fast-and-slow-by-daniel-kahneman-book-review.html
https://www.nytimes.com/2011/10/23/magazine/dont-blink-the-hazards-of-confidence.html

Astrology reliably gives accurate predictions of daily events in individuals' lives.
Astrology typically gives pretty accurate descriptions of peoples' characters.
“Astrology” made it clear that unless one worships the deities of ancient Greek and Roman
mythology, Zeus, Neptune, Mars, etc., then there is little reason to accept astrological theory.
And while no empirical data about tests of astrology were included in the required readings,
optional material was provided on the home page that show astrological predictions are either too vague to be tested or when testable and tested are correct no more often than random guessing.
333

Some Common Misconceptions
Sean Carlson, “A double blind test of astrology,” Nature v318 (1985) 419-425.
Sean Carlson, “Astrology,” Experientia 44 (1988) 290-297
Geoffrey Dean, “The Case for and against Astrology,” in Bryan Farha, ed., Paranormal
Claims: A Critical Analysis (UPA, 2007) pp. 115-129. ISBN 9780761837725
Anyone who studies evolutionary theory in any way is inevitably morally corrupted by that
study.
While some use this to express their rejection of evolutionary theory, what it says has little
if any plausibility. Many of the clergy who reject evolutionary theory must have studied it.
(Otherwise, their rejection would be the hot air of the ignorant and surely, they are not engaged merely in the venting of overheated gases.) Did they become moral monsters as a result of their studies? Some of those same clergy also exhort others to “know your enemy,”
where they take the enemy to be evolutionary theory. Presumably, they are not giving advice
that they think will lead inevitably to moral degradation for those who follow their advice.
Indeed, anyone who has read this book or Kitcher’s Abusing Science (or both) has studied
some evolutionary theory but doing so was surely not guaranteed to transform them into
moral monsters.
None of the following claims has much going for it either:
“Study of crime leads inevitably to commission of crimes.”
“Study of mental illness leads inevitably to mental illness.”
“Study of [bad ideas or behavior of kind K] leads inevitably to [bad ideas or behavior
of kind K].”
In many instances, study of something bad can instead lead to its avoidance, prevention or
cure. While there is such a thing as dangerous knowledge, there are few if any things the
study of which is guaranteed to have terrible consequences in thought or behavior. (Young
children and the severely cognitively challenged might be more vulnerable. But the statement does not limit itself to such highly vulnerable populations.)
Modern evolutionary theory attempts to explain the origin of the universe.
Modern evolutionary theory attempts to explain the origin of the first living things on Earth
Evolutionary theory (modern or not) seeks to explain the diversity of living things - the
Origin of the Species, as Darwin titled his most famous book, though he might, more clumsily,
have entitled it the Origin of the Diversity of Species. Natural selection in an environment,
mutation, recombination and species (defined in terms of relative reproductive isolation)
are all concepts that apply to existing life to yield new forms of life. Attempts to explain the
origin of the universe belong to the branch of physics called “cosmology.” Nothing in evolutionary theory attempts in any way to explain the origin of the entire universe.
Only science tries to meet high standards in giving explanations.
The main and defining aim of science is to find facts.
In doing science, one never accepts any statements on faith or without proof.
Science must be based on observation and thus may talk about only what can be observed
with the five senses: what can be seen, heard, felt, tasted or smelled.
“Lessons about Gatekeeping” (RSLM) gives an account of the origin of the first of these four
falsehoods, as arising from a pernicious confusion between the list and approval uses of

334

Some Common Misconceptions
“scientific.” “Separation by Cognitive Standards” (RSLM) hammers some additional nails into
the coffin.
On the second falsehood: The main and defining aim of science is to develop good theories
that explain any facts discovered. This is a theme hammered throughout the discussion of
science and pseudoscience.
(While discovering new, as yet unexplained phenomena is indeed part of science, the ultimate goal is to explain those phenomena. The latter is so important that some of astrology's
critics seem to forget about the discovery of as yet unexplained phenomena!)
The importance of explanation is reflected in criticism by science educators of the current
state of US science (mis)education:
F. James Rutherford, director of Project 2061, a science curriculum reform project of the
American Association for the Advancement of Science, laments that “much of what’s
done in schools focuses on facts and information, on separate disciplines and subject matter that is often unconnected.” The AAAS report states that “Today’s overstuffed curriculum places too much emphasis on memorizing countless formulas and generalizations,
which severely hinders students’ abilities to learn and understand materials.... students
should spend more time learning how science touches their lives and less time memorizing facts.” More and more studies are calling for hands-on methods of science instruction,
which include interdisciplinary approaches to current issues such as global warming, famines, earthquakes, volcanoes, etc.
Rutherford gives an example of good and bad science education in teaching about cells.
High school texts list 120 technical words about the cell, and students are often required
to memorize the words. “We found that 11 of the words were sufficient,” he said. “If you
concentrate on what goes on in the cell, how it relates to the system, you come out with
a deeper understanding.” The committee doesn’t want to eliminate learning facts, just
reduce the workload so time can be spent on understanding what the terms are about. It
seems, then, that unless we change the way science is taught to our young people, it is
unlikely that many of them will come away with an understanding of the nature of science, much less with a desire to scientifically understand the world they live in.
http://www.skepdic.com/skeptimedia/skeptimedia94.htm
"... if science educators, policy makers, and the public are serious about having students
actually understand a set of important science ideas (rather than simply memorizing isolated facts that will soon be forgotten), then improving textbook coherence needs to be
taken seriously."
"Science classes shouldn’t be about simply memorizing facts. What our children need to
learn are the concepts and skills that will help them to understand how the world works
and to solve problems." (Project 2061, American Association for the Advancement of Science)
http://www.project2061.org/publications/2061Connections/2010/2010-02b.htm
On faith and proof in science: As "Separation by Foundation"(RSLM) and Plantinga's "Is Belief
in God Properly Basic?" argued, any set of rational beliefs will be based on foundational
(properly basic) beliefs, and to accept a belief on faith is for it to be foundational. Because
doing science at least sometimes requires being rational, it follows that in doing science, one
335

Some Common Misconceptions
must accept some beliefs on faith, e.g., the non-CSE, foundational beliefs of Common Sense
Foundationalism.
Also, as Kitcher points out in Chapter 2 of Abusing Science, if "proof" (as contrasted with
"faith") requires certainty, then in almost all of science it is irrational even to make proof an
ultimate goal (that is, it would be a bad ideal).
“In order to be scientific, a statement must be capable of direct experimental test.” “Astrology” suggested that this common belief is false, and “Separation by Reference” explains why
it is false: One of the most successful scientific theories, The Standard Model of nuclear matter, postulates the existence of quarks, the building blocks of protons, neutrons and other
nuclear particles. The Standard Model would suffer massive predictive failure if it did not
also say that quarks are not directly observable. Several teams of experimental high-energy
particle physicists at LHC announced in late 2012 the results of experiments that provide
strong additional confirmation of The Standard Model (the discovery of the Higgs boson
http://home.web.cern.ch/about/physics/search-higgs-boson). The 2013 Nobel Prize in
Physics was awarded for this work and the theoretical developments that led to it.
X The First Amendment to the US Constitution states the Doctrine of Separation of Church
and State, which forbids any discussion of religious beliefs in US public school classes. X
Both Edwards v Aguillard and Kitzmiller v Dover explicitly permit and acknowledge the importance of discussing religious beliefs in US public school classes. What they forbid is government endorsement of religious beliefs by presenting those beliefs as scientific theories in
US public school science classes. The latter is a very different matter.
Religious doctrines cannot change.
The only religions are: Christianity, Islam and Judaism.
There is enormous diversity in the world's religions ("Separation by Reference"). Much of
that diversity is the result of changes in religious doctrines. There are different kinds of
Christianity and different kinds of Islam (not to mention different kinds of Judaism), and
Christianity shares some but certainly not all doctrines with Judaism and Islam – a kind of
diversity produced by change in religious doctrines. Even within particular religious traditions, doctrines change over time.
For any person and any action, if a person believes that an action is morally right then that
action is morally right, and for any person and any action, if a person believes that an action
is not morally right then that action is not morally right.
For any person and any action, if a culture believes that the person's performing the action
is morally right, then the person's performing the action is morally right; and if a culture
believes that the person's performing the action is morally wrong, then the person's performing the action is morally wrong.
For any person and any action, there are moral facts about which actions performed
by the person are morally right and about which actions performed by the person are
morally wrong: and these moral facts are not ultimately and wholly determined by
individual beliefs and these moral facts are not ultimately and wholly determined by
cultural beliefs.
A series of arguments against the sort of Naïve Moral Relativism expressed in the first two
false statements was given in “Separation by Fact and Value.” The bad reasons for endorsing
336

Some Common Misconceptions
Naïve Moral Relativism were neutralized – Naïve Moral Relativism is not needed to explain
the fact of disagreement about what is morally right or wrong, Naïve Moral Relativism does
nothing whatever to promote tolerance of any desirable sort, Naïve Moral Relativism leads
to contradictions, and a clear counterexample was given (Predatory Pedophile Paradise).
Many students have in past surveys endorsed both claims. But it is grossly implausible to
suppose that both can be true. There can be and in fact are frequently conflicts between individuals and the cultures of which they are members: Smith may hold a moral opinion about
what a person (P) should (not) do (A) in particular circumstances (C) that contradicts a
moral opinion accepted in her culture about what that same person should (not) do in the
same circumstances.224 Anyone who endorses both claims thereby holds that
It’s not the case that person P is not morally obligated to do x in C (because Smith’s
opinion is that person P is morally obligated to do x in C)
and also
Person P is morally obligated to do x in C (because Smith’s culture is of the opinion that
P is morally obligated to do x in C)
If the opinions really were the ultimate basis of morality, then it would follow that
P is and is not morally obligated to do x in C.
-which cannot be true.
“I never think that something is true when it isn't really true.” [Equivalently: "I never have
any false beliefs.”]
Unless you think that you are, like God is supposed to be, infallible, then you will surely grant
that a falsehood or two is among the beliefs that you have. That's to say that you have at least
once during your life been wrong about something.
In “Belief, Knowledge and Classical Self-Evidence,” it’s pointed out:
To believe a statement, S, is simply to think that S is true. (To say, “My beliefs are true
for me” is to assert the triviality, “I think that my beliefs are true;” that is: “My beliefs
are my beliefs.”)
If you read “Every statement that I believe is true” as “Every statement that I believe is true
for me” = “Every statement that I believe, I believe” [=”If I think it’s true, then I think it’s true”]
then the statement must be true, always, about everyone. But then the claim would be a triviality not worth asking about. If you did not read, “Every statement that I believe is true” as
a triviality, then it’s got to be false – unless you are divinely infallible.
“I never think that something is true about my mind when it isn't really true about my
mind." [Equivalently: "I never have any false beliefs about my mind.”]
From Separation (Principles) Matrix, Common Mistake #2:
Four Common Mistakes to be Avoided
2. "Anything I know or believe about my current mental states is classically self-evident for me."
This does not follow from the definition of "classically self-evident" and is false. For
example, almost any beliefs you have about the causes of your current mental states
are possibly mistaken and so not classically self-evident.
224 One example (alluded to in “Separation by Fact and Value”) that’s been in the news concerns “honor killings” in Afghanistan, where male relatives of a woman who has been raped believe that they must kill the
raped woman to prevent dishonoring their family. Some of the raped women disagree.

337

Some Common Misconceptions
"My current visual sensation of words in black type is caused by seeing these
very words in black type"
is a claim about how your visual sensation is caused and thinking that this claim is
true does not logically necessitate its truth - you might after all be dreaming.
"It seems to be that I'm now seeing some words in black type"
(or, what comes to about the same, "I'm now having a visual sensation of words that
look like this in black type") is however classically self-evident for you now.
Similarly, "My current visual sensation of words in black is caused by a dream of
words in black" is not classically self-evident for you now.
So, whether the conjectured route of causation is external or internal, the conjecture
is fallible and not classically self-evident for you now.
More generally, most people don’t know what causes most of their thoughts or feelings. Some even spend years in therapy trying to come up with useful guesses.
Because numbers can be referred to in many ways (e.g., base 10, base 2, base 16, Roman
numerals) it's not necessarily true that the number 6 is an even number.
Because numbers can be referred to in many ways (e.g., base 10, base 2, base 16, Roman numerals) it's not necessarily true that the sentence, "6 is an even number,” expresses a truth.
Notice that the two statements are different. As the PHI 340 FAQs and at even greater length,
the PHI 340 IQs emphasize, adding or removing words from test questions is a very bad idea
because words do work, and adding or removing words can and typically will change the
question.
It is not even necessarily true that the sentence "6 is an even number" exists, no less that it
expresses a truth. Similarly, it is not necessarily true that the numeral "6" exists.
"6" ≠ 6 because the name of the number 6 (the numeral "6") is not the same thing as the
number 6, just as your name ≠ you: your name is a sequence of letters, but you are not a
sequence of letters. Even if all occurrences of your name were somehow erased, you would
not thereby cease to exist.
Confusing numerals with numbers leads people to say ridiculous things, e.g., that 2+4 might
not have been equal to 6 (false) because the names of the numbers 2, 4 and 6 might have
been different (true). More generally, confusing names with what they name encourages
confusion of a statement S with the sentence "S." And the latter in turn makes it easier to
confuse the statement made by using "S" with the statement made by using "I believe that S"
and thus helps to promote incoherent relativism about truth and morality. A sentence "S"
represents the statement that S much as "6" represents 6. "I believe that S" says in effect, "I
have a mental representation of the statement that S," so if one confuses the sentence "S"
with the statement that S, it is much easier to confuse the statement I believe that S with the
statement that S.
To make the point a different way: sentences and the words that they contain are, as representations, analogous to pictures, and no one should confuse a picture with what it depicts:
x ≠ a picture of x, and thus S ≠ the sentence "S" and N ≠ the name "N".

338

Some Common Misconceptions
There is some evidence that the universe is the product of design by one or more intelligent beings.
The claim was given support in “UFOlogy” and very strong support in “Separation by Explanation.” Every great philosopher since the time of ancient Greece (before 400 BCE) has accepted this claim, the overwhelming majority of scientists (including of course Charles Darwin) have agreed, and at least half of the scientists now alive would also accept it (and all of
them should accept it). David Hume, the 18th century philosopher who famously criticized
Arguments from Design for the existence of God would not have bothered to criticize such
design arguments at book-length had he not accepted the claim. For a more recent defense
of the claim by an atheist other than Hume, see:
Bradley Monton, Seeking God in Science: An Atheist Defends Intelligent Design (Broadview Press, 2009) DH Hill Stacks (3rd floor) BL262 .M65 2009 http://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/record/NCSU2253598

There is no argument or evidence for the claim that there are non-physical minds ("souls");
and thus, the claim that there are non-physical minds ("souls") must be believed on faith, if
it is believed at all.
If someone were unacquainted with Descartes’ Argument for the Distinctness of Mind and
Body, then they might think that belief in non-physical souls is baseless. You, however, are
better off than that. (There are other arguments, dating to the time of Plato and Aristotle, for
the existence of non-physical souls.
http://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ancient-soul/#3 http://plato.stanford.edu/entries/consciousness/ )

Anything (even a computer) that always behaved as intelligently as I do, would have to be
intelligent.
This claim is sometimes called the “Turing Test for Intelligence.” Block’s decisive refutation
of it (Unthinking Waldo Simulator) is at the heart of the chapter, “Behaviorism,” and paves
the way for Machine Functionalism.
One of the best ways to show that an explanation is wrong is to show that its proponents
are typically immoral.
This is one of the worst ways to show that an explanation is wrong. It misses the target. This
is emphasized beginning in the chapter "Astrology" and re-emphasized repeatedly in both
this text and in Abusing Science. There is no good reason to believe that the cognitive skill
required to provide good explanations is even strongly correlated with having an above average moral character. The world contains many very smart, nasty people who argue for and
explain truths.
Suppose for example that Adolf Hitler explained why the ground was wet in this way (after
it had rained):
1. Whenever it rains, the ground is wet.
2. It's now raining.
3. Therefore, the ground is wet.
His explanation is logically valid and provides an excellent way to explain why the ground's
wet when it rains, and nothing that could be said about his terrible character or awful behavior could possibly show otherwise.
Finally, there is a twenty fifth mistake well worth avoiding:
339

Some Common Misconceptions
“I’m entitled to my opinion.”
Or: “Well, that’s your interpretation. I’m entitled to my interpretation.”
Or: “It’s all a matter of perspective – and my perspective is as good as your perspective.”
[-often preliminary to, “You just want students to say what you think.”]

In almost every case where such a claim might be apt, it is also false. It is typically given as
an excuse for ignoring or rejecting without argument a claim against which evidence and
arguments have been given. This was well-argued in the first reading:
Jamie Whyte, “The Right to Your Opinion,” Crimes Against Logic.
Instead, the obligation is to examine thoughtfully the reasons for which you hold an opinion
or favor an interpretation. Either the claim will turn out to be properly basic (which is not
very likely as such claims are rarely even challenged), or it will need support from evidence
and argument.
It’s got to be either one or the other of those.
In the latter, common case, the question is whether your evidence does indeed support your
claim, and whether the argument that you have used to connect evidence to claim is a good
argument. Good arguments often do, of course, yield good explanations. The criteria for a
good explanation were outlined early in this text (“systematicity”) and by Kitcher in Chapter
2 of Abusing Science.
Two texts suitable for self-teaching are:
Richard Feldman, Reason and Argument 2nd ed. (Prentice Hall, 1999) DH Hill BC177
.F45 1999 http://catalog.lib.ncsu.edu/record/NCSU2186422
Reid Hastie and Robyn Dawes, Rational Choice in an Uncertain World: The Psychology
of Judgment and Decision Making, 2nd ed. (Sage, 2010) UNC Davis Library BF447 .H37
2010
Thoughtful examination is as difficult as it is important; failure to engage in it kills
many people.
[To verify the latter claim, see for example,
http://www.theatlantic.com/magazine/archive/2010/11/lies-damned-lies-andmedical-science/8269/

It is a disturbing truth about fact-resistant opinions that being intelligent and generally welleducated does not seem to help those bright, well-trained people to abandon their
340

Some Common Misconceptions
entrenched, ill-supported beliefs. See the discussions at Yale Law School’s Dan Kahan’s
http://www.culturalcognition.net/ for evidence that the better educated people are, the
more likely it is that being informed about the reasons that their opinions on major issues
are incorrect or ill-supported can make people even more resistant to changing their opinions.225
Cases of persistent controversy over decision-relevance science don’t stem from defects
in public science comprehension; they are not a result of the failure of scientists to clearly
communicate their own technical knowledge; nor are they convincingly attributable to
orchestrated deception, as treacherous as such behavior genuinely is. Rather such disputes are a consequence of one or another form of disruption to the system of conventions that normally enable individuals to recognize valid science despite their inability to
understand it. (1)
But such a system is vulnerable to a distinctive pathology: identity-protective cognition
(IPC). IPC occurs when a policy-relevant fact that admits of empirical inquiry becomes
entangled in antagonistic social meanings that transform positions on them into badges
of identity in, and loyalty to, competing cultural groups …. The cost under those conditions
of forming factually incorrect beliefs on matters such as whether humans are heating up
the earth or whether fracking will exhaust or contaminate drinking water sources is essentially zero: individuals’ personal views and actions are not consequential enough to
affect the level of risk they face, or the likely adoption of ameliorating (or simply pointless
or even perverse) regulatory responses. But given what beliefs on these subjects (correct
or incorrect) have come to signify about the kind of person one is—about whose side one
is on, in what has become a struggle for status among competing cultural groups—the
personal cost of forming the wrong ones in relation to one’s own cultural identity could
be punishingly high ….
In such circumstances, individuals can be expected to use their reason to form and persist
in beliefs that reliably vouch for their group identities regardless of whether those beliefs
are factually accurate.
Indeed, individuals who enjoy the highest level of proficiency will display this form of motivated reasoning to the greatest extent, precisely because they will be the most adept at
using their reasoning proficiency to secure the interest that they share to form identityexpressive beliefs….226 (12)
Dan M. Kahan, “On the Sources of Ordinary Science Knowledge and Extraordinary Science
Ignorance,” Oxford Handbook on the Science of Science Communication (forthcoming)
http://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=2794799

225 Persistent acceptance of many of the falsehoods above – especially the four misconceptions about the nature of science - has been negatively correlated with course grades in PHI 340.
226 Kahan, D.M. The Politically Motivated Reasoning Paradigm. Emerging Trends in Social & Behavioral Sciences, (in press), http://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstr act_id=2703011

On Neo, the neon sign: Neo has no classically self-evident beliefs. It is not logically necessary
that Neo, the neon sign, will display the correct message, even if Neo is well-designed. Nor
does Neo have any beliefs although we will interpret Neo as displaying a belief that we would
express with the words that Neo displays. Neo lacks original intentionality, as Haugeland
would put it.
